V_{geo} = \pi \left(\dfrac{25}{2}\right)^2 \cdot 40 = \pi \cdot 156.25 \cdot 40 = 6250\pi \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2: Effective Material Volume per Layer**

$$
V_{layer,eff} = V_{geo} \cdot \phi \cdot \dfrac{h}{H} \quad \text{(per unit height fraction)}
$$

But more directly, the per-layer area is the cylinder cross-section and the per-layer effective volume is:

$$
V_{layer,eff} = \pi \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2}\right)^2 \cdot h \cdot \phi
$$

$$
V_{layer,eff} = \pi \cdot 156.25 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 0.50 = 15.625\pi \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3: Trace Length per Layer**

$$
L_{layer} = \dfrac{V_{layer,eff}}{w \cdot h} = \dfrac{15.625\pi}{0.4 \cdot 0.2} = \dfrac{15.625\pi}{0.08} = 195.3125\pi \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Number of Layers**

$$
N = \dfrac{H}{h} = \dfrac{40}{0.2} = 200
$$

**Step 5: Total Build Time**

$$
t_{build} = N \cdot \dfrac{L_{layer}}{v} = 200 \cdot \dfrac{195.3125\pi}{60}
$$

$$
t_{build} = \dfrac{200 \cdot 195.3125\pi}{60} = \dfrac{39062.5\pi}{60} \approx 2045.4 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{build} \approx 34.09 \text{ minutes}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):**
> **Two common mistakes** students make:
> 1. Forgetting to multiply the volume by **infill density φ**. If omitted, the answer becomes ~68 minutes — examiners will **deduct 2 marks** for "failure to account for infill."
> 2. Computing per-layer *area* of trace instead of *length*. The trace length formula $L = V/(w \cdot h)$ is derived from $V = L \cdot w \cdot h$ — examiners expect this **unit analysis** to be shown for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — 3D Printing**

- ✅ **ASTM/ISO Definition**: 3D printing = *layer-by-layer addition* of material from a digital 3D model.
- ✅ **ASTM F42 has 7 categories** of AM processes — Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Sheet Lamination, Directed Energy Deposition.
- ✅ **FDM** uses molten thermoplastic filament (PLA, PCL, PHB); nozzle T = 180–220 °C for PLA.
- ✅ **SLA** uses UV laser (λ ≈ 405 nm) to cure photopolymer; **Jacob's working curve**: $C_d = D_p \ln(E_{max}/E_c)$.
- ✅ **SLS** uses CO₂ laser (λ = 10.6 µm) to sinter biopolymer powder.
- ✅ **Bioprinting techniques**: Extrusion-based (broadest viscosity, lowest viability), Inkjet (highest speed, low viscosity), Laser-assisted (highest resolution, no nozzle).
- ✅ **Bio-ink** = hydrogel + living cells; cell density 10⁶–10⁷ cells/mL; cell viability > 70% required.
- ✅ **Scaffold porosity**: $\varepsilon = 1 - \rho_{scaffold}/\rho_{bulk}$. For bone scaffolds, optimum pore size = 300–500 µm with ε = 60–80%.
- ✅ **Build time formula**: $t_{build} = V_{part} / (h \cdot v \cdot w)$. Always **multiply by infill density φ** in the effective volume.
- ✅ **Beer-Lambert origin** of cure depth — must show $E_c = E_{max} \cdot e^{-C_d/D_p}$ as a *separate step* (2 marks in derivations).
- ✅ **Stereolithography resolution** (X-Y): 25–50 µm — orders of magnitude finer than FDM (100–200 µm).
- ✅ **Shear stress in nozzle**: $\tau = \eta \cdot 4Q / (\pi r_n^3)$ — higher $Q$ or smaller $r_n$ dramatically increases shear (and reduces cell viability in bioprinting).
- ✅ **Standard build volumes** for desktop printers: 100–600 mm³; layer thickness: 10–500 µm.
- ✅ **Slicing software** generates G-code from STL files; common slicers: **Cura, Simplify3D, Slic3r, PrusaSlicer**.
- ✅ **Post-processing for medical 3D prints**: support removal, surface finishing, **sterilization** (EtO, γ-irradiation, autoclave — biopolymer-dependent).
- ✅ **Course Outcome Tag**: This topic maps to **CO2** (Apply advanced processing techniques) and **CO3** (Analyze structure–property relationships in biocomposites).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT415 - ADVANCED BIOPOLYMERS & BIOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Biopolymers & Biocomposites (PEPOT415)**, the term **"Properties"** refers to the comprehensive set of **physical, mechanical, thermal, chemical, barrier, optical, rheological, surface, electrical, and biological (biodegradation) characteristics** exhibited by a biopolymer matrix, a natural fibre, or a hybrid biocomposite system under defined environmental and loading conditions. These properties dictate the **end-use applicability, processing window, durability, sustainability quotient, and service-life predictability** of the material in industries such as **biomedical implants, food packaging, automotive interiors, agricultural mulches, and structural panels**.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a property is a **quantifiable response function** $P(f, T, t, \dot{\varepsilon}, \phi)$ that depends on applied force $f$, absolute temperature $T$, time $t$, strain rate $\dot{\varepsilon}$, and composition/fibre volume fraction $\phi$ of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Properties):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly requires the student to *characterize, distinguish, and quantitatively predict* the property profile of bio-based polymers (e.g., PLA, PHA, starch blends, chitosan, cellulose) and their fibre-reinforced biocomposites (e.g., flax/PLA, jute/epoxy, hemp/PP) with respect to **mechanical, thermal, barrier, and biodegradation** responses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Wooden Boat" Analogy** 🛶
>
> Imagine you are building a small fishing canoe. You have two choices:
>
> 1. **A single piece of softwood log** (this is your **neat biopolymer** — e.g., neat PLA).
> 2. **A wooden frame with bamboo strips glued along its length, sealed with natural resin** (this is your **biocomposite** — e.g., flax-fibre reinforced PLA).
>
> The plain log is light and easy to shape, but it warps in water, scratches easily, and cracks under sudden load. The bamboo-reinforced canoe is **stiffer, resists swelling, has higher load-bearing capacity, but is harder to manufacture and slightly heavier**.
>
> This is precisely the **property trade-off** that engineers must characterize: **reinforcement improves strength and stiffness, but alters flexibility, density, processing behaviour, and degradation kinetics**. Every "property" we measure is simply our way of *quantifying* these trade-offs in numbers (MPa, °C, g·m/m²·day, %).

## 1.3 Classification of Properties (KTU-Aligned Taxonomy)

Properties of biopolymers and biocomposites are broadly classified into **nine functional domains**, as mapped below:

| # | Property Domain | Representative Metric | SI Unit | KTU Relevance |
|---|----------------|----------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Mechanical | Tensile strength $\sigma_t$ | **MPa** | High |
| 2 | Thermal | Glass transition $T_g$, Melting $T_m$ | **°C / K** | High |
| 3 | Barrier | Water Vapour Permeability (WVP) | **g·mm/m²·day** | High |
| 4 | Chemical | Solvent resistance, Hydrolytic stability | **% mass loss** | Medium |
| 5 | Optical | Transmittance, Haze | **%** | Medium |
| 6 | Rheological | Melt Flow Index (MFI) | **g/10 min** | Medium |
| 7 | Surface | Contact angle $\theta$, Surface energy | **° / mN·m⁻¹** | High |
| 8 | Electrical | Dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ | dimensionless | Medium |
| 9 | Biological | Biodegradation rate $k_d$ | **% / day** | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants Used in Property Evaluation:**
> - Standard laboratory temperature: $T_0 = 23^\circ C = 296.15 \text{ K}$
> - Standard relative humidity: $RH = 50\%$
> - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \text{ J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1}$
> - Gravitational acceleration: $g = 9.81 \text{ m·s}^{-2}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanical Properties — The Structural Backbone

Mechanical properties describe how a biopolymer/biocomposite **responds to applied load**. For a fibre-reinforced biocomposite, properties are governed by the **rule of mixtures** and the **interfacial shear strength (IFSS)** between fibre and matrix.

### 2.1.1 Tensile Strength ($\sigma_t$)

The maximum tensile stress a specimen sustains before fracture.

$$\sigma_t = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A_0}$$

where $F_{\max}$ is the peak load (N) and $A_0$ is the original cross-sectional area (m²).

### 2.1.2 Young's Modulus ($E$) — Stiffness

$$E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$$

where $L_0$ is the gauge length and $\Delta L$ is the elongation.

### 2.1.3 Elongation at Break ($\varepsilon_b$)

$$\varepsilon_b = \dfrac{L_{\text{fracture}} - L_0}{L_0} \times 100\%$$

### 2.1.4 Impact Strength (Izod/Charpy)

$$I = \dfrac{E_{\text{absorbed}}}{t \cdot b}$$

measured in **kJ·m⁻²**.

### 2.1.5 Why these matter for biocomposites

- **Fibre volume fraction ($V_f$)** dominates strength: $\sigma_c \uparrow$ as $V_f \uparrow$ up to a critical value.
- **Fibre aspect ratio ($l/d$)** governs load transfer efficiency.
- **Fibre-matrix interface** is the weakest link in most natural-fibre systems (hydrophilic fibre + hydrophobic polymer = poor adhesion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition — Why natural fibres fail at high $V_f$:**
> At low $V_f$, the matrix efficiently wets the fibre. Beyond ~30–40% fibre loading, fibres begin to **agglomerate**, creating stress concentration points. Above 50%, the matrix can no longer fully wet the fibres, leaving **voids** that act as crack initiators. The strength thus **peaks and then declines**.

## 2.2 Thermal Properties — Defining the Processing Window

### 2.2.1 Glass Transition Temperature ($T_g$)

The temperature at which amorphous polymer segments gain mobility. Below $T_g$ the polymer is rigid (glassy), above it is rubbery.

**Fox–Flory equation** for molecular-weight dependence:

$$T_g = T_{g,\infty} - \dfrac{K}{M_n}$$

where $M_n$ is the number-average molecular weight and $K$ is the Fox constant.

**Couchman–Karasz equation** for copolymer blends:

$$T_g = \dfrac{w_1 T_{g,1} + k w_2 T_{g,2}}{w_1 + k w_2}$$

where $k = \Delta C_{p,2} / \Delta C_{p,1}$ is the ratio of heat-capacity jumps at $T_g$.

### 2.2.2 Melting Temperature ($T_m$)

For semi-crystalline biopolymers (PLA, PHB, PHBV).

$$T_m = T_m^0 \left(1 - \dfrac{2\sigma_s}{\Delta H_f \cdot l_c}\right)$$

where $l_c$ is the lamellar thickness, $\sigma_s$ is the surface free energy, and $\Delta H_f$ is the heat of fusion.

### 2.2.3 Degree of Crystallinity ($\chi_c$) — from DSC

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^0 \cdot w_p} \times 100\%$$

where $\Delta H_m$ is the measured melting enthalpy, $\Delta H_m^0$ is the enthalpy of fusion for 100% crystalline polymer, and $w_p$ is the polymer weight fraction.

### 2.2.4 Thermal Degradation — Broido's Equation

$$\ln\left[\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{1-\alpha}\right)\right] = -\dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \text{const}$$

where $\alpha$ is the fractional mass loss and $E_a$ is the apparent activation energy.

## 2.3 Barrier Properties — Critical for Packaging

### 2.3.1 Water Vapour Permeability (WVP)

$$\text{WVP} = \dfrac{\Delta m \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta t \cdot \Delta P}$$

measured in **g·mm/m²·day·kPa**.

### 2.3.2 Oxygen Permeability (OP)

$$\text{OP} = \dfrac{q \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta p}$$

measured in **cm³·mm/m²·day·atm**.

### 2.3.3 Fick's First Law (Diffusion)

$$J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$$

where $J$ is the flux, $D$ is the diffusion coefficient (m²/s), and $dC/dx$ is the concentration gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why barrier properties matter for biopolymers:**
> Most biopolymers (PLA, PHB, starch) are **hydrophilic** and have **moderate-to-poor water-vapour barrier** but **excellent oxygen barrier** at low RH. This makes them ideal for **fresh-produce packaging** (low O₂ slows respiration) but poor for **liquid or high-moisture foods**.

## 2.4 Surface Properties — Wettability & Adhesion

### 2.4.1 Contact Angle (Young's Equation)

$$\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$$

where $\gamma_{sv}$, $\gamma_{sl}$, $\gamma_{lv}$ are solid-vapour, solid-liquid, and liquid-vapour interfacial tensions.

- $\theta < 90^\circ$ → **hydrophilic** (good wetting)
- $\theta > 90^\circ$ → **hydrophobic** (poor wetting)

### 2.4.2 Owens–Wendt Surface Energy

$$\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$$

where $d$ denotes dispersive and $p$ polar contributions.

## 2.5 Biodegradation Properties

### 2.5.1 Mineralization Kinetics (First-Order)

$$M(t) = M_\infty \left(1 - e^{-k_d t}\right)$$

where $M(t)$ is the cumulative CO₂ evolved at time $t$, $M_\infty$ is the theoretical maximum, and $k_d$ is the degradation constant (day⁻¹).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table — it appears in ~80% of KTU ESE numericals and derivations.**

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Unit |
|---|----------|--------------------|---------------|--------------|
| 1 | Tensile Strength | $\sigma_t = F_{\max}/A_0$ | Load, area | MPa |
| 2 | Young's Modulus | $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$ | Stress, strain | GPa |
| 3 | Elongation at break | $\varepsilon_b = \Delta L / L_0$ | Length change | % |
| 4 | Crystallinity (DSC) | $\chi_c = \Delta H_m / (\Delta H_m^0 w_p)$ | Enthalpies, mass fraction | % |
| 5 | Fox–Flory $T_g$ | $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ | Molecular weight | °C |
| 6 | Gibbs–Thomson $T_m$ | $T_m = T_m^0(1 - 2\sigma_s / \Delta H_f l_c)$ | Lamellar thickness | °C |
| 7 | Broido degradation | $\ln[\ln(1/(1-\alpha))] = -E_a/(RT) + C$ | Mass loss, T | kJ/mol |
| 8 | WVP | $\text{WVP} = \Delta m L / (A \Delta t \Delta P)$ | Mass, thickness, area, P | g·mm/m²·day·kPa |
| 9 | Fick's diffusion | $J = -D \, dC/dx$ | Flux, gradient | mol/m²·s |
| 10 | Young's contact angle | $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv}\cos\theta$ | Surface tensions | mN/m |
| 11 | Biodegradation | $M(t) = M_\infty(1 - e^{-k_d t})$ | CO₂, time | % |
| 12 | Rule of mixtures (E) | $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Moduli, Vf, Vm | GPa |
| 13 | Inverse rule (E) | $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ | Moduli, Vf, Vm | GPa |
| 14 | Halpin–Tsai (E) | $E_c = E_m (1 + 2\eta V_f)/(1 - \eta V_f)$ | Moduli, $\eta$ | GPa |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** These equations are the **backbone of material selection charts (Ashby plots)** used by biomedical device designers (selecting $E$ for bone-mimicking scaffolds), food-packaging engineers (selecting WVP and OP for shelf life), and automotive interior designers (selecting impact strength and HDT for door trims).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Rule of Mixtures for Composite Tensile Strength

The **Rule of Mixtures (RoM)** is the *most-asked derivation* in KTU ESE papers for biocomposites. We derive it for a **unidirectional, continuous-fibre-reinforced biocomposite** under iso-strain (Voigt) loading.

### Step 1: Define the iso-strain assumption

In a unidirectional composite loaded parallel to the fibre axis, both fibre and matrix experience the **same strain** $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$ (perfect bonding, no slip).

### Step 2: Express the composite load

The total tensile load $F_c$ on the composite is shared between fibre and matrix:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

### Step 3: Convert forces to stresses using cross-sectional areas

Since $F = \sigma \cdot A$:

$$\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m$$

### Step 4: Introduce volume fractions

Divide through by $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}$$

Recognising that for a uniform cross-section, $V_i = A_i / A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$

### Step 5: Apply the iso-strain condition $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$

Substituting $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$:

$$\sigma_c = E_f \varepsilon_c V_f + E_m \varepsilon_c V_m = \varepsilon_c (E_f V_f + E_m V_m)$$

Therefore the **composite modulus** is:

$$E_c = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon_c} = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \text{(Voigt/RoM)}$$

### Step 6: For transverse loading (iso-stress, Reuss model)

When the load is perpendicular to the fibre axis, both phases experience the **same stress** $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m$ but **different strains**:

$$\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication for biocomposites:** Natural fibres (flax, jute, hemp) have $E_f \approx 30-80$ GPa, while PLA has $E_m \approx 3.5$ GPa. At $V_f = 0.3$, the longitudinal modulus is $E_c \approx 0.3(50) + 0.7(3.5) = 17.45$ GPa — roughly **5× the neat PLA modulus**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Degree of Crystallinity from DSC Data

### Step 1: Define crystallinity

Crystallinity is the **mass fraction of polymer chains packed in an ordered (crystalline) phase**:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{m_{\text{crystalline}}}{m_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^0}$$

### Step 2: Account for non-polymeric additives/fibres

In a biocomposite, only the **polymer fraction** contributes to melting. If the polymer mass fraction is $w_p$:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{w_p \cdot \Delta H_m^0}$$

### Step 3: Numerical demonstration

Suppose PLA biocomposite: $\Delta H_m = 32.4$ J/g, $w_p = 0.85$, $\Delta H_m^0 \text{(100\% cryst. PLA)} = 93.6$ J/g.

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{32.4}{0.85 \times 93.6} = \dfrac{32.4}{79.56} = 0.407 \approx 40.7\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 40.7% crystallinity PLA composite is **semi-crystalline**, suitable for applications requiring moderate stiffness and dimensional stability (e.g., injection-moulded cutlery, 3D-printing filament).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Permeability from Mass-Change Experiment

### Step 1: Fick's First Law in 1D

Steady-state flux through a film of thickness $L$:

$$J = -D \dfrac{\Delta C}{L}$$

### Step 2: Convert flux to mass rate

$J = \dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot \Delta t}$, so:

$$\dfrac{\Delta m}{A \cdot \Delta t} = \dfrac{D \cdot \Delta C}{L}$$

### Step 3: Convert concentration difference to partial-pressure difference

Using Henry's law $\Delta C = S \cdot \Delta P$, where $S$ is the solubility:

$$\dfrac{\Delta m \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta t \cdot \Delta P} = D \cdot S = P$$

where $P = D \cdot S$ is the **permeability coefficient**.

### Step 4: Substitution

$$\boxed{\text{WVP} = \dfrac{\Delta m \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta t \cdot \Delta P}}$$

in units of **g·mm·m⁻²·day⁻¹·kPa⁻¹**.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Biodegradation Half-Life from First-Order Kinetics

### Step 1: Governing ODE

$$\dfrac{dM}{dt} = k_d (M_\infty - M)$$

### Step 2: Integrate with $M(0) = 0$

$$\int_0^{M} \dfrac{dM'}{M_\infty - M'} = \int_0^t k_d \, dt$$

$$-\ln(M_\infty - M) + \ln(M_\infty) = k_d t$$

$$M(t) = M_\infty (1 - e^{-k_d t})$$

### Step 3: Half-life derivation

Set $M(t_{1/2}) = M_\infty / 2$:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} = 1 - e^{-k_d t_{1/2}}$$

$$e^{-k_d t_{1/2}} = \dfrac{1}{2}$$

$$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k_d} = \dfrac{0.693}{k_d}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical Property Ranges for Common Biopolymers

| Biopolymer | $\sigma_t$ (MPa) | $E$ (GPa) | $\varepsilon_b$ (%) | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) | WVP (g·mm/m²·day) | Biodegradability |
|------------|------------------|-----------|---------------------|-----------|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **PLA** | 50–70 | 3.0–3.5 | 2–6 | 55–62 | 150–170 | 0.25 | Slow (compost, 6 mo) |
| **PHB / PHBV** | 25–40 | 1.5–3.5 | 5–10 | 0–5 | 170–180 | 0.18 | Fast (soil, 2–8 wk) |
| **Starch (thermoplastic)** | 5–10 | 0.1–0.5 | 30–100 | – | – (decomposes) | 1.5–2.5 | Very fast (days) |
| **Chitosan** | 30–60 | 1–3 | 10–40 | 140 (decomposes) | – | 0.9 | Fast (weeks) |
| **Cellulose (regen.)** | 60–120 | 5–15 | 5–20 | – | – | 0.7 | Moderate (months) |
| **Neat Epoxy (synthetic)** | 55–90 | 2.5–6.0 | 2–10 | 80–180 | – | 0.04 | None |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this table is KTU-critical:** Comparison questions like *"Compare the properties of PLA and PHBV for food packaging"* appear almost every semester in Part A. Memorize the bold trends: **PHB is tougher and faster-degrading**, **PLA is stiffer and slower-degrading**, **starch is the most permeable**, **epoxy is the most dimensionally stable**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the term "Degree of Crystallinity" of a biopolymer. How is it determined from a DSC thermogram?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Degree of crystallinity $\chi_c$ is the mass fraction of polymer chains present in a three-dimensionally ordered (crystalline) phase, as opposed to the disordered (amorphous) phase. It is a dimensionless quantity, expressed as a percentage.

**DSC Method (2 marks):**
- A small sample (~5–10 mg) is heated from below $T_g$ to above $T_m$ at a controlled rate (typically 10 °C/min) under nitrogen purge.
- The **endothermic melting peak** is integrated to obtain $\Delta H_m$ (J/g).
- The crystallinity is then calculated using:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_m}{\Delta H_m^0 \cdot w_p} \times 100\%$$

where $\Delta H_m^0$ is the theoretical heat of fusion for a 100% crystalline sample (e.g., 93.6 J/g for PLA) and $w_p$ is the polymer mass fraction in the composite.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between the tensile behaviour of neat PLA and a 30 wt% flax-fibre reinforced PLA biocomposite.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Neat PLA | Flax/PLA (30 wt%) |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| Young's Modulus | ~3.2 GPa | ~7–10 GPa (≈2–3× higher) |
| Tensile Strength | 50–70 MPa | 60–90 MPa |
| Elongation at Break | 2–6 % | 1–3 % (brittle) |
| Failure Mode | Necking + ductile | Brittle fibre pull-out |

**Key Explanation (1 mark):** The flax fibres act as a stiff, load-bearing phase that increases modulus and strength, but the fibre-matrix interface restricts polymer chain mobility, reducing elongation. The composite becomes **brittle** but **stiffer and stronger** than the neat matrix.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:**
> - Total: **14 marks**
> - Sub-part (a): **7 marks**
> - Sub-part (b): **7 marks**
> - Internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B** (both from the same module).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

#### (a) Derive the rule of mixtures for a unidirectional fibre-reinforced biocomposite under iso-strain loading. Discuss its limitations when applied to natural fibre systems such as jute/epoxy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation (5 marks):** [Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above]

1. Iso-strain assumption: $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$
2. Force balance: $F_c = F_f + F_m$
3. Stress conversion: $\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$
4. Volume fraction: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$
5. Final result: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$

**Limitations for jute/epoxy (2 marks):**

1. **Fibre agglomeration:** Natural fibres are not perfectly aligned and tend to bundle, creating resin-rich and fibre-rich regions.
2. **Hydrophilic-hydrophobic mismatch:** Jute is highly hydrophilic (lignocellulosic, ~12% moisture), while epoxy is hydrophobic → poor interfacial bonding → actual $E_c$ is much lower than RoM prediction.
3. **Void content:** Natural fibres have lumens that trap air; voids reduce effective load-bearing area.
4. **Fibre length distribution:** RoM assumes continuous, uniform fibres; jute is short and variable.
5. **Anisotropy:** RoM gives only longitudinal $E$; transverse $E$ is far lower (Reuss model).

#### (b) Explain with a neat sketch how the degree of crystallinity of a semi-crystalline biopolymer is determined using Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC). What is the significance of the cooling scan vs. heating scan? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**DSC Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Sample preparation:** 5–10 mg biopolymer is sealed in an aluminium pan; an empty pan serves as reference.
2. **Heating scan (1st heat):** Eliminates thermal history of the sample. The melting endotherm $\Delta H_m$ gives the "as-received" crystallinity.
3. **Cooling scan:** The sample is cooled at a controlled rate. The **exothermic crystallization peak** yields the crystallization temperature $T_c$ and enthalpy $\Delta H_c$. This peak shows the polymer's **intrinsic ability to crystallize** under controlled conditions.
4. **2nd heating scan:** Removes the influence of processing history. The melting peak $\Delta H_{m,2}$ is used to calculate the **intrinsic crystallinity**:

$$\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_{m,2}}{\Delta H_m^0 \cdot w_p} \times 100\%$$

5. **Sketch:** A typical DSC thermogram shows a **step change at $T_g$** (no peak, just baseline shift), a **cold-crystallization exotherm** (if any, on heating amorphous PLA), and a sharp **melting endotherm** at $T_m$.

**Significance of cooling vs. heating (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Heating Scan | Cooling Scan |
|--------|--------------|--------------|
| Gives | $\Delta H_m$ (used for $\chi_c$) | $\Delta H_c$, $T_c$ |
| Indicates | Order already present | Tendency to crystallize |
| Affected by | Thermal history | Cooling rate, nucleating agents |
| Used in | Quality control | Processing window design |

**Key Insight:** Adding **nucleating agents** (e.g., talc, CNCs) increases $T_c$ and sharpens the cooling exotherm → faster cycle times in injection moulding.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### (a) Discuss the water vapour permeability (WVP) of PLA, PHB, and starch-based films. How does the addition of nanocellulose affect the barrier properties? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Film | WVP (g·mm/m²·day·kPa) | Reason |
|------|------------------------|--------|
| PLA | ~0.25 | Hydrophobic, semicrystalline |
| PHB/PHBV | ~0.18 | Higher crystallinity, denser packing |
| Starch (TPS) | ~1.5–2.5 | Highly hydrophilic, amorphous |
| Cellophane (regen. cellulose) | ~0.7 | Moderate crystallinity |

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- **PLA** has the best WVP among the three because of its moderate crystallinity and hydrophobicity.
- **PHB** is even better due to higher $\chi_c$ and tighter chain packing.
- **Starch** has the worst WVP due to abundant –OH groups that absorb water and act as plasticizers, increasing free volume.

**Effect of Nanocellulose Addition (2 marks):**

- Nanocellulose (CNC/CNF) acts as a **high-aspect-ratio nano-filler** that creates a **tortuous path** for diffusing water molecules.
- Permeability reduction follows the **Nielsen model**:

$$P_c = P_m \cdot \dfrac{1 - V_f}{1 + (L/2t)V_f}$$

where $L/t$ is the aspect ratio of the nano-filler.

- A 5 wt% CNF addition can reduce WVP by **40–60%** in PLA/CNF films.
- **Limitation:** At higher loadings, CNF agglomerates and may increase WVP due to nano-voids.

#### (b) What is the significance of surface energy in biocomposite design? Explain the Owens–Wendt method for determining surface energy using contact angle measurements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of Surface Energy (3 marks):**

1. **Wettability:** Determines whether a matrix can wet and adhere to a natural fibre.
2. **Adhesion strength:** Higher surface energy mismatch → poor adhesion → weak composite.
3. **Fibre-matrix interface:** Critical for stress transfer; low surface energy mismatch improves IFSS.
4. **Coating and printing:** Important for packaging labels and biomedical coatings.
5. **Cell adhesion:** In tissue engineering scaffolds, surface energy affects protein adsorption and cell attachment.

**Owens–Wendt Method (4 marks):**

1. **Principle:** Total surface energy is the sum of **dispersive** ($\gamma^d$) and **polar** ($\gamma^p$) components:

$$\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$$

2. **Procedure:** Measure the contact angle $\theta$ of **at least two probe liquids** of known $\gamma_l^d$ and $\gamma_l^p$ (e.g., water and diiodomethane) on the solid surface.

3. **Governing equation (Owens–Wendt–Rabel–Kaelble):**

$$\gamma_l (1 + \cos\theta) = 2 \left( \sqrt{\gamma_s^d \gamma_l^d} + \sqrt{\gamma_s^p \gamma_l^p} \right)$$

4. **Solve simultaneously** for $\gamma_s^d$ and $\gamma_s^p$ using the two-liquid data; then compute $\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$.

5. **Typical values for biopolymers:**
   - PLA: $\gamma_s \approx 38$ mN/m ($\gamma^d \approx 30$, $\gamma^p \approx 8$)
   - Jute fibre: $\gamma_s \approx 45$ mN/m (polar dominant due to –OH)
   - **Polar mismatch** → need **chemical treatment** (alkali, silane, acetylation) to reduce fibre polarity and improve compatibility with PLA.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2–4 marks in KTU ESE — avoid these!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $w_p$ factor in crystallinity calculation.** Students often write $\chi_c = \Delta H_m / \Delta H_m^0$ for composites, losing 1 mark. Always **multiply the denominator by $w_p$** when the material contains fibres/fillers.
>
> 2. **Confusing iso-strain and iso-stress models.** A transverse-loaded composite uses the **inverse rule of mixtures** (Reuss), not the standard rule. Drawing a wrong stress-strain sketch loses up to 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Reporting units incorrectly for WVP.** The correct unit is **g·mm/m²·day·kPa**, not g/m²·day. The "mm" comes from film thickness.
>
> 4. **Mixing up $T_g$ and $T_m$.** $T_g$ is for **amorphous** phase transition; $T_m$ is for **crystalline** melting. PHB has both, starch has neither (it decomposes).
>
> 5. **Ignoring fibre treatment in natural-fibre composites.** Examiners specifically look for keywords like **alkali treatment, silane coupling, acetylation, maleic anhydride grafting**. Leaving these out of any natural-fibre question = −2 marks.
>
> 6. **Using the wrong Halpin–Tsai form.** Halpin–Tsai is for *short-fibre* composites; use $\xi = 2(l/d)$ as the shape factor. Students often skip the $\xi$ term and lose the derivation credit.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 5 times before the exam.**

### ✅ Mechanical Properties
- $\sigma_t = F_{\max}/A_0$; $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$; $\varepsilon_b = \Delta L / L_0$
- RoM: $E_c = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ (longitudinal, iso-strain)
- Inverse RoM: $1/E_c = V_f/E_f + V_m/E_m$ (transverse, iso-stress)
- Halpin–Tsai for short fibres: $E_c = E_m \dfrac{1 + \xi \eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, $\eta = (E_f/E_m - 1)/(E_f/E_m + \xi)$
- Critical fibre length: $l_c = \sigma_f d / (2 \tau_i)$ where $\tau_i$ = IFSS
- Natural fibres need **chemical treatment** to reduce polarity and improve adhesion

### ✅ Thermal Properties
- **Fox–Flory:** $T_g = T_{g,\infty} - K/M_n$ — molecular weight effect
- **Couchman–Karasz:** for polymer blends
- **Gibbs–Thomson:** $T_m$ depends on lamellar thickness $l_c$
- **Crystallinity from DSC:** $\chi_c = \Delta H_m / (\Delta H_m^0 \cdot w_p)$
- **Broido plot:** $\ln[\ln(1/(1-\alpha))]$ vs $1/T$ gives slope $= -E_a/R$ → activation energy of thermal degradation

### ✅ Barrier Properties
- **Fick's first law:** $J = -D \, dC/dx$
- **WVP** and **OP** are critical for food packaging
- Permeability $P = D \times S$ (diffusivity × solubility)
- **Nielsen tortuosity model:** $P_c = P_m (1 - V_f)/(1 + (L/2t)V_f)$
- PLA: good O₂ barrier, moderate H₂O barrier
- Starch: poor H₂O barrier, moderate O₂ barrier
- Adding **nanocellulose** or **nanoclay** reduces permeability by 40–70%

### ✅ Surface Properties
- **Young's equation:** $\gamma_{sv} = \gamma_{sl} + \gamma_{lv} \cos\theta$
- $\theta < 90^\circ$ → hydrophilic; $\theta > 90^\circ$ → hydrophobic
- **Owens–Wendt:** $\gamma_s = \gamma_s^d + \gamma_s^p$; use 2 probe liquids
- Surface energy mismatch → poor adhesion → weak composite

### ✅ Biodegradation
- **First-order:** $M(t) = M_\infty (1 - e^{-k_d t})$
- **Half-life:** $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_d = 0.693 / k_d$
- Testing methods: **soil burial, enzymatic hydrolysis, compost mineralization (CO₂ evolution)**
- Factors affecting rate: crystallinity, $T_g$ vs. ambient $T$, moisture, microbial activity, pH

### ✅ Numerical Must-Knows
- $\Delta H_m^0$ (100% crystalline PLA) = **93.6 J/g**
- $\Delta H_m^0$ (100% crystalline PHB) = **146 J/g**
- $\Delta H_m^0$ (100% crystalline PP) = **207 J/g**
- Standard test conditions: **23 °C, 50% RH**

### ✅ Quick Comparison Mnemonics
- **"SSSH"** = **S**tarch, **S**oft, **S**wells, **H**ydrolyzes (fastest to degrade)
- **"PPPL"** = **P**LA, **P**HBV, **P**rocessable, **L**onger-life (slow degradation)
- **"NF-CMT"** = **N**atural **F**ibre needs **C**hemical **M**odification **T**reatment

### ✅ Frequently Confused Pairs
- $T_g$ (glass transition, amorphous) ≠ $T_m$ (melting, crystalline)
- Crystallinity **increases** strength, modulus, density, and HDT — but **decreases** elongation, impact strength, and transparency
- WVP **decreases** with crystallinity and hydrophobicity
- Biodegradation **decreases** with crystallinity and **increases** with hydrophilicity

### ✅ KTU 2024 Hot-Topics (Likely 14-Mark Questions)
1. RoM derivation + limitations for natural fibre systems
2. DSC-based crystallinity + significance of 1st and 2nd heating
3. WVP/OP comparison across biopolymers + effect of nano-fillers
4. Biodegradation kinetics (first-order model, half-life derivation)
5. Surface energy + Owens–Wendt for fibre-matrix compatibility
6. Halpin–Tsai derivation for short-fibre composites

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: CHARACTERIZATION AND APPLICATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characterization of Biopolymers & Biocomposites** is the systematic evaluation of the **chemical structure, physico-mechanical properties, thermal stability, morphological features, and degradation behavior** of bio-based polymeric materials and their composites using standardized analytical techniques. The goal is to establish **structure–property–performance–application (SPPA)** correlations, which form the scientific foundation for tailoring biopolymers to targeted engineering and biomedical end-uses.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Code: PEPOT415)**, characterization is not a stand-alone lab exercise — it is the **decision-making tool** that links a biopolymer's molecular architecture (Module 1 content on starch, cellulose, chitosan, PLA, PHB, PHA, etc.) to **real-world deployment in packaging, biomedical, automotive, agricultural, and electronic sectors**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Doctor's Diagnostic Test" Analogy**
> Imagine you have built a new car (the biopolymer) from novel bio-materials. Before you sell it, you need to certify that it is safe, durable, fuel-efficient, and biodegradable. You cannot just "look" at it — you need:
> - **Blood Test** → Chemical structure (FTIR, NMR)
> - **X-Ray / MRI** → Morphology & crystalline form (SEM, XRD)
> - **Stress Test on Treadmill** → Mechanical strength (Tensile, Impact)
> - **Fever Chart** → Thermal stability (TGA, DSC)
> - **Crash Test** → Barrier & degradation behavior (WVTR, soil burial)
> - **Customer Trial** → Application testing in field conditions

Each **characterization tool** answers a specific engineering question:

$$\text{Question: "Is this biopolymer fit for use?"} \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Answer via Multi-Technique Characterization}$$

### 1.3 The Need for Characterization in Biopolymers

| Property Being Verified | Engineering Risk if NOT Characterized |
|---|---|
| Molecular weight ($M_w$, $M_n$) | Premature failure in load-bearing applications |
| Crystallinity (%) | Unpredictable degradation rate and barrier performance |
| Glass transition ($T_g$) / Melting ($T_m$) | Thermal failure during processing (extrusion, injection moulding) |
| Tensile strength / Modulus | Mechanical collapse in structural use |
| Hydrophilicity / Contact angle | Loss of function in wet physiological environments |
| Biodegradation rate | Premature or delayed material disappearance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board-Exam Favourites):**
> - **ISO 527** — Tensile properties of plastics
> - **ASTM D638** — Tensile testing
> - **ASTM D790** — Flexural testing
> - **ISO 14855** — Aerobic biodegradability under controlled composting
> - **ASTM D6400** — Compostability specification
> - **ISO 10993** — Biological evaluation of medical devices

### 1.4 Categorization of Characterization Techniques

The KTU 2024 syllabus logically groups characterization into **five functional clusters**:

1. **Spectroscopic / Structural** — FTIR, NMR, UV-Vis, Raman, XPS
2. **Thermal / Thermodynamic** — TGA, DSC, DMA, TMA
3. **Mechanical / Rheological** — Universal Testing Machine (UTM), Impact, Hardness, Melt Flow Index (MFI)
4. **Morphological / Crystallographic** — SEM, TEM, AFM, XRD, POM
5. **Degradation / Barrier** — Soil burial, hydrolytic, enzymatic, water vapor & oxygen permeability

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cluster I — Spectroscopic Characterization

### 2.1.1 Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR)

- **Principle**: Molecules absorb IR radiation at frequencies matching the **vibrational modes** of their chemical bonds (stretching, bending, wagging, scissoring).
- **Output**: Transmittance (%) vs. Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) spectrum.
- **Why it matters for biopolymers**: Confirms presence of functional groups (–OH, C=O, C–O–C, N–H), identifies polymer type, and detects chemical modifications (e.g., esterification of starch, acetylation of cellulose).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characteristic IR Peaks (KTU High-Yield):**
> - **O–H stretch**: 3200–3600 cm⁻¹ (broad, H-bonded)
> - **C–H stretch (aliphatic)**: 2850–2960 cm⁻¹
> - **C=O carbonyl ester**: 1700–1750 cm⁻¹
> - **C–O–C (glycosidic)**: 1000–1200 cm⁻¹
> - **N–H bend (amides)**: 1500–1650 cm⁻¹
> - **C–O stretch (alcohols)**: 1000–1260 cm⁻¹

### 2.1.2 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectroscopy

- **¹H NMR & ¹³C NMR** probe the **local magnetic environment** of nuclei.
- Provides:
  - **Tacticity** (isotactic / syndiotactic / atactic)
  - **Degree of substitution (DS)** in cellulose derivatives
  - **Copolymer composition** (e.g., PLA–PHB blends)
- **Reference standard**: Tetramethylsilane (TMS) at $\delta = 0$ ppm.

### 2.1.3 X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS)

- Measures **elemental composition** and **chemical states** in the top 1–10 nm of a surface.
- Crucial for **biomedical surface engineering** (e.g., verifying surface –COOH groups on PLA scaffolds for cell adhesion).

---

## 2.2 Cluster II — Thermal Characterization

### 2.2.1 Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA)

- **Principle**: Sample mass is recorded continuously as temperature is ramped (typically 10 °C/min under N₂ or air).
- **Key Outputs**:
  - $T_{onset}$ — Temperature at which 5% mass loss occurs (thermal stability index)
  - $T_{max}$ — Temperature at maximum degradation rate
  - **Char residue (%)** at 700–800 °C
- **Why important**: Biopolymers have **lower thermal stability** than petroleum polymers. PLA degrades at ~300 °C; PHB at ~250 °C; cellulose at ~350 °C.

### 2.2.2 Differential Scanning Calorimetry (DSC)

- **Principle**: Measures **heat flow difference** between sample and reference during heating/cooling.
- **Key Outputs**:
  - **Glass transition temperature ($T_g$)**
  - **Crystallization temperature ($T_c$)** (exotherm)
  - **Melting temperature ($T_m$)** (endotherm)
  - **Crystallinity ($\chi_c$)**

The most critical equation in thermal analysis of biopolymers:

$$\chi_c (\%) = \frac{\Delta H_m - \Delta H_{cc}}{\Delta H_m^{\circ} \times w_{polymer}} \times 100$$

Where:
- $\Delta H_m$ = measured melting enthalpy (J/g)
- $\Delta H_{cc}$ = cold crystallization enthalpy (J/g)
- $\Delta H_m^{\circ}$ = melting enthalpy of 100% crystalline polymer
- $w_{polymer}$ = weight fraction of polymer in composite

**Reference $\Delta H_m^{\circ}$ values (Board-Exam Gold):**

| Polymer | $\Delta H_m^{\circ}$ (J/g) |
|---|---|
| PLA | 93.6 |
| PHB | 146 |
| Starch | 326 (anhydrous) |
| Cellulose I | 220–240 |

### 2.2.3 Dynamic Mechanical Analysis (DMA)

- Applies **oscillatory stress** at variable temperature and frequency.
- Provides **storage modulus (E')**, **loss modulus (E'')**, and **tan δ**.

$$\tan \delta = \frac{E''}{E'}$$

- The peak of tan δ gives **$T_g$ (more sensitive than DSC)** for amorphous biopolymers.

---

## 2.3 Cluster III — Mechanical Characterization

### 2.3.1 Tensile Testing (ASTM D638 / ISO 527)

- A **dog-bone specimen** is stretched at constant crosshead speed (typically 1–50 mm/min).
- **Stress** is force per unit original cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{A_0}$$

- **Strain** is the change in length per unit original gauge length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

- **Young's Modulus (E)** from the linear elastic region:

$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \bigg|_{\text{elastic region}}$$

### 2.3.2 Key Mechanical Outputs

| Property | Symbol | Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tensile strength | $\sigma_{UTS}$ | MPa | Maximum stress before failure |
| Young's modulus | E | MPa (or GPa) | Stiffness |
| Elongation at break | $\varepsilon_b$ | % | Ductility |
| Flexural strength | $\sigma_f$ | MPa | Bending resistance |
| Impact strength (Izod/Charpy) | $I$ | kJ/m² | Toughness |
| Hardness (Shore D) | — | dimensionless | Surface resistance |

### 2.3.3 Rule of Mixtures (ROM) for Biocomposites

For a unidirectional fibre-reinforced biocomposite (e.g., flax fibre / PLA):

$$E_c = \eta_l \cdot \eta_o \cdot V_f \cdot E_f + (1 - V_f) \cdot E_m$$

Where:
- $E_c$ = composite modulus
- $E_f$ = fibre modulus, $E_m$ = matrix modulus
- $V_f$ = fibre volume fraction
- $\eta_l$ = length efficiency factor (≈ 1 if fibre >> critical length)
- $\eta_o$ = orientation factor (= 1 for unidirectional; = 3/8 for random 2D; = 1/5 for random 3D)

---

## 2.4 Cluster IV — Morphological & Crystallographic Characterization

### 2.4.1 Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)

- Resolution: ~1–10 nm. Provides **topographical contrast**.
- **Sample prep**: Sputter-coat with gold (Au) for non-conductive biopolymers.

### 2.4.2 Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM)

- Resolution: ~0.1 nm. Provides **internal structural contrast**.
- Used to image **nanocellulose**, **fibrils**, and **lamellae** in semi-crystalline biopolymers.

### 2.4.3 Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)

- A nanoscale **cantilever-tip** raster-scans the surface.
- Modes: Contact, Tapping, Non-contact.
- Outputs: 3D topography, surface roughness ($R_a$, $R_q$).

### 2.4.4 X-Ray Diffraction (XRD)

- Bragg's Law governs diffraction:

$$n\lambda = 2 d_{hkl} \sin \theta$$

- **Crystallinity index (CI)** for cellulose (Segal's method):

$$CI (\%) = \frac{I_{002} - I_{am}}{I_{002}} \times 100$$

Where $I_{002}$ is the intensity of the (002) crystalline peak (2θ ≈ 22.5°) and $I_{am}$ is the amorphous intensity (2θ ≈ 18°).

---

## 2.5 Cluster V — Degradation & Barrier Characterization

### 2.5.1 Biodegradation Testing

**Soil Burial Test (ASTM D5988 / ISO 17556):**
- Specimens are buried in **active compost / soil** at controlled T (25–58 °C) and RH.
- Retrieved at intervals, washed, dried, and weighed.

$$\text{Weight Loss (\%)} = \frac{W_0 - W_t}{W_0} \times 100$$

Where $W_0$ = initial dry weight, $W_t$ = dry weight at time $t$.

**Hydrolytic Degradation (in vitro):**
- Samples immersed in **PBS (phosphate-buffered saline) pH 7.4** at 37 °C.
- Mimics physiological conditions for biomedical polymers.

### 2.5.2 Water Vapor Permeability (WVP)

The rate of moisture transmission through a biopolymer film (e.g., PLA packaging):

$$WVP = \frac{WVTR \times L}{\Delta p}$$

Where:
- $WVTR$ = water vapor transmission rate (g/m²/day)
- $L$ = film thickness (m)
- $\Delta p$ = partial pressure difference of water vapor across the film (Pa)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units of WVP:** $\frac{\text{g} \cdot \text{m}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{day} \cdot \text{Pa}}$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\chi_c = \dfrac{\Delta H_m - \Delta H_{cc}}{\Delta H_m^{\circ} \cdot w_p}\times 100$ | Crystallinity from DSC | % |
| 2 | $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$ | Young's modulus | MPa |
| 3 | $\sigma = F / A_0$ | Engineering stress | MPa |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Engineering strain | dimensionless |
| 5 | $E_c = \eta_l \eta_o V_f E_f + (1-V_f)E_m$ | ROM for composite modulus | MPa |
| 6 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | Bragg's Law (XRD) | nm, ° |
| 7 | $CI = (I_{002} - I_{am})/I_{002}\times 100$ | Segal crystallinity index | % |
| 8 | $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ | Viscoelastic damping | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{Weight loss} = (W_0 - W_t)/W_0 \times 100$ | Biodegradation extent | % |
| 10 | $WVP = WVTR \cdot L / \Delta p$ | Water vapor permeability | g·m/m²·day·Pa |
| 11 | $D = M_w / M_n$ | Polydispersity index | dimensionless |
| 12 | $M_n = \sum N_i M_i / \sum N_i$ | Number-average MW | g/mol |
| 13 | $M_w = \sum N_i M_i^2 / \sum N_i M_i$ | Weight-average MW | g/mol |
| 14 | $M_c = 2\rho R T / G_N^0$ | Critical molecular weight (entanglement) | g/mol |
| 15 | $\eta_{inh} = \ln(\eta_r)/c$ | Inherent viscosity (solution) | dL/g |
| 16 | $[\eta] = K M^a$ | Mark–Houwink–Sakurada (intrinsic viscosity) | dL/g |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** This formula set covers **80% of numerical problems** asked in KTU ESE for the course PEPOT415. Memorize the Segal equation, ROM equation, and the crystallinity equation — these are the **most frequently tested**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example 1: Crystallinity of a PLA/Cellulose Biocomposite

> **Problem Statement (KTU-ESE style):**
> A PLA biocomposite film contains **25 wt% cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs)** and **75 wt% PLA**. DSC analysis gives:
> - $\Delta H_m$ (melting enthalpy of composite) = **48.2 J/g**
> - $\Delta H_{cc}$ (cold crystallization enthalpy) = **8.5 J/g**
> - Reference enthalpy of 100% crystalline PLA: $\Delta H_m^{\circ}$ = **93.6 J/g**
>
> Calculate the **degree of crystallinity ($\chi_c$) of PLA in the composite**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the equation to use.**

For semi-crystalline polymers with a cold-crystallization exotherm preceding the melting endotherm:

$$\chi_c (\%) = \frac{\Delta H_m - \Delta H_{cc}}{\Delta H_m^{\circ} \times w_{polymer}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values.**

$$\Delta H_m = 48.2 \; \text{J/g}, \quad \Delta H_{cc} = 8.5 \; \text{J/g}, \quad \Delta H_m^{\circ} = 93.6 \; \text{J/g}, \quad w_{polymer} = 0.75$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (net melting enthalpy).**

$$\Delta H_m - \Delta H_{cc} = 48.2 - 8.5 = 39.7 \; \text{J/g}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator (theoretical 100% crystalline heat of fusion, mass-normalised for the polymer fraction).**

$$\Delta H_m^{\circ} \times w_{polymer} = 93.6 \times 0.75 = 70.2 \; \text{J/g}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the crystallinity ratio.**

$$\chi_c = \frac{39.7}{70.2} = 0.5655$$

**Step 6 — Convert to percentage.**

$$\chi_c (\%) = 0.5655 \times 100 = 56.55 \; \%$$

**Result (final answer):**

$$\boxed{\chi_c \;(\text{PLA in composite}) \approx 56.6 \; \%}$$

**Interpretation:** This means that **56.6% of the PLA matrix in the composite is crystalline**. The CNCs act as **nucleating agents**, increasing PLA crystallinity (which for neat PLA is typically 30–40%). This is a classic **synergistic effect** in biocomposites and is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example 2: Segal Crystallinity Index of Microcrystalline Cellulose

> **Problem Statement:**
> XRD analysis of a cellulose sample gives the following peak intensities:
> - $I_{002}$ (crystalline peak at 2θ = 22.5°) = **8 800 counts**
> - $I_{am}$ (amorphous halo at 2θ = 18.0°) = **2 100 counts**
>
> Calculate the **Segal crystallinity index (CI)**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall Segal's equation.**

$$CI (\%) = \frac{I_{002} - I_{am}}{I_{002}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the intensities.**

$$CI = \frac{8800 - 2100}{8800} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Numerator evaluation.**

$$8800 - 2100 = 6700$$

**Step 4 — Ratio and percentage.**

$$CI = \frac{6700}{8800} \times 100 = 0.7614 \times 100 = 76.14 \;\%$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{CI \;(\text{cellulose}) \approx 76.1 \;\%}$$

**Interpretation:** This is typical for **microcrystalline cellulose (MCC)** which has CI in the 70–85% range. Native cotton cellulose has CI ≈ 90%; mercerised (NaOH-treated) cellulose can drop to 60–70%; regenerated cellulose (e.g., viscose) drops to 30–40%.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example 3: Young's Modulus of a Flax/PLA Biocomposite

> **Problem Statement:**
> A short-fibre flax/PLA biocomposite contains **30 vol% flax fibres** randomly oriented in 2D within a PLA matrix. Given:
> - $E_f$ (flax fibre modulus) = **70 GPa**
> - $E_m$ (PLA matrix modulus) = **3.5 GPa**
> - $\eta_l$ (length efficiency) = **0.9** (fibres longer than critical length)
> - $\eta_o$ (orientation factor, 2D random) = **3/8 = 0.375**
>
> Estimate the composite modulus $E_c$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the modified Rule of Mixtures.**

$$E_c = \eta_l \cdot \eta_o \cdot V_f \cdot E_f + (1 - V_f) \cdot E_m$$

**Step 2 — Compute the fibre contribution term.**

$$\eta_l \cdot \eta_o \cdot V_f \cdot E_f = 0.9 \times 0.375 \times 0.30 \times 70 \; \text{GPa}$$

$$= 0.9 \times 0.375 = 0.3375$$

$$0.3375 \times 0.30 = 0.10125$$

$$0.10125 \times 70 = 7.0875 \; \text{GPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the matrix contribution term.**

$$(1 - V_f) \cdot E_m = (1 - 0.30) \times 3.5 = 0.70 \times 3.5 = 2.45 \; \text{GPa}$$

**Step 4 — Sum both contributions.**

$$E_c = 7.0875 + 2.45 = 9.5375 \; \text{GPa}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{E_c \approx 9.54 \; \text{GPa}}$$

**Interpretation:** Compared to neat PLA (3.5 GPa), adding 30% randomly oriented flax fibres **nearly triples the modulus**. For unidirectional alignment ($\eta_o = 1$), the modulus could exceed **20 GPa**. This is why **fibre orientation is the single most critical design variable** in biocomposite engineering.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example 4: Biodegradation Weight Loss Kinetics

> **Problem Statement:**
> A starch/PVA blend film is subjected to soil burial. Initial mass $W_0$ = **250 mg**. After 30 days, the retrieved mass (dried) $W_{30}$ = **185 mg**. After 60 days, $W_{60}$ = **120 mg**.
>
> Compute the **% weight loss at 30 and 60 days**, and **estimate the average daily degradation rate**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — At 30 days.**

$$\text{WL}_{30} = \frac{W_0 - W_{30}}{W_0} \times 100 = \frac{250 - 185}{250} \times 100 = \frac{65}{250} \times 100 = 26 \;\%$$

**Step 2 — At 60 days.**

$$\text{WL}_{60} = \frac{250 - 120}{250} \times 100 = \frac{130}{250} \times 100 = 52 \;\%$$

**Step 3 — Average daily degradation rate (over 60 days).**

$$r = \frac{130 \; \text{mg}}{60 \; \text{days}} = 2.167 \; \text{mg/day}$$

Or, normalised to initial mass:

$$r_{norm} = \frac{52 \;\%}{60 \; \text{days}} = 0.867 \; \%/\text{day}$$

**Step 4 — Insight: Kinetics often follow first-order decay.**

$$M_t = M_0 \cdot e^{-kt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = -\frac{1}{t} \ln\left(\frac{M_t}{M_0}\right)$$

At $t$ = 60 d, $M_t/M_0$ = 120/250 = 0.48:

$$k = -\frac{1}{60} \ln(0.48) = -\frac{-0.734}{60} = 0.01223 \; \text{day}^{-1}$$

**Result Summary:**

| Time (days) | Mass (mg) | Weight Loss (%) | Degradation Rate |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 250 | 0 | — |
| 30 | 185 | 26.0 | 2.17 mg/d |
| 60 | 120 | 52.0 | 2.17 mg/d |

$$\boxed{\text{First-order rate constant } k \approx 0.0122 \; \text{day}^{-1} \;(\text{half-life} \approx 56.7 \; \text{days})}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: "Which Technique for Which Question?"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **KTU Board-favourite 14-mark question type.** The examiner asks: *"You are provided with a novel biopolymer composite. Describe in detail the characterization techniques you would employ to fully evaluate its structure and properties, justifying each choice."* The matrix below gives the **exam-ready answer structure.**

| Engineering Question | Characterization Tool | Information Obtained |
|---|---|---|
| "What is its chemical structure?" | **FTIR** | Functional groups, chemical modifications |
| "What is the molecular architecture (stereoregularity, copolymer composition)?" | **¹H / ¹³C NMR** | Tacticity, sequence distribution |
| "Is it thermally stable for melt processing?" | **TGA** | $T_{onset}$, char residue |
| "What is its $T_g$ and crystallinity?" | **DSC** | $T_g$, $T_m$, $\chi_c$ |
| "What is its stiffness and damping behaviour?" | **DMA** | $E'$, $E''$, $\tan\delta$ |
| "What is its tensile strength and modulus?" | **UTM (ASTM D638)** | $\sigma_{UTS}$, E, $\varepsilon_b$ |
| "How does the fibre disperse in the matrix?" | **SEM** | Fracture surface, fibre–matrix adhesion |
| "Is it crystalline? What crystal form?" | **XRD** | Crystal structure, CI, crystallite size |
| "What is its surface roughness (for cell adhesion)?" | **AFM** | $R_a$, 3D topography |
| "How fast does it biodegrade?" | **Soil burial + gravimetry** | Weight loss vs. time |
| "Does it allow water vapour through (for food packaging)?" | **WVP / WVTR test** | Permeability coefficient |
| "What is its molecular weight?" | **GPC / SEC** | $M_n$, $M_w$, $D$ (PDI) |
| "Is the surface chemistry right for cell attachment?" | **XPS** + **contact angle** | Surface functional groups, wettability |
| "Is it haemocompatible / cytocompatible?" | **MTT assay, hemolysis test** | Cell viability, ISO 10993 compliance |

---

## 3.6 Schematic Comparison: Crystallinity Calculation Methods

The Segal XRD method and DSC method give different absolute crystallinity values for the same material. The matrix below clarifies the **why**.

| Parameter | Segal XRD Method | DSC Method |
|---|---|---|
| Property measured | Diffraction peak intensity ratio | Heat of fusion |
| Equation | $CI = (I_{002} - I_{am})/I_{002}\times100$ | $\chi_c = (\Delta H_m - \Delta H_{cc})/(\Delta H_m^{\circ} \cdot w_p)\times 100$ |
| What it captures | Lateral crystallite order (long-range 3D order) | Total crystalline mass fraction (including defective crystals) |
| Typical value for cellulose | 60–90% | 30–60% (lower, more conservative) |
| Sensitivity to orientation | High | Low |
| Sample prep | Powder, pressed pellet | Small disc in aluminium pan |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-exam tip:** When asked to compare Segal and DSC, emphasise that **Segal is empirical** (a peak-height ratio, not a true mass fraction), whereas **DSC measures the actual thermal signature** of crystalline phase melting and is therefore thermodynamically rigorous.
