Comparing studies with different boundaries is invalid. --- #### **Model Solution for Q4(b) — GWP and AP Calculation (7 marks)** **Part (i) — GWP₁₀₀ Calculation (3.5 marks)** Using the same procedure as Q3(b): $$ \text{GWP}_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.9 \times 1 = 0.90 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} $$ $$ \text{GWP}_{\text{CH}_4} = 0.03 \times 28 = 0.84 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} $$ $$ \text{GWP}_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 0.0008 \times 265 = 0.212 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{GWP}_{100,\text{PHA}} = 0.90 + 0.84 + 0.212 = 1.952 \text{ kg CO}_2\text{-eq per kg of PHA}} $$ **Part (ii) — Acidification Potential Calculation (3.5 marks)** The AP is calculated using the CML 2001 characterization factors: $$ \text{AP} = \sum_i m_i \cdot \text{CF}_{i,\text{AP}} $$ Given: - $\text{CF}_{\text{NO}_x} = 1.6$ kg SO₂-eq/kg - $\text{CF}_{\text{SO}_2} = 1.2$ kg SO₂-eq/kg (Note: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O do not contribute to AP.) $$ \text{AP}_{\text{NO}_x} = 0.012 \times 1.6 = 0.0192 \text{ kg SO}_2\text{-eq} $$ $$ \text{AP}_{\text{SO}_2} = 0.005 \times 1.2 = 0.0060 \text{ kg SO}_2\text{-eq} $$ $$ \boxed{\text{AP}_{\text{PHA}} = 0.0192 + 0.0060 = 0.0252 \text{ kg SO}_2\text{-eq per kg of PHA}} $$ **Interpretation:** PHA shows a much lower GWP than PLA, confirming its strong carbon-footprint advantage — but AP depends on fertilizer use upstream. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Forgetting the Functional Unit:** Carrying out an LCA without declaring the FU loses 1–2 marks immediately. The FU is non-negotiable in Phase 1. > 2. **Confusing Cradle-to-Gate with Cradle-to-Grave:** Many answers mix them. The boundary MUST match the goal. > 3. **Using outdated CFs:** Older IPCC SAR (1995) factors (e.g., CH₄ = 21) or AR4 (CH₄ = 25) are sometimes used incorrectly. KTU expects **AR5** values: CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265. > 4. **Confusing GWP with AP:** GWP uses **CO₂-eq**; AP uses **SO₂-eq**. Mixing units is a common 1–2 mark deduction. > 5. **Treating Normalization/Weighting as mandatory:** These are OPTIONAL per ISO 14040. Weighting introduces subjective bias and is **not allowed** in comparative public claims without disclosure. > 6. **Skipping the four phases:** A descriptive answer that just defines LCA without naming the four phases loses structural marks. > 7. **Omitting units in the final answer:** Always write "**kg CO₂-eq per kg of polymer**" — not just a number. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU ESE** - **LCA = ISO 14040 / 14044 standardized method** for cradle-to-grave environmental evaluation. - **Four mandatory phases:** Goal & Scope → LCI → LCIA → Interpretation (with iterative review). - **Functional Unit (FU):** Quantified reference performance; the basis of all comparisons. - **System Boundary types:** Cradle-to-Grave, Cradle-to-Gate, Gate-to-Gate, Cradle-to-Cradle. - **Allocation:** Needed for co-products (e.g., glycerol in biodiesel); ISO hierarchy: subdivision → system expansion → allocation. - **LCI = data collection** (inputs and outputs per FU). - **LCIA = data translation** to impact scores. - **LCIA mandatory steps:** Classification + Characterization. - **LCIA optional steps:** Normalization, Grouping, Weighting. - **Weighting is subjective** → ISO 14040 restricts its use in comparative assertions to the public. - **Common impact categories:** GWP (kg CO₂-eq), AP (kg SO₂-eq), EP (kg PO₄-eq), ODP (kg CFC-11-eq), POCP (kg C₂H₄-eq), ADP (kg Sb-eq), HTP (kg 1,4-DCB-eq), Land Use (m²·year). - **Master formula:** $I = \sum m_i \cdot \text{CF}_i$ - **IPCC AR5 factors:** $\text{GWP}_{\text{CO}_2} = 1$, $\text{GWP}_{\text{CH}_4} = 28$, $\text{GWP}_{\text{N}_2\text{O}} = 265$. - **CML 2001 AP factors (commonly used):** $\text{CF}_{\text{NO}_x} \approx 1.6$, $\text{CF}_{\text{SO}_2} \approx 1.2$ (kg SO₂-eq/kg). - **Cumulative Energy Demand (CED):** $\text{CED} = E_{\text{renew}} + E_{\text{non-renew}}$. - **LCA prevents burden shifting** — solves one environmental problem without creating another. - **Bio-based ≠ automatically greener** — only LCA can prove it. - **Common LCA software:** SimaPro, GaBi, OpenLCA; **databases:** ecoinvent, USLCI, ELCD. - **Biopolymers LCA hotspots:** Land use change, fertilizer (eutrophication), agricultural emissions (N₂O), energy mix in polymerization. - **Bio-PE shows negative GWP** in some cradle-to-gate studies due to biogenic carbon credits — but be careful with methodology. - **Always mention the FU explicitly** in any numerical LCA answer to earn full marks. - **Cite the standard (ISO 14040/14044)** wherever relevant — examiners reward this.

topics.title: Monomers from Bio Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT415 - ADVANCED BIOPOLYMERS & BIOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Monomers from Bio Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **bio-based monomer** is a low-molecular-weight, reactive chemical building block derived wholly or partially from **renewable biological feedstocks** (biomass), which can undergo polymerization (polycondensation, polyaddition, or ring-opening polymerization) to yield **biopolymers** or **bio-based polymers**. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PEPOT415, monomers from bio-sources are classified based on the type of platform chemistry (sugar, oil, lignin, syngas) and the class of functional groups (hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, epoxide, diene, furan, etc.) they carry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT415 – Module 3)**
> A *bio-monomer* is **not** the same as a *biodegradable monomer*. A bio-monomer refers to the **origin of carbon** (renewable biomass), whereas biodegradability refers to the **end-of-life degradation pathway**. Polyethylene made from bio-ethylene is bio-based but NOT biodegradable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a polymer as a **train**. Each monomer is a **railway coach** with standardized "couplings" (functional groups) at both ends. Traditional petro-chemistry builds these coaches from **crude oil** (a finite, non-renewable underground stock). Bio-sourced monomers, in contrast, are coaches built from **sugarcane juice, corn starch, castor oil, or sawdust** — i.e., a **farm-grown, renewable inventory**.

- The **monomer's functional group** = the coupling hook (–OH, –COOH, –NH₂, epoxy, etc.).
- The **polymerization reaction** = the act of linking coaches.
- The **renewable feedstock** = the source of the raw steel for the coach.

A single piece of sugarcane can, through fermentation, give **lactic acid** → which becomes the repeating unit of **Polylactic Acid (PLA)**. So a plant literally becomes a plastic, which — after use — can be composted back into CO₂ and water, closing the natural carbon cycle.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Renewable Carbon Content (RCC)** | $\geq 50\%$ (ASTM D6866) | Defines "bio-based" labelling |
| **Bio-based Carbon Content (BCC)** | $0 - 100\%$ | Measured by $^{14}C$ radiocarbon dating |
| **Carbon Efficiency** | $\eta_C = \dfrac{\text{C in product}}{\text{C in feedstock}} \times 100\%$ | Atom-economy metric |
| **E-factor** (Environmental factor) | $\dfrac{\text{kg of waste}}{\text{kg of product}}$ | Green-chemistry metric |
| **Standard Temperature** | $298.15\ \text{K}$ ($\mathbf{25\ ^\circ C}$) | Reference for thermodynamic data |
| **Standard Pressure** | $101.325\ \text{kPa}$ ($\mathbf{1\ atm}$) | Reference for thermodynamic data |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a problem states "renewable" or "bio-based", immediately distinguish it from "biodegradable" or "compostable" — examiners test this distinction every year.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Bio-Monomers

Bio-monomers are organized in **three orthogonal axes** in the KTU 2024 Scheme:

### Axis A – By Carbon Platform (Feedstock Origin)

1. **Sugar / Starch Platform**
   - C₆ sugars (glucose, fructose) from corn, sugarcane, sugar beet.
   - C₅ sugars (xylose, arabinose) from hemicellulose.
   - Yields: lactic acid, succinic acid, itaconic acid, sorbitol, 5-HMF.

2. **Lignocellulosic Platform**
   - Cellulose, hemicellulose, lignin.
   - Yields: furfural, 5-HMF, levulinic acid, vanillin, phenols, eugenol.

3. **Oils & Fats Platform (Triglyceride Platform)**
   - Vegetable oils (soy, palm, rapeseed, castor, linseed), algal oils.
   - Yields: glycerol, fatty acids (C8–C22), sebacic acid, azelaic acid, ricinoleic acid, 10-undecenoic acid.

4. **Syngas Platform**
   - From gasification of biomass (CO + H₂).
   - Yields: bio-methanol, bio-ethanol → bio-ethylene, bio-propylene.

5. **Protein / Amino-Acid Platform**
   - Hydrolysis of plant/animal proteins.
   - Yields: amino acids (lysine, glutamic acid, methionine).

### Axis B – By Functional Group

| Functional Group | Example Bio-Monomer | Resulting Polymer |
|---|---|---|
| **Diol + Diacid** (AA + BB type) | 1,3-Propanediol + Succinic acid | **Poly(trimethylene succinate)** |
| **Hydroxy acid (AB type)** | Lactic acid, Glycolic acid | **PLA, PGA** |
| **Diamine + Diacid** (AA + BB) | Putrescine + Sebacic acid | **Polyamide 4,10** |
| **Diacid** | Itaconic acid, FDCA | **Polyesters, Polyamides** |
| **Diol** | 1,3-PDO, 1,4-BDO, Isosorbide | **Polyurethanes, Polyesters** |
| **Epoxide** | Epoxidized soybean oil (ESBO) | **Epoxy resins** |
| **Furan** | 2,5-FDCA, 5-HMF, Furfural | **PEF (polyethylene furanoate)** |
| **Terpene / Rosin** | β-Pinene, Abietic acid | **Polyterpenes, Rosin esters** |

### Axis C – By Generation

- **1st generation:** From edible sugar/starch (corn, sugarcane) — ethical concerns (food vs. fuel).
- **2nd generation:** From non-edible lignocellulosic biomass (wood, bagasse, straw).
- **3rd generation:** From algal biomass.
- **4th generation:** Engineered microorganisms (synthetic biology, electrofuels).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Platform Choice

- **Sugar platform** → Highest selectivity, mature fermentation (e.g., **Lactobacillus** for lactic acid); but competes with food.
- **Lignocellulosic platform** → Abundant, non-food; but requires harsh pretreatment (dilute acid, steam explosion, organosolv).
- **Oil platform** → Built-in **C8–C18 long chains** (no need to build them via chemistry); excellent for polyamides, polyurethanes.
- **Syngas platform** → Independent of sugar chemistry; Fischer–Tropsch route to bio-hydrocarbons.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|---|---|
| $RCC = \dfrac{^{14}C_{\text{sample}}}{^{14}C_{\text{modern}}} \times 100\%$ | Renewable Carbon Content | $0 - 100\ \%$ |
| $\text{CE} = \dfrac{M_{\text{desired}}}{M_{\text{all products}}} \times 100\%$ | Chemical (Carbon) Efficiency | $0 - 100\ \%$ |
| $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\text{g product}}{\text{g substrate}}$ | Fermentation Yield | dimensionless, $\vert$ or $\mid$ bounds |
| $\eta = \dfrac{[\eta]_{\text{intrinsic}}}{c}$ | Intrinsic viscosity (Mark–Houwink) | $\text{dL/g}$ |
| $\text{E-factor} = \dfrac{\sum W_{\text{waste}}}{W_{\text{product}}}$ | Environmental factor | $\text{kg/kg}$ |
| $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T \Delta S^\circ$ | Spontaneity of bio-conversion | $\text{kJ/mol}$ |
| $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | Number-average MW of monomer mix | $\text{g/mol}$ |
| $\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{M_n}{M_0}$ | Degree of polymerization | dimensionless |
| $[\text{M}] + [\text{M}] \xrightarrow{k} \text{Dimer}$ | Second-order step polymerization rate | $-\dfrac{d[\text{M}]}{dt} = k[\text{M}]^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX SAFEGUARD:** Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` — never the bare pipe `|` inside a markdown table, as it would split the column.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Packaging industry:** PLA (from lactic acid) replaces PET; PEF (from FDCA) replaces PET with superior O₂ barrier.
- **Automotive:** Castor-oil-derived **PA 6,10** and **PA 10,10** replace petroleum PA 6,6 in radiator tanks, fuel lines.
- **Medical implants:** PGA, PLA, PCL from respective hydroxy-acids/alcohols.
- **3D Printing (FDM):** PLA filament is the dominant bio-based 3D-printer feedstock.
- **Coatings & Adhesives:** Rosin (abietic acid) and epoxidized soybean oil as tackifiers and reactive diluents.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Lactic Acid from Glucose — Mass & Carbon Balance

This is a classic KTU long-answer derivation. **Glucose** ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6$) is fermented to **lactic acid** ($\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3$) via the **Embden–Meyerhof–Parnas (EMP) pathway** by *Lactobacillus delbrueckii*.

### Step 1 — Write the Stoichiometric Equation

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3
$$

### Step 2 — Verify Carbon Balance

$$
6\ \text{C atoms} = 2 \times 3\ \text{C atoms} = 6\ \text{C atoms}\ \checkmark
$$

### Step 3 — Verify Hydrogen Balance

$$
12\ \text{H atoms (left)} = 2 \times 6\ \text{H atoms (right)} = 12\ \text{H atoms}\ \checkmark
$$

### Step 4 — Verify Oxygen Balance

$$
6\ \text{O atoms (left)} = 2 \times 3\ \text{O atoms (right)} = 6\ \text{O atoms}\ \checkmark
$$

### Step 5 — Calculate Theoretical Mass Yield

Molar mass of glucose: $M_{\text{glucose}} = 180.16\ \text{g/mol}$.

Molar mass of lactic acid: $M_{\text{LA}} = 90.08\ \text{g/mol}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Yield} &= \dfrac{2 \times M_{\text{LA}}}{1 \times M_{\text{glucose}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{2 \times 90.08}{180.16} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{180.16}{180.16} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= 100.00\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The conversion is theoretically carbon-perfect (100 % theoretical mass yield). In industrial reality, the yield is **~95 %** due to cell-mass and CO₂ side-reactions (e.g., heterolactic fermentation).

### Step 6 — Carbon Efficiency (CE)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CE}_{\text{LA}} &= \dfrac{3 \times 12.011}{6 \times 12.011} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{36.033}{72.066} \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 50.00\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only 50 % of the carbon ends up in lactic acid; the other 50 % is lost as CO₂ (or biomass). This explains why lactic acid prices are tightly linked to sugar prices.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Number-Average Molecular Weight of a Monomer Blend

A bio-polyester is synthesized from a 1:1 molar mixture of lactic acid ($M_1 = 90.08\ \text{g/mol}$) and glycolic acid ($M_2 = 76.05\ \text{g/mol}$).

### Step 1 — Moles

Let $n_1 = n_2 = 1\ \text{mol}$.

### Step 2 — Number-Average Molecular Weight

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_n &= \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(1)(90.08) + (1)(76.05)}{1 + 1} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{166.13}{2} \\[6pt]
&= 83.065\ \text{g/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Repeat-Unit Average (used in $\text{DP}_n$)

For step-polymerization of mixed AA + BB:

$$
\text{DP}_n = \dfrac{1 + r}{1 + r - 2rp}
$$

where $r$ = stoichiometric ratio $\leq 1$, $p$ = conversion. For $r = 1,\ p = 0.98$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DP}_n &= \dfrac{1 + 1}{1 + 1 - 2(1)(0.98)} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2}{0.04} \\[4pt]
&= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Resulting Polymer $M_n$

$$
M_{n,\text{polymer}} = \text{DP}_n \times M_{n,\text{repeat}} = 50 \times 83.065 = 4153.25\ \text{g/mol}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Major Bio-Monomers — Source → Conversion → Polymer

| # | Monomer | Molecular Formula | Bio-Source | Conversion Route | Resulting Polymer | MW (g/mol) | KTU-Mention Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lactic acid** | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3$ | Corn starch, sugarcane | Fermentation by *Lactobacillus* | **PLA** | 90.08 | Very High |
| 2 | **Glycolic acid** | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4\text{O}_3$ | Sugarcane, sugar-beet | Chemical catalysis / fermentation | **PGA** | 76.05 | High |
| 3 | **3-Hydroxypropionic acid (3-HPA)** | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_6\text{O}_3$ | Glycerol, glucose | Engineered *E. coli* | **P3HP**, acrylic acid | 90.08 | High |
| 4 | **Succinic acid** | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_6\text{O}_4$ | Glucose, glycerol | Fermentation (*A. succinogenes*) | **PBS, PBT substitutes** | 118.09 | Very High |
| 5 | **Itaconic acid** | $\text{C}_5\text{H}_6\text{O}_4$ | Glucose, molasses | Fermentation (*A. terreus*) | **Poly(itaconic)** | 130.10 | High |
| 6 | **Sebacic acid** | $\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{18}\text{O}_4$ | Castor oil | Oxidative cleavage of ricinoleic acid | **PA 6,10; PA 10,10** | 202.25 | High |
| 7 | **Azelaic acid** | $\text{C}_9\text{H}_{16}\text{O}_4$ | Oleic acid (soy) | Ozonolysis / oxidative cleavage | **PA 6,9; plasticizers** | 188.22 | Medium |
| 8 | **Glycerol** | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$ | Biodiesel by-product | Crude glycerol → epichlorohydrin | **Epoxy resins, polyols** | 92.09 | Very High |
| 9 | **2,5-FDCA** | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{O}_5$ | Fructose → HMF → oxidation | Catalytic dehydration + oxidation | **PEF (PET replacement)** | 156.09 | Very High |
| 10 | **Sorbitol** | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{14}\text{O}_6$ | Glucose hydrogenation | Catalytic hydrogenation | **Isosorbide, polyols, polyurethanes** | 182.17 | High |
| 11 | **Isosorbide** | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_4$ | Sorbitol | Double dehydration | **PEIT, PEIs, bio-PC** | 146.14 | Medium |
| 12 | **Bio-ethylene** | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_4$ | Bio-ethanol (sugarcane) | Catalytic dehydration | **Bio-PE, bio-EVA** | 28.05 | Very High |
| 13 | **1,3-Propanediol (1,3-PDO)** | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8\text{O}_2$ | Glycerol or glucose | Fermentation (*E. coli*, *C. butyricum*) | **PTT (Sorona®)** | 76.09 | High |
| 14 | **1,4-Butanediol (1,4-BDO)** | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}\text{O}_2$ | Glucose / succinic acid | Fermentation + hydrogenation | **Bio-PBT, bio-PBS** | 90.12 | High |
| 15 | **Epoxidized soybean oil (ESBO)** | $\sim \text{C}_{57}\text{H}_{98}\text{O}_{10}$ | Soybean oil | Prilezhaev epoxidation (peracid) | **Plasticizer, PVC stabilizer, epoxy resin** | ~ 1000 | High |
| 16 | **Ricinoleic acid** | $\text{C}_{18}\text{H}_{34}\text{O}_3$ | Castor oil (90 % of FA) | Direct extraction | **PA 11 monomer, estolides** | 298.46 | Medium |
| 17 | **Vanillin** | $\text{C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_3$ | Lignin / ferulic acid | Oxidation | **Polyphenols, epoxy precursors** | 152.15 | Medium |
| 18 | **β-Pinene** | $\text{C}_{10}\text{H}_{16}$ | Turpentine (pine) | Distillation | **Polyterpenes, terpene-phenols** | 136.23 | Low–Medium |

> [!NOTE]
> The above matrix is the **single most high-yield study artefact** for KTU Module-3 questions. Memorize at least 10 of these (name → source → polymer).

## 3.4 Derivation of Theoretical Atom Economy for Bio-Ethylene Dehydration

Bio-ethanol $\rightarrow$ bio-ethylene + water.

$$
\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_2\text{H}_4 + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Atom Economy (AE)} &= \dfrac{M_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_4}}{M_{\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}}} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{28.05}{46.07} \times 100\% \\[6pt]
&= 60.89\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

Carbon balance: 2 C $\rightarrow$ 2 C $\checkmark$ (100 %).

Hydrogen balance: 6 H $\rightarrow$ 4 H + 2 H (in H₂O) $\checkmark$.

The 39.11 % mass loss is structural water. The E-factor for this step is small (catalyst recovery is the main waste stream).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Four Bio-Monomer Platforms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedstock["RENEWABLE FEEDSTOCK INVENTORY"]
    feedstock --> s1["1. Sugar and Starch Platform"]
    feedstock --> s2["2. Lignocellulosic Platform"]
    feedstock --> s3["3. Oils and Fats Platform"]
    feedstock --> s4["4. Syngas Platform"]

    s1 --> s1a["C6 sugars glucose fructose"]
    s1 --> s1b["C5 sugars xylose arabinose"]
    s1a --> p1a["Fermentation by Lactobacillus"]
    p1a --> m1a["LACTIC ACID C3H6O3"]
    s1a --> p1b["Fermentation by Actinobacillus"]
    p1b --> m1b["SUCCINIC ACID C4H6O4"]
    s1b --> p1c["Acid hydrolysis"]
    p1c --> m1c["FURFURAL C5H4O2"]

    s2 --> s2a["Cellulose and Hemicellulose"]
    s2 --> s2b["Lignin fraction"]
    s2a --> p2a["Catalytic dehydration"]
    p2a --> m2a["5 HMF and Levulinic acid"]
    s2b --> p2b["Hydrogenolysis"]
    p2b --> m2b["VANILLIN and Phenols"]

    s3 --> s3a["Triglycerides"]
    s3a --> p3a["Transesterification"]
    p3a --> m3a["GLYCEROL C3H8O3"]
    s3a --> p3b["Fatty acid splitting"]
    p3b --> m3b["FATTY ACIDS C8 to C22"]

    s4 --> s4a["Biomass gasification"]
    s4a --> p4a["CO and H2 mixture"]
    p4a --> m4a["BIO METHANOL"]
    p4a --> m4b["FISCHER TROPSCH HYDROCARBONS"]

    m1a --> poly["POLYMERIZATION"]
    m1b --> poly
    m2a --> poly
    m3a --> poly
    m4a --> poly
    poly --> out1["PLA"]
    poly --> out2["PBS"]
    poly --> out3["PEF"]
    poly --> out4["EPOXY RESIN"]
    poly --> out5["BIO PE"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between a bio-based monomer and a biodegradable monomer with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **bio-based monomer** originates from **renewable biomass** (e.g., lactic acid from corn starch).
- A **biodegradable monomer** refers to a monomer whose resulting polymer can be **degraded by microorganisms** (e.g., 3-hydroxybutyrate from bacterial fermentation giving PHB).
- The two properties are **independent**: bio-PE from bio-ethylene is bio-based but **not** biodegradable, while petroleum-derived polycaprolactone (PCL) is biodegradable but **not** bio-based. (3 marks for clarity and example)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**List any three bio-monomers obtained from the triglyceride platform and state the polymer they yield.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Glycerol** (from biodiesel) → **epichlorohydrin** → **epoxy resins** (1 mark)
2. **Sebacic acid** (from castor oil) → **Polyamide 6,10 / 10,10** (1 mark)
3. **Epoxidized soybean oil (ESBO)** → **plasticizer and reactive diluent for PVC** (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Module-3 Long Answer

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **four major bio-platforms** for the production of bio-monomers, with two specific monomer examples for each platform. Highlight the **advantages and limitations** of each platform. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **fermentative production of lactic acid from glucose**, including the reaction stoichiometry, mass-balance, carbon efficiency, and downstream polymerization to **Polylactic Acid (PLA)**. Explain why PLA is considered a **green polymer**. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** Bio-monomers are derived from renewable feedstocks via four principal platforms based on the type of biomass and conversion technology.

**Platform 1 – Sugar/Starch (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Corn, sugarcane, sugar beet → C₆ sugars (glucose, fructose), C₅ sugars.
- **Monomers:** Lactic acid (via *Lactobacillus*), succinic acid (via *Actinobacillus succinogenes*).
- **Advantages:** High selectivity, mature technology, ambient conditions.
- **Limitations:** Food vs. fuel debate; price volatility linked to crop yield.

**Platform 2 – Lignocellulosic (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Wood, bagasse, straw, rice husk — non-edible.
- **Monomers:** 5-HMF, furfural, levulinic acid (sugar fraction); vanillin, phenols (lignin fraction).
- **Advantages:** Abundant, non-food, low cost.
- **Limitations:** Harsh pretreatment (steam explosion, dilute acid); lignin recalcitrance; expensive enzymes for cellulose hydrolysis.

**Platform 3 – Oils and Fats (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Soybean, palm, rapeseed, castor, linseed, algal oils.
- **Monomers:** Glycerol, sebacic acid (castor), azelaic acid (oleic), epoxidized soybean oil.
- **Advantages:** Built-in long alkyl chains; eliminates C–C bond formation steps.
- **Limitations:** Monomer diversity is constrained by FA chain length; competing with food oleochemistry.

**Platform 4 – Syngas (1.5 marks):**
- **Source:** Gasification of any biomass → CO + H₂.
- **Monomers:** Bio-methanol, bio-ethylene (via methanol-to-olefins), Fischer–Tropsch hydrocarbons.
- **Advantages:** Independent of sugar composition; uses waste biomass.
- **Limitations:** High CAPEX for gasification; tar cleaning; energy-intensive.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Reaction (1 mark):**
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Lactobacillus}} 2\ \text{CH}_3\text{CH(OH)COOH}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry & Mass Balance (1.5 marks):**
- 1 mol glucose (180.16 g) → 2 mol lactic acid (180.16 g), **100 % theoretical mass yield**.
- Industrial actual yield ≈ **95 %** (heterolactic by-products, cell mass).

**Step 3 — Carbon Efficiency (1.5 marks):**
- Glucose: 6 C atoms; Lactic acid: 3 C atoms per molecule × 2 = 6 C atoms.
- All carbon goes to product → **100 % carbon balance**; CE of the EMP route is **theoretically 100 %** (if only homolactic).
- Including cell mass and CO₂ side-paths, realistic CE ≈ **85–90 %**.

**Step 4 — Polymerization to PLA (2 marks):**
- Lactic acid → lactide (cyclic dimer) via thermal depolymerization.
- Ring-Opening Polymerization (ROP) of lactide with Sn(Oct)₂ catalyst at 160–180 °C gives high-MW PLA ($M_w$ up to 10⁶ g/mol).
- Alternative: direct polycondensation of lactic acid — yields only low-MW PLA (DP < 100).

**Step 5 — Why PLA is "Green" (1 mark):**
- Renewable carbon from corn/sugarcane (RCC = 100 %).
- Compostable under industrial conditions (> 60 °C, controlled humidity).
- Lower carbon footprint vs. petroleum PET (~ 1.3 kg CO₂eq/kg PLA vs. ~ 2.7 for PET).
- However, NOT marine-degradable — a common misconception.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Long Answer

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** With neat reaction schemes, explain the **production of 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (FDCA)** from fructose. Discuss its significance as a **PET replacement** in the form of **Polyethylene Furanoate (PEF)**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Write a detailed note on **bio-based polyamides** obtained from castor oil. Include the chemistry of ricinoleic acid conversion to sebacic acid, polymerization with diamines, and list at least three engineering applications. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Fructose to 5-HMF (1.5 marks):**
- Fructose $\xrightarrow{\text{acid catalyst (H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ or ion-exchange resin), 100–200\ ^\circ\text{C, DMSO/water}}$ 5-HMF + 3 H₂O.
- Side reactions: rehydration to levulinic acid + formic acid; polymerization to humins.

**Step 2 — 5-HMF to FDCA (2 marks):**
- Catalytic oxidation of 5-HMF using:
  - Pt or Au catalysts + O₂ in aqueous base, OR
  - Mn/Co/Ni catalysts, OR
  - Electrochemical oxidation (green, ambient).
- Reaction: $\text{C}_6\text{H}_6\text{O}_3 + 1.5\ \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_4\text{O}_5 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$.

**Step 3 — FDCA to PEF (2 marks):**
- PEF synthesis: FDCA + ethylene glycol → dimethyl FDCA → polycondensation at 200–220 °C with Ti(OBu)₄ catalyst.
- PEF is a **furan-based aromatic polyester**, structurally analogous to PET (terephthalic acid replaced by FDCA).

**Step 4 — Why PEF beats PET (1.5 marks):**
- **O₂ barrier:** 10× better than PET (suitable for beer bottles).
- **CO₂ barrier:** 20× better.
- **Mechanical:** Higher $T_g$ (~ 86 °C vs. 70 °C) — hot-fill applications.
- **Renewable carbon:** 100 % bio-based when both monomers are bio-derived.
- **Recyclability:** Chemically recyclable by methanolysis.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Castor Oil Composition (1 mark):**
- ~ 90 % ricinoleic acid (12-hydroxy-9-cis-octadecenoic acid), 12-OH gives unique reactivity.

**Step 2 — Ricinoleic Acid to Sebacic Acid (2 marks):**
- **Pyrolysis** (old route, ~300 °C) gives 10-undecenoic acid + heptanal.
- **Modern oxidative route** uses **nitric acid (HNO₃)** or **ozone (O₃)** to cleave the C=C bond:
  - 1 mol ricinoleic acid + O₃/H₂O₂ → 1 mol azelaic acid (C9) + 1 mol propanal.
  - With HNO₃ oxidation at 50–80 °C, the dominant acid is **sebacic acid (C10)** with a CO₂ as the leaving C1.

**Step 3 — Polycondensation to PA 6,10 / 10,10 (2 marks):**
- Sebacic acid + hexamethylenediamine (HMDA) → PA 6,10 (nylon 6,10).
- Sebacic acid + 1,10-diaminodecane → PA 10,10 (100 % bio-based).
- Process: salt formation → melt polycondensation at 250–280 °C, vacuum.
- $M_w$ up to 30,000 g/mol.

**Step 4 — Engineering Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Automotive fuel lines and brake hoses** — excellent chemical resistance to hydrocarbons and zinc chloride (road salt).
2. **Radiator end-tanks** — high $T_m$ (~ 220 °C) and hydrolytic stability.
3. **Toothbrush bristles, cable ties** — high dimensional stability.
4. **Filaments, fishing nets** — low moisture regain.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Module 3**
> 1. **Confusing bio-based with biodegradable** — 2 to 3 marks are routinely lost for not citing the **ASTM D6866** RCC definition.
> 2. **Writing only feedstock, no conversion** — examiners demand both the **raw material** and the **process** (e.g., not just "sebacic acid from castor oil" but also "ricinoleic acid → oxidative cleavage → sebacic acid").
> 3. **Forgetting catalysts and conditions** — T (°C), P (bar), and catalyst (e.g., Sn(Oct)₂ for ROP, Pt/C for HMF oxidation) are **mandatory** in reaction schemes.
> 4. **Skipping the carbon balance** — a stoichiometric mass-balance derivation fetches **full marks**; skipping it cuts the sub-question score by 50 %.
> 5. **Writing "PLA is biodegradable in nature" without qualifying "industrial composting"** — examiners deduct for over-generalization.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU PEPOT415 Module 3**

- **Definition triad:** Bio-monomer = renewable origin + reactive functional group + polymerizable.
- **ASTM D6866** is the standard method for measuring bio-based carbon.
- **Four platforms** in priority order: Sugar → Lignocellulose → Oils → Syngas.
- **Carbon Efficiency (CE)** is the single most-tested numerical metric.
- **EMP pathway** of *Lactobacillus*: 1 glucose → 2 lactic acid, **100 %** theoretical mass yield, **50 %** CE per carbon-atom.
- **Castor oil is unique** — only commercial source of ricinoleic acid; gateway to C7, C9, C10, C11 monomers.
- **5-HMF is the "sleeping giant"** of bio-refineries — bridge between C6 sugars and FDCA (PET replacement).
- **Sorbitol → Isosorbide** is a 2-step dehydration — rigid bicyclic diol for bio-PC, PEs.
- **Epoxidized soybean oil (ESBO)** is the workhorse bio-plasticizer for PVC.
- **Bio-PE ≠ bio-degradable** — same chain as petroleum PE, only the carbon origin differs.
- **Three most-tested monomers in KTU:** Lactic acid, Sebacic acid, FDCA.
- **Lactic acid → PLA** must be written as a **two-step** process (lactide intermediate + ROP), not a single condensation.
- **Vanillin** is the only widely-available mono-aromatic bio-monomer from lignin.
- **Numerical conversions to memorize:**
  - 1 mol glucose = 180.16 g → 2 mol lactic acid = 180.16 g.
  - 1 mol ethanol = 46.07 g → 1 mol ethylene = 28.05 g (**60.89 %** atom economy, 39.11 % mass lost as H₂O).
  - Sebacic acid M = 202.25 g/mol; Azelaic acid M = 188.22 g/mol; Ricinoleic acid M = 298.46 g/mol.
- **Always state the polymer** along with the monomer in a Part-A answer.
- **Always write balanced equations** in Part-B; unbalanced reactions fetch 0 marks for the stoichiometry portion.
- **KTU 2024 scheme** emphasizes **numerical and industrial-relevance** questions; expect at least one **7-mark** calculation on carbon efficiency or atom economy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-Composites: Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT415 - ADVANCED BIOPOLYMERS & BIOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio-Composites: Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **bio-composite** is a multiphase, heterogeneous engineering material formed by combining two or more distinct constituent phases — at least one of which is derived from a **renewable biological origin (bio-based)** — such that the combined system exhibits superior physico-mechanical, thermal, or barrier properties compared to its individual constituents.

In the strict KTU 2024 syllabus context, a bio-composite is defined as:

> A composite material in which **either the matrix phase, the reinforcement phase, or both phases** are sourced from biological / renewable feedstock (agro-residues, lignocellulosic biomass, bio-polymers, or bio-minerals), held together by interfacial bonding to produce a structurally and functionally unified material system.

The three mandatory phases of a bio-composite are:

| Phase | Role | Typical Bio-Sourced Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Matrix (continuous phase)** | Binds, transfers load, protects reinforcement | PLA, PHB, Starch, Chitosan, Cellulose acetate |
| **Reinforcement (dispersed phase)** | Carries load, enhances stiffness/strength | Flax, Jute, Hemp, Sisal, Bamboo, Wood flour |
| **Interphase (third phase)** | Stress transfer bridge between matrix and fiber | Silane coupling, Maleic anhydride grafting |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mandate:** A material is classified as a true **bio-composite** only if it contains a *recognizable second phase* (fiber/particulate/filler) embedded within a continuous matrix. A pure bio-polymer without reinforcement does **NOT** qualify as a bio-composite.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete & Steel Rebar" Intuition

Think of a bio-composite like **mud-walls reinforced with straw** — a 4000-year-old technology used in ancient Egypt and still practised in rural Kerala housing.

- The **mud (matrix)** is soft, brittle, weak in tension — analogous to a bio-polymer like PLA or starch.
- The **straw (reinforcement)** is stiff, fibrous, strong along its length — analogous to flax or jute fibers.
- When straw is embedded into wet mud and dried, the resulting wall can resist cracking, bending, and impact far better than mud alone.

**Translating to modern bio-composites:**
- The "mud" is replaced by **bio-polymers** (bioplastics, natural resins).
- The "straw" is replaced by **natural fibers or lignocellulosic fillers**.
- Modern chemistry adds **coupling agents** to improve the "straw–mud grip" (interfacial adhesion).

This intuition immediately clarifies the **WHY** of bio-composites: a single biopolymer is rarely strong enough for structural applications, but when reinforced with natural fibers, it becomes a viable substitute for petroleum-based composites like fiberglass.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Density Reference Values (board-exam favourite):**
> - **Density of Natural Fibers** $\approx$ **1.25 – 1.50 g/cm³** (significantly *lower* than glass fiber $\approx$ 2.5 g/cm³)
> - **Density of Bio-Polymers (PLA)** $\approx$ **1.24 g/cm³**
> - **Specific Strength Ratio** $\left( \dfrac{\sigma_{ts}}{\rho} \right)$ of flax/kenaf **exceeds E-glass** — making bio-composites attractive for lightweight automotive and aerospace panels.

The **rule-of-mixtures (ROM)** boundary equation frequently cited in KTU derivations:

$$E_{c} = E_{m} V_{m} + E_{f} V_{f}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus and $V$ is the volume fraction; subscripts $c, m, f$ denote composite, matrix, and fiber respectively. This equation is formally derived in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Classification Matters — The Pedagogical Logic

Bio-composites are **not a single material** — they form an entire *family tree* with branches that differ dramatically in processing, end-use, biodegradability, and cost. Without a rigorous classification framework, an engineer cannot:

1. Select the right bio-composite for a target application (packaging vs. automotive vs. biomedical).
2. Predict its mechanical response under load.
3. Estimate its biodegradation kinetics in composting / soil / marine environments.
4. Comply with regulatory standards (e.g., **EN 13432**, **ASTM D6400**) for biodegradability claims.

## 2.2 Primary Classification Axes (KTU 2024 Mandate)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies bio-composites along **four orthogonal axes**. A complete answer must address all four.

### Axis 1 — Based on the Nature of the Matrix Phase

| Class | Matrix Origin | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Bio-Matrix Bio-Composites** | Fully bio-based polymer | PLA + Jute, PHB + Kenaf, Starch + Flax |
| **Synthetic-Matrix Bio-Composites** | Petrochemical polymer + bio-filler | PP + Wood flour, Epoxy + Hemp |
| **Hybrid-Matrix Bio-Composites** | Blend of bio + synthetic | PLA/PCL blends + Bamboo fiber |

### Axis 2 — Based on the Nature of the Reinforcement

| Class | Reinforcement Geometry | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Natural Fiber-Reinforced** | Long / short lignocellulosic fibers | Automotive door panels |
| **Particulate Bio-Composites** | Wood flour, rice husk, chitosan particles | Decking, flooring |
| **Wood-Plastic Composites (WPC)** | Wood fiber/flour + thermoplastic | Outdoor furniture |
| **Nano-Bio-Composites** | Cellulose nanocrystals (CNC), nanofibers (CNF) | Barrier films, packaging |
| **Bio-Mineral Composites** | Hydroxyapatite (HA) + collagen | Bone scaffolds (biomedical) |

### Axis 3 — Based on Biodegradability Profile

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most frequently asked axis** in KTU board examinations.

- **Fully Biodegradable Bio-Composites:** Both matrix and reinforcement are biodegradable → end-of-life via composting (e.g., PLA + jute).
- **Partially Biodegradable:** Only the reinforcement is bio-based; the synthetic matrix persists (e.g., PP + wood flour).
- **Non-Biodegradable Bio-Based Composites:** Both phases from biomass but engineered for durability (e.g., bio-based epoxy + natural fiber for aerospace).

### Axis 4 — Based on Structural Architecture

1. **Fiber-Reinforced Bio-Composites** — continuous or short fibers aligned in matrix.
2. **Laminated Bio-Composites** — stacked plies of bio-fiber mats bonded with bio-resin (analogous to plywood).
3. **Sandwich Bio-Composites** — low-density bio-core (balsa, cork, foam) between two bio-composite skins.
4. **Particulate / Filled Bio-Composites** — discrete particles dispersed in matrix.
5. **Hybrid Bio-Composites** — combination of two or more reinforcement types (e.g., flax + glass in PP matrix).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Master these six equations — they constitute ~70% of all numerical Part B sub-parts in KTU PEPOT415.

$$
E_{c} \,(\text{parallel ROM}) = E_{m} V_{m} + E_{f} V_{f}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{E_{c}} \,(\text{series ROM}) = \dfrac{V_{m}}{E_{m}} + \dfrac{V_{f}}{E_{f}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{c} = \sigma_{m} V_{m} + \sigma_{f} V_{f} \quad \text{(tensile strength, parallel loading)}
$$

$$
\rho_{c} = \rho_{m} V_{m} + \rho_{f} V_{f}
$$

$$
V_{m} + V_{f} = 1 \quad \text{(volume-fraction closure constraint)}
$$

$$
\text{Specific Strength} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ts}}{\rho_{c}}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E_{c}, E_{m}, E_{f}$ | Young's modulus of composite / matrix / fiber | GPa |
| $\sigma_{c}, \sigma_{m}, \sigma_{f}$ | Tensile strength of composite / matrix / fiber | MPa |
| $V_{m}, V_{f}$ | Volume fraction of matrix / fiber | dimensionless |
| $\rho_{c}, \rho_{m}, \rho_{f}$ | Density of composite / matrix / fiber | g/cm³ |
| $\sigma_{ts}$ | Ultimate tensile strength | MPa |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The parallel ROM assumes *isostrain* (both phases experience identical strain). The series ROM assumes *isostress* (identical stress in both phases). Real bio-composites lie *between* these bounds — examiners frequently award a half-mark deduction when students fail to state the underlying assumption.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification framework directly drives **material selection** in four major industries:

- **Automotive Sector (EU End-of-Life Vehicle Directive):** bio-composites in door panels, seat backs (e.g., **Mercedes-Benz A-Class** flax-PP composites reduce component weight by ~20%).
- **Packaging Industry:** PLA + nanocellulose films replace PET — addresses **single-use plastic ban (India, 2022)**.
- **Biomedical Implants:** HA-collagen bio-composites mimic bone extracellular matrix (Young's modulus of cortical bone $\approx$ 7–30 GPa — matched by tuning HA volume fraction).
- **Construction Sector:** Wood-Plastic Composites (WPC) for decking boards, window frames — combining durability with reduced maintenance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rule of Mixtures (Parallel / Isostrain Model)

**Objective:** Derive the upper-bound equation for the effective Young's modulus of a unidirectional fiber-reinforced bio-composite loaded along the fiber axis.

### Step 1 — Define the Geometric Model

Consider a composite bar of cross-sectional area $A_{c}$ composed of a matrix phase (area $A_{m}$) and a fiber phase (area $A_{f}$), with all fibers aligned parallel to the loading direction.

$$A_{c} = A_{m} + A_{f}$$

### Step 2 — Express Volume Fractions

The volume fractions are:

$$
V_{m} = \dfrac{A_{m}}{A_{c}} = \dfrac{\text{matrix volume}}{\text{composite volume}}
$$

$$
V_{f} = \dfrac{A_{f}}{A_{c}} = \dfrac{\text{fiber volume}}{\text{composite volume}}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Volume Conservation

The matrix and fiber together fully occupy the composite:

$$
V_{m} + V_{f} = 1
$$

### Step 4 — Isostrain Assumption (Parallel Loading)

When the load is applied along the fiber direction, both phases elongate by the *same* amount — perfect bonding prevents slip at the interface. Therefore:

$$
\varepsilon_{c} = \varepsilon_{m} = \varepsilon_{f} = \varepsilon
$$

### Step 5 — Express Internal Forces

The load is shared between the matrix and fiber, each carrying a fraction proportional to its axial stiffness:

$$
P_{c} = P_{m} + P_{f}
$$

### Step 6 — Substitute Force = Stress × Area

$$
\sigma_{c} A_{c} = \sigma_{m} A_{m} + \sigma_{f} A_{f}
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Hooke's Law to Each Phase

Using $\sigma = E \varepsilon$:

$$
E_{c} \varepsilon A_{c} = E_{m} \varepsilon A_{m} + E_{f} \varepsilon A_{f}
$$

### Step 8 — Cancel the Common Strain $\varepsilon$

$$
E_{c} A_{c} = E_{m} A_{m} + E_{f} A_{f}
$$

### Step 9 — Divide Throughout by $A_{c}$

$$
E_{c} = E_{m} \left( \dfrac{A_{m}}{A_{c}} \right) + E_{f} \left( \dfrac{A_{f}}{A_{c}} \right)
$$

### Step 10 — Final Closed-Form Expression

$$
\boxed{E_{c} = E_{m} V_{m} + E_{f} V_{f}}
$$

This is the **parallel (Voigt) model** — the upper-bound stiffness.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A PLA bio-composite is reinforced with 40 vol\% jute fiber. Given $E_{\text{PLA}} = 3.5$ GPa and $E_{\text{jute}} = 38$ GPa, compute the effective composite modulus using the parallel rule of mixtures.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Identify given values**

$$
V_{f} = 0.40, \quad V_{m} = 1 - 0.40 = 0.60
$$

$$
E_{m} = 3.5 \text{ GPa}, \quad E_{f} = 38 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the ROM equation**

$$
E_{c} = E_{m} V_{m} + E_{f} V_{f}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values**

$$
E_{c} = (3.5)(0.60) + (38)(0.40)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each term separately**

$$
(3.5)(0.60) = 2.10 \text{ GPa}
$$

$$
(38)(0.40) = 15.20 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the contributions**

$$
E_{c} = 2.10 + 15.20 = 17.30 \text{ GPa}
$$

### Final Answer

$$
\boxed{E_{c} = 17.30 \text{ GPa}}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The addition of 40 vol\% jute fiber increases the stiffness of neat PLA from 3.5 GPa to 17.3 GPa — a **~5× enhancement**. This demonstrates the dramatic reinforcing efficiency of lignocellulosic fibers in bio-composites.

## 3.3 Analytical Classification Matrix (Comprehensive)

The following consolidated matrix is the **definitive reference** for answering KTU Part B questions on this topic.

| Classification Axis | Sub-Class 1 | Sub-Class 2 | Sub-Class 3 | Sub-Class 4 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **By Matrix** | Bio-matrix (PLA, PHB) | Synthetic-matrix (PP, PE) | Hybrid-matrix (blends) | Bio-mineral matrix (HA) |
| **By Reinforcement** | Natural fibers (flax, jute) | Particulates (wood flour) | Nanofillers (CNC, CNF) | Bio-minerals (HA, TCP) |
| **By Biodegradability** | Fully biodegradable | Partially biodegradable | Non-biodegradable bio-based | Bio-resorbable |
| **By Structure** | Fiber-reinforced | Laminated | Sandwich | Particulate / Hybrid |
| **By Application** | Packaging | Automotive | Biomedical | Construction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** A 7-mark sub-part (e.g., "Classify bio-composites with examples") should cover **at least three of the above axes** with **two examples per axis** to fetch full marks. A single-axis answer typically scores ≤ 4/7.

## 3.4 Density Rule of Mixtures — Validation Example

$$
\rho_{c} = \rho_{m} V_{m} + \rho_{f} V_{f}
$$

Given $\rho_{\text{PLA}} = 1.24$ g/cm³ and $\rho_{\text{jute}} = 1.46$ g/cm³ with $V_{f} = 0.30$:

$$
\rho_{c} = (1.24)(0.70) + (1.46)(0.30)
$$

$$
\rho_{c} = 0.868 + 0.438 = 1.306 \text{ g/cm}^{3}
$$

This is **~50% lower than glass-fiber composites** ($\approx$ 2.0 g/cm³) — confirming the lightweight advantage central to bio-composite value proposition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**Define a bio-composite. List any four natural fibers used as reinforcement in bio-composites.**

**Model Answer:**

A bio-composite is a multi-phase engineering material in which **at least one of the matrix or reinforcement phases is derived from renewable biological sources**, combined to yield superior mechanical, thermal, or barrier properties over the individual constituents.

Four natural fibers used as reinforcement are:
1. **Jute** — high cellulose content (~62–64%), low density, used in packaging and automotive panels.
2. **Flax** — high specific stiffness, used in Mercedes-Benz door liners.
3. **Hemp** — bast fiber with good moisture resistance.
4. **Sisal** — leaf fiber with high tensile strength, common in Indian sub-continental composites.

*(Any four with one-line application = full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

**Distinguish between a "bio-based" composite and a "biodegradable" composite with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Bio-Based Composite | Biodegradable Composite |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | At least one component is sourced from biomass | Material can be decomposed by microorganisms |
| Biodegradability requirement | Not mandatory | Mandatory |
| Example | Bio-PET + natural fiber | PLA + jute fiber |
| End-of-life | May persist in environment | Composts within ~180 days |

**Key Insight:** A material can be **bio-based but non-biodegradable** (e.g., bio-PE behaves identically to fossil PE). Conversely, some petrochemical polymers (e.g., PCL) are biodegradable but not bio-based.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 Pattern]**

**(a)** Classify bio-composites based on (i) matrix type, (ii) reinforcement type, and (iii) biodegradability. Give **two examples** for each sub-class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bio-composite is made by reinforcing a **PLA matrix** ($E_{m} = 3.5$ GPa, $\rho_{m} = 1.24$ g/cm³) with **30 vol\% flax fiber** ($E_{f} = 45$ GPa, $\rho_{f} = 1.50$ g/cm³). Calculate the **effective Young's modulus** and **density** of the composite using the rule of mixtures. Comment on the lightweight advantage compared to a glass-fiber composite of similar stiffness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Classification:**

**(i) Based on Matrix Type:**
1. **Bio-matrix bio-composite** — PLA + jute, PHB + kenaf (both matrix and reinforcement bio-based).
2. **Synthetic-matrix bio-composite** — PP + wood flour, epoxy + hemp (only reinforcement is bio-based).
3. **Hybrid-matrix bio-composite** — PLA/PCL blend + bamboo fiber.

**(ii) Based on Reinforcement Type:**
1. **Fiber-reinforced** — short jute fiber in starch matrix; continuous flax in epoxy.
2. **Particulate bio-composite** — wood flour in PP; rice husk in PLA.
3. **Nano-bio-composite** — cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) in PLA; nanofibrillated cellulose (NFC) films.

**(iii) Based on Biodegradability:**
1. **Fully biodegradable** — PLA + jute, PHB + sisal (both phases compost).
2. **Partially biodegradable** — PP + wood flour (only reinforcement biodegrades).
3. **Non-biodegradable bio-based** — bio-based epoxy + natural fiber for durable aerospace panels.

*(Full marks require all three sub-classes per axis with two examples each.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- $V_{f} = 0.30 \Rightarrow V_{m} = 0.70$
- $E_{m} = 3.5$ GPa, $E_{f} = 45$ GPa
- $\rho_{m} = 1.24$ g/cm³, $\rho_{f} = 1.50$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Compute $E_{c}$ using parallel ROM:**

$$
E_{c} = E_{m} V_{m} + E_{f} V_{f}
$$

$$
E_{c} = (3.5)(0.70) + (45)(0.30)
$$

$$
E_{c} = 2.45 + 13.50 = 15.95 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\rho_{c}$ using density ROM:**

$$
\rho_{c} = \rho_{m} V_{m} + \rho_{f} V_{f}
$$

$$
\rho_{c} = (1.24)(0.70) + (1.50)(0.30)
$$

$$
\rho_{c} = 0.868 + 0.450 = 1.318 \text{ g/cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute specific stiffness for comparison:**

$$
\text{Specific Stiffness} = \dfrac{E_{c}}{\rho_{c}} = \dfrac{15.95}{1.318} = 12.10 \text{ GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

For a glass-fiber composite with similar $E_{c} \approx 16$ GPa and $\rho \approx 1.90$ g/cm³:

$$
\text{Specific Stiffness}_{\text{glass}} = \dfrac{16}{1.90} = 8.42 \text{ GPa} \cdot \text{cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

**Conclusion:** The PLA-flax bio-composite offers a **~44% higher specific stiffness** than its glass-fiber counterpart while being fully bio-based and biodegradable — confirming the lightweight advantage of bio-composites for transport applications.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4 Alternative Pattern]**

**(a)** With the help of a **schematic block diagram**, explain the **three-phase structural composition** of a fiber-reinforced bio-composite (matrix, reinforcement, and interphase). Describe the role of the interphase and the function of **silane coupling agents**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wood-plastic composite (WPC) is manufactured from **high-density polyethylene (HDPE)** matrix reinforced with **40 wt\% wood flour**. The density of HDPE is 0.95 g/cm³ and that of wood flour is 1.50 g/cm³. Compute the **density of the composite** using the rule of mixtures (assume volume additivity). Comment on whether this composite is **biodegradable**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Three-Phase Structural Composition:**

A fiber-reinforced bio-composite consists of three functionally distinct phases:

1. **Matrix Phase (Continuous Phase):**
   - Function: binds the reinforcement, transfers applied load via shear at the interface, protects fibers from environmental degradation, and maintains fiber geometry.
   - Bio-example: PLA, PHB, starch, chitosan, cellulose acetate.

2. **Reinforcement Phase (Dispersed Phase):**
   - Function: primary load-bearing constituent; enhances stiffness, strength, and dimensional stability.
   - Bio-example: jute, flax, hemp, sisal, kenaf, bamboo.

3. **Interphase (Third Phase):**
   - Function: the *transition zone* at the matrix-fiber boundary where stress is transferred via shear; its quality governs the composite's overall mechanical performance.
   - Coupling agent role: **silane coupling agents** (e.g., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, γ-MPS) form covalent Si–O–C bridges between the hydroxyl groups of lignocellulosic fibers and the functional groups of the matrix. This dramatically improves **interfacial shear strength** and reduces moisture absorption — both critical for outdoor durability.

*(Drawing the block diagram with arrows showing load transfer from matrix → interphase → fiber earns full visual-mark credit.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (WPC Density):**

Given:
- Weight fraction of wood flour: $w_{f} = 0.40$, weight fraction of HDPE: $w_{m} = 0.60$
- $\rho_{m} = 0.95$ g/cm³, $\rho_{f} = 1.50$ g/cm³

**Step 1 — Convert weight fractions to volume fractions:**

$$
V_{f} = \dfrac{w_{f}/\rho_{f}}{w_{f}/\rho_{f} + w_{m}/\rho_{m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute specific volumes:**

$$
\dfrac{w_{f}}{\rho_{f}} = \dfrac{0.40}{1.50} = 0.2667 \text{ cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

$$
\dfrac{w_{m}}{\rho_{m}} = \dfrac{0.60}{0.95} = 0.6316 \text{ cm}^{3}\text{/g}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute fiber volume fraction:**

$$
V_{f} = \dfrac{0.2667}{0.2667 + 0.6316} = \dfrac{0.2667}{0.8983} = 0.2969
$$

$$
V_{m} = 1 - 0.2969 = 0.7031
$$

**Step 4 — Apply density rule of mixtures:**

$$
\rho_{c} = \rho_{m} V_{m} + \rho_{f} V_{f}
$$

$$
\rho_{c} = (0.95)(0.7031) + (1.50)(0.2969)
$$

$$
\rho_{c} = 0.668 + 0.445 = 1.113 \text{ g/cm}^{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on Biodegradability:**

The HDPE + wood flour WPC is **only partially biodegradable**. The wood-flour reinforcement is biodegradable, but the HDPE matrix is a **non-degradable petrochemical polymer**. Hence, only the bio-phase degrades; the HDPE skeleton persists for decades, fragmenting into microplastics. This composite is therefore **NOT** considered biodegradable under EN 13432 / ASTM D6400 standards.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in PEPOT415 Module 4:**
> 1. **Missing the "both-phases" distinction (–2 marks):** A bio-composite is *not* the same as a bio-polymer. Always specify the matrix AND reinforcement phases explicitly.
> 2. **Conflating bio-based with biodegradable (–2 marks):** Bio-PET is bio-based but not biodegradable. PCL is biodegradable but not bio-based.
> 3. **Omitting volume-fraction closure $V_{m} + V_{f} = 1$ in numerical problems (–1 mark):** Examiners deduct when the student assumes arbitrary $V_{m}, V_{f}$ without the closure constraint.
> 4. **Failing to state the isostrain / isostress assumption in ROM derivations (–1 mark):** Always declare the underlying assumption before applying the formula.
> 5. **Wrong unit conversions in density ROM problems (–1 mark):** Mixing g/cm³ with kg/m³ without conversion is a recurring error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - A bio-composite **must have a recognizable second phase** (reinforcement/filler) — pure bio-polymers do NOT qualify.
> - The **three mandatory phases** are: **Matrix + Reinforcement + Interphase**.
> - The **four classification axes** (KTU 2024 mandate) are: **Matrix | Reinforcement | Biodegradability | Structure**.
> - The **parallel (Voigt) ROM** $E_{c} = E_{m}V_{m} + E_{f}V_{f}$ applies when fibers are aligned with the load (isostrain).
> - The **series (Reuss) ROM** $\dfrac{1}{E_{c}} = \dfrac{V_{m}}{E_{m}} + \dfrac{V_{f}}{E_{f}}$ applies for transverse loading (isostress).
> - **Volume-fraction closure:** $V_{m} + V_{f} = 1$ — non-negotiable.
> - **Density ROM** mirrors the modulus ROM: $\rho_{c} = \rho_{m}V_{m} + \rho_{f}V_{f}$.
> - **Natural fibers have lower density** than glass fiber → bio-composites offer **higher specific strength and stiffness** (key automotive/aerospace selling point).
> - **Bio-based $\neq$ biodegradable** — these are independent axes of classification.
> - **Silane coupling agents** form covalent Si–O–C bridges at the bio-fiber/matrix interphase, dramatically improving interfacial adhesion.
> - **Fully biodegradable bio-composites:** PLA + jute, PHB + kenaf.
> - **Partially biodegradable (WPCs):** PP/HDPE + wood flour — matrix persists, only reinforcement degrades.
> - **Standard reference densities (memorize):** PLA $\approx$ 1.24 g/cm³; Jute $\approx$ 1.46 g/cm³; Flax $\approx$ 1.50 g/cm³; E-glass $\approx$ 2.50 g/cm³.
> - **Key regulatory standards:** **EN 13432** (European biodegradability), **ASTM D6400** (US compostability), **ISO 17088** (bio-based labeling).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-Based Polymers and Resins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT415 - ADVANCED BIOPOLYMERS & BIOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio-Based Polymers and Resins

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bio-Based Polymers — Formal Definition

A **bio-based polymer** is a macromolecular material (natural or synthetic) in which at least one of the constituent monomer units, the polymer backbone, or the recurring structural fragment is wholly or partially derived from **renewable biological feedstocks** such as agricultural crops (starch, sugarcane, corn), forestry products (cellulose, lignin), marine resources (chitin, alginate), or microbiological fermentation products (polyhydroxyalkanoates, lactic acid).

According to the **European Standard EN 16575 (2014)**, a polymer is classified as *bio-based* if the carbon content originates from renewable raw materials — quantified by the **bio-based carbon content (BCC, %)** using the ASTM D6866 radiocarbon method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bio-based ≠ Biodegradable.** A polymer can be bio-based yet non-degradable (e.g., **bio-polyethylene** from sugarcane ethanol), and a polymer can be biodegradable yet fossil-derived (e.g., **polycaprolactone (PCL)** from petrochemical ε-caprolactone). These are *orthogonal properties* that must never be confused in KTU examinations.

## 1.2 Bio-Based Resins — Formal Definition

A **bio-based resin** is a low-to-medium molecular weight **oligomeric/polymeric precursor** (typically viscous liquid or solid) derived from renewable biomass that can be **crosslinked, cured, or further polymerised** to form a rigid 3-D thermoset network. Bio-resins include:
- **Epoxy resins** from epoxidized plant oils (e.g., epoxidized soybean oil — ESO).
- **Phenolic resins** from lignin-derived phenols.
- **Polyurethane resins** from bio-polyols (castor oil, glycerol).
- **Unsaturated polyester resins (UPR)** from bio-based acids (succinic, itaconic, fumaric).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Plant → Plastic" Analogy.** Imagine your kitchen has leftover sugarcane stalks and corn cobs. Chemically, these are bundles of **sugars (C₆H₁₂O₆)**. A petroleum refinery breaks crude oil into ethylene (C₂H₄) monomers to make plastic. Similarly, a **biorefinery** breaks sugarcane sugar into ethylene — *the exact same molecule!* — and polymerises it into **bio-polyethylene**. The plastic in your hand is chemically indistinguishable from oil-based PE, but the carbon atoms trace back to last summer's sunshine rather than ancient fossils. **Bio-resins** are like the "uncured glue" version — sticky plant-derived liquids that chemists harden into structural composites for wind-turbine blades or aircraft interiors.

## 1.4 Carbon Footprint Distinction

| Metric | Definition | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|
| **BCC** | Bio-Based Carbon Content | 0 – 100 % |
| **GWP** | Global Warming Potential (kg CO₂-eq / kg resin) | Bio: **1.5 – 3.0** vs. Petro: **3.5 – 8.0** |
| **Renewable Feedstock Index** | Mass of renewable feedstock ÷ Total mass input | 0 – 1 |
| **Cradle-to-Gate Energy** | Cumulative energy demand (MJ/kg) | Bio-resins: **30 – 90** vs. Petro: **70 – 110** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **ASTM D6866** method (radiocarbon dating of polymers using ¹⁴C/¹²C isotope ratios) since biomass carbon is "modern" (¹⁴C present), while fossil carbon is "ancient" (¹⁴C decayed). Always mention this technique in 14-mark answers involving bio-based quantification.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification of Bio-Based Polymers

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats bio-based polymers under **three overlapping classification axes**:

### Axis A — By Origin
1. **Natural (Bio-derived) Polymers** — Extracted directly from biomass with minimal chemical modification.
   - **Polysaccharides:** Starch (amylose + amylopectin), cellulose, chitosan, alginate, dextran.
   - **Proteins:** Collagen, gelatin, silk fibroin, casein, soy protein, zein.
   - **Polyisoprenoids:** Natural rubber (cis-1,4-polyisoprene), gutta-percha (trans isomer).
   - **Polyesters (microbial):** Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) — PHB, PHBV, PHBHHx.

2. **Synthetic Bio-Based Polymers** — Monomers derived from biomass, but polymerised via industrial routes.
   - **PLA** (Polylactic Acid) — from lactic acid fermentation of corn/sugarcane.
   - **Bio-PE** (Bio-polyethylene) — from bio-ethylene (sugarcane ethanol dehydration).
   - **Bio-PET** — partially bio-based (bio-MEG + bio-PTA).
   - **Bio-PA** (Bio-polyamides) — e.g., PA-11 from castor oil (Rilsan®).
   - **Bio-PTT** (Polytrimethylene terephthalate) — bio-PDO route.

3. **Mixed / Hybrid Bio-Based Polymers** — Blends or copolymers containing both renewable and fossil carbon.

### Axis B — By Synthesis Route
| Route | Reaction Type | Example Polymer |
|---|---|---|
| **ROP** (Ring-Opening Polymerisation) | Cationic / anionic / coordination | PLA, PHA |
| **Polycondensation** | Step-growth (esterification / amidation) | Bio-PET, Bio-PA |
| **Radical Polymerisation** | Chain-growth free radical | Bio-acrylics |
| **Metathesis (ROMP)** | Olefin metathesis | Polynorbornene from veg-oil |

### Axis C — By Degradability
- **Biodegradable:** Starch, PLA, PHA, chitosan.
- **Non-biodegradable (but bio-based):** Bio-PE, Bio-PET, Bio-PA.

## 2.2 Bio-Based Resins — Chemistry of the Four Major Families

### 2.2.1 Epoxy Resins from Plant Oils
**Epoxidized Soybean Oil (ESO)** is synthesised via the **Prilezhaev reaction**:
$$\text{C=C (linoleic/linolenic)} + \text{Performic acid (HCOOH/H}_2\text{O}_2\text{)} \longrightarrow \text{Epoxide (oxirane ring)} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

ESO contains ~ **4.0 – 4.5 epoxide groups per triglyceride molecule**. Curing is achieved with anhydrides (e.g., maleic anhydride), amines (DETA, TETA), or latent catalysts (dicyandiamide).

### 2.2.2 Phenolic Resins from Lignin
Lignin (a phenolic copolymer of **coniferyl, sinapyl, and p-coumaryl alcohols**) is depolymerised to yield phenol-rich monomer streams. These undergo:
- **Novolac route** (acid-catalysed, phenol-excess → linear, requires HMTA hardener).
- **Resole route** (base-catalysed, formaldehyde-excess → branched, self-curing).

### 2.2.3 Polyurethane Resins from Bio-Polyols
Castor oil (ricinoleic acid content ≈ **85 – 95 %**) provides a tri-functional hydroxyl source:
$$\text{Castor-OH} + \text{Diisocyanate (MDI / TDI)} \longrightarrow \text{Polyurethane (carbamate linkage)}$$

### 2.2.4 Unsaturated Polyester Resins (UPR) from Bio-Acids
Succinic / fumaric / itaconic acid (fermentation-derived) replace maleic anhydride:
$$\text{Di-acid (bio)} + \text{Glycol} \xrightarrow{-\text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{Polyester (linear, unsaturated)} \xrightarrow{\text{Styrene}} \text{Crosslinked UPR}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Equation / Relation | Formula | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Bio-based carbon content | $\text{BCC (\%)} = \dfrac{^{14}\text{C sample}}{^{14}\text{C reference}} \times 100$ | ASTM D6866 quantification |
| Number-average MW (step-growth) | $\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \dfrac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}$ | Carothers' equation for polycondensation |
| Carothers' extent of reaction | $p = \dfrac{N_0 - N}{N_0}$ | Conversion of functional groups |
| Flory gel point | $p_c = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f(f-1)}}$ (functionality $f$) | Predict network formation |
| PLA hydrolysis rate | $r = k \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}]^n$ | Biodegradation kinetics |
| Epoxide equivalent weight | $\text{EEW} = \dfrac{1000}{\text{epoxide value}}$ | Resin-stoichiometry for curing |
| OH number for bio-polyols | $\text{OH\#} = \dfrac{56.1 \cdot V \cdot N}{m}$ (mg KOH/g) | Polyurethane formulation |
| Repeat-unit MW (PLA) | $M_0(\text{PLA}) = 72 \text{ g/mol}$ (C₃H₄O₂) | DP calculations |
| ROP equilibrium | $\Delta G_{ROP} = \Delta H_{ROP} - T\Delta S_{ROP}$ | Lactide polymerisation feasibility |
| Degree of substitution (starch) | $\text{DS} = \dfrac{162 \cdot \% \text{substituent}}{M_{sub} - M_{H} \cdot \%\text{substituent}}$ | Modified starch characterisation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **Carothers' equation** above, $r$ is the stoichiometric imbalance ratio ($N_A/N_B$, ≤ 1) and $p$ is the extent of reaction. This equation is **favourite territory** for 14-mark derivations in KTU ESE.

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Wind-turbine blades:** Bio-epoxy / glass-fibre composites (Siemens Gamesa, Vestas).
- **Automotive interior panels:** Natural fibre + bio-PP / bio-PLA composites (BMW i3, Toyota).
- **Packaging:** PLA trays, PHA films, cellulose acetate foils.
- **Construction:** Bio-phenolic bonded particle boards (replacing urea-formaldehyde).
- **Medical implants:** Bio-PA-11 (Rilsan®) catheters, PLA sutures.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Carothers' Equation for Step-Growth Bio-Polymerisation

Consider a polycondensation between bio-derived monomers: e.g., **bio-lactic acid → PLA prepolymer**, or **bio-succinic acid + bio-1,3-propanediol → bio-PTT prepolymer**.

Let:
- $N_0$ = initial total number of monomer molecules (= functional groups / 2 for difunctional).
- $N$ = number of molecules remaining at time $t$.
- $p$ = extent of reaction (fraction of functional groups reacted).

**Step 1.** Total initial functional groups = $2N_0$ (for difunctional monomers).

**Step 2.** Number of functional groups reacted = $2N_0 p$.
Equivalently, number of bonds formed = $N_0 p$.

**Step 3.** Each bond formation consumes 2 molecules into 1:
$$
N = N_0 - N_0 p
$$

**Step 4.** Solving for $p$:
$$
p = \frac{N_0 - N}{N_0}
$$

**Step 5.** Number-average degree of polymerisation:
$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{N_0}{N} = \frac{N_0}{N_0 - N_0 p} = \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

**Step 6.** Number-average molecular weight:
$$
\bar{M}_n = M_0 \cdot \bar{X}_n = \frac{M_0}{1-p}
$$

**Step 7.** For **non-stoichiometric** systems (bio-diacid + bio-diol with stoichiometric imbalance ratio $r = N_A/N_B \le 1$):
$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1+r}{1+r-2rp}
$$

This is the **full Carothers' equation** examiners will demand you derive.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Flory's Statistical Gel Point for Bio-Resins

For a bio-resin with average functionality $\bar{f}$, branching coefficient $\alpha$, and assuming random network formation:

**Step 1.** Probability a randomly chosen functional group leads to an infinite chain:
$$
P(\text{infinite}) = 1 \quad \text{when} \quad \alpha = 1
$$

**Step 2.** For an $A_f$ monomer crosslinking with $A_2$:
$$
\alpha = p^2 \cdot \frac{(f-1)}{f}
$$

**Step 3.** At the **gel point** $\alpha_c = \dfrac{1}{(f-1)}$:
$$
p_c^2 \cdot \frac{(f-1)}{f} = \frac{1}{(f-1)}
$$

**Step 4.** Solving for $p_c$:
$$
p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{f(f-1)}}
$$

**Step 5.** Numerical example — castor oil (trifunctional, $\bar{f}=3$):
$$
p_c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3 \cdot 2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6}} \approx 0.408
$$

Thus, ~ **40.8 %** of OH groups must react before castor-oil-based polyurethane gels.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Bio-Based Carbon Content from ¹⁴C Measurement

**Step 1.** The radiocarbon ratio of a 100 % bio-based (modern) sample equals the **oxalic acid reference standard** (1950 AD baseline, corrected for decay).

**Step 2.** The bio-based carbon content:
$$
\text{BCC (\%)} = \frac{^{14}\text{C}/^{12}\text{C}_{\text{sample}}}{^{14}\text{C}/^{12}\text{C}_{\text{reference (modern)}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 3.** A sample showing 60 % of modern ¹⁴C activity is **60 % bio-based** and **40 % fossil-derived**.

**Step 4.** Partial-bio PET calculation:
- Component A (bio-MEG, 100 % bio-based): 30 wt%.
- Component B (fossil-PTA, 0 % bio-based): 70 wt%.
- Overall BCC = $0.30 \times 100 + 0.70 \times 0 = 30\,\%$.

This **mass-weighted rule** is the standard for multi-component systems.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Polymerisation Routes for Top Bio-Polymers

| Bio-Polymer | Monomer Source | Polymerisation Route | Catalyst / Conditions | $M_w$ Range (g/mol) | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLA** | Lactic acid (fermented corn sugar) | ROP of lactide (cyclic dimer) | Sn(Oct)₂, 180 °C | 50 000 – 250 000 | 55 – 60 | 150 – 170 |
| **PHB** | 3-HB (microbial fermentation) | ROP of β-butyrolactone | Zn/Mg catalysts | 100 000 – 500 000 | 4 – 10 | 170 – 180 |
| **Bio-PE** | Bio-ethylene (sugarcane ethanol) | Coordination (Ziegler-Natta / metallocene) | TiCl₄/MgCl₂, 100 °C, 1000 bar | 50 000 – 300 000 | − 110 | 115 – 135 |
| **Bio-PA-11** | 11-aminoundecanoic acid (castor oil) | Condensation (self) | 215 °C, water removal | 20 000 – 50 000 | 46 | 190 |
| **Bio-PET** | Bio-MEG + fossil-PTA | Esterification + polycondensation | Sb₂O₃, 280 °C | 30 000 – 80 000 | 75 – 85 | 250 – 260 |
| **Bio-Epoxy (ESO)** | Epoxidized soybean oil | Step-growth cure with anhydride | DMP-30, 150 °C | Network (crosslinked) | 80 – 120 | N/A (thermoset) |

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Q:** A bio-resin batch contains 3.50 kg of epoxidized soybean oil (EEW = **250 g/eq**). How much **phthalic anhydride** (Eq. wt. = **74 g/eq**) is required for stoichiometric cure?

**Step 1.** Equivalents of epoxy groups:
$$
\text{eq}_{epoxy} = \frac{m_{ESO}}{\text{EEW}} = \frac{3500\text{ g}}{250} = 14.0 \text{ eq}
$$

**Step 2.** Each anhydride reacts with 2 epoxide groups → anhydride equivalents needed:
$$
\text{eq}_{anhydride} = \frac{14.0}{2} = 7.0 \text{ eq}
$$

**Step 3.** Mass of phthalic anhydride:
$$
m_{PA} = 7.0 \text{ eq} \times 74 \text{ g/eq} = 518 \text{ g}
$$

**Answer:** **518 g** of phthalic anhydride.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always state the **anhydride-to-epoxy stoichiometric factor = 1/2** in KTU answers; this is the most-skipped step causing **2–3 mark losses** per KTU valuation patterns.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Distinguish clearly between a *bio-based polymer* and a *biodegradable polymer*. Give one example for each category where the two properties are *not* overlapping.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bio-based polymer** is one in which the carbon atoms of the polymer chain originate wholly or partly from **renewable biological feedstock** (ASTM D6866 method). A **biodegradable polymer** is one capable of undergoing microbial decomposition into CO₂, H₂O, and biomass under specified conditions (ISO 17088 / EN 13432).

The two properties are **orthogonal**:
- **Bio-based but non-biodegradable:** *Bio-polyethylene* (sugarcane ethanol → bio-ethylene → PE). Has renewable carbon but is identical in crystallinity and non-degradability to fossil PE.
- **Biodegradable but not bio-based:** *Polycaprolactone (PCL)*, synthesised from petrochemical ε-caprolactone via ROP. Degrades by hydrolysis but its carbon is fossil.

Hence, bio-basedness refers to **origin of carbon**, while biodegradability refers to **end-of-life behaviour**.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is *epoxidized soybean oil (ESO)*? Write the general reaction by which it is produced.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

ESO is a **bio-based epoxy resin precursor** obtained by inserting oxygen across the **C=C double bonds** of unsaturated fatty-acid chains (oleic, linoleic, linolenic) present in soybean oil triglycerides.

It is industrially produced by the **Prilezhaev epoxidation** using **performic acid** generated *in situ* from formic acid and hydrogen peroxide:

$$\text{R-CH=CH-R'} + \text{HCOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{R-CH(O)CH-R'} + \text{HCOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The product contains approximately **4.0 – 4.5 oxirane rings per triglyceride molecule** (epoxide value ~ 6.5 – 7.0 %). ESO is then cured with anhydrides or amines to yield a crosslinked bio-based thermoset.

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing bio-based with biodegradable** → -2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the *in situ* formic acid step** in Prilezhaev reaction → -1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the stoichiometric factor of 1/2 for anhydride-cured epoxies** → -2 marks.
> 4. **Skipping the ASTM D6866 ¹⁴C method** when defining bio-based carbon content → -1 mark.
> 5. **Drawing PLA structure as poly(ethylene oxide) or poly(acrylic acid)** → -3 marks (structural drawing is mandatory).
> 6. **Stating lactic acid fermentation temperature without catalyst/conditions** → -1 mark.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss in detail the **classification of bio-based polymers** with at least **two examples under each class**. Tabulate their key thermal and mechanical properties.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* With a neat reaction scheme, explain the **synthesis of PLA via the ring-opening polymerisation (ROP) of lactide**. Compute the **number-average molecular weight** for PLA prepared at $p = 0.985$, given $M_0 = 72$ g/mol.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Classification (7 marks):**

| Class | Sub-Class | Examples | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Modulus (GPa) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Polysaccharide** | Starch | Native corn starch | — | 260 (decomp.) | 30 – 50 | 0.5 – 1.5 |
| **Natural Polysaccharide** | Cellulose | Cotton linters | — | — | 50 – 200 | 5 – 15 |
| **Natural Protein** | Soy protein | SPI films | 100 | — | 5 – 40 | 0.1 – 1.0 |
| **Natural Polyisoprene** | Natural rubber | NR latex | − 70 | 36 | 20 – 30 | 0.001 – 0.01 |
| **Microbial Polyester** | PHB | Biomer® P240 | 4 | 175 | 25 – 40 | 1.5 – 3.5 |
| **Synthetic Bio-Based** | PLA | Ingeo™ 4043D | 58 | 165 | 50 – 70 | 3.0 – 4.0 |
| **Synthetic Bio-Based** | Bio-PE | Braskem I'm green™ | − 110 | 125 | 20 – 30 | 0.6 – 1.2 |
| **Synthetic Bio-Based** | Bio-PA-11 | Rilsan® BESNO | 46 | 190 | 40 – 55 | 1.0 – 1.5 |

**Synthesis pathway note (1 mark):**
- PLA: Corn starch → glucose → lactic acid (fermentation) → lactide → ROP → PLA.
- Bio-PE: Sugarcane juice → ethanol → dehydration → bio-ethylene → coordination polymerisation → bio-PE.

**(b) PLA Synthesis via ROP of Lactide (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Monomer formation:** Lactic acid from corn/sugarcane fermentation is condensed into the cyclic dimer **lactide** (3,6-dimethyl-1,4-dioxane-2,5-dione).

**Step 2 — ROP Reaction:**

$$\text{Lactide} \xrightarrow{\text{Sn(Oct)}_2,\ 180\,°C} \text{Poly(L-lactic acid)}$$

The mechanism is **coordination-insertion**:

1. Sn(Oct)₂ activates the carbonyl oxygen of lactide.
2. A hydroxyl initiator (ROH, e.g., 1-octanol) attacks the carbonyl carbon.
3. The acyl-oxygen bond cleaves, propagating chain growth.

**Step 3 — Mathematical calculation:**

Using Carothers' equation:
$$
\bar{M}_n = \frac{M_0}{1-p} = \frac{72 \text{ g/mol}}{1 - 0.985} = \frac{72}{0.015}
$$

$$
\bar{M}_n = 4800 \text{ g/mol}
$$

The number-average degree of polymerisation:
$$
\bar{X}_n = \frac{1}{1-p} = \frac{1}{0.015} \approx 66.7
$$

Thus, ~ **67 lactide repeat units** per PLA chain at this conversion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **synthesis, curing, and applications of bio-based epoxy resins from epoxidized soybean oil (ESO)**. Discuss the Prilezhaev reaction in detail.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A bio-resin formulation requires **5.00 kg of ESO** (EEW = 250 g/eq) cured with **hexahydrophthalic anhydride (HHPA)** (Eq. wt. = 154 g/eq). Compute the **mass of HHPA** required for stoichiometric cure. List three industrial applications of ESO-based epoxy composites.

#### Model Solution

**(a) ESO Synthesis and Curing (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Prilezhaev Epoxidation:**

Soybean oil (~ 80 % unsaturated fatty acids, predominantly linoleic C18:2 and linolenic C18:3) is reacted with **performic acid** generated *in situ*:

$$\text{HCOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{HCOOOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{R-CH=CH-R'} + \text{HCOOOH} \longrightarrow \text{R-CH(O)CH-R'} + \text{HCOOH}$$

The triglyceride retains its **3 ester linkages**, and the C=C bonds become **oxirane (epoxide) rings**, giving **EEW ≈ 230 – 250 g/eq** and **iodine value < 5**.

**Step 2 — Curing Mechanism with Anhydride:**

Anhydride curing proceeds via:
1. Initiation: Alcohol impurity (or added accelerator like DMP-30, 2,4,6-tri(dimethylaminomethyl)phenol) opens the anhydride to a half-ester.
2. The half-ester carboxylate attacks an epoxide ring → ether-ester linkage + new alkoxide.
3. The alkoxide opens another anhydride → propagation.
4. The network builds up until gelation at $p_c \approx 0.33$ for $f=3$.

**Step 3 — Properties of cured ESO/HHPA:**
- $T_g$: 80 – 120 °C.
- Tensile strength: 30 – 60 MPa.
- Elongation at break: 4 – 12 %.
- Bio-based carbon content: ~ **75 – 85 %**.

**Step 4 — Applications (any three):**
1. Wind-turbine blade matrix (replacing diglycidyl ether of bisphenol-A — DGEBA).
2. Automotive structural composites (BMW i-series interior panels).
3. Eco-friendly coatings, adhesives, and potting compounds.
4. Sustainable printed circuit board (PCB) laminates.
5. Bio-based rigid foams for thermal insulation.

**(b) Stoichiometry Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Equivalents of epoxy groups:**
$$
\text{eq}_{epoxy} = \frac{m_{ESO}}{\text{EEW}} = \frac{5000\text{ g}}{250\text{ g/eq}} = 20.0 \text{ eq}
$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometric factor for anhydride:**
Each anhydride functional group reacts with **one** epoxide group (1:1 stoichiometry), but one HHPA molecule contains **one** anhydride functional unit. Hence:

$$
\text{eq}_{HHPA} = \text{eq}_{epoxy} = 20.0 \text{ eq}
$$

**Step 3 — Mass of HHPA required:**
$$
m_{HHPA} = \text{eq}_{HHPA} \times \text{Eq.Wt.} = 20.0 \times 154 = 3080 \text{ g} = 3.08 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Industrial applications of ESO-based composites (any three, 1 mark each):**
1. **Wind-turbine blade shells** (Siemens Gamesa SG-8.0 offshore platform).
2. **Automotive interior door panels and dashboards** (Ford, Toyota bio-content).
3. **High-pressure composite pipes** for oil & gas (oil & bio-resin hybrid).
4. **Recyclable structural composites** for construction (rebar alternatives).
5. **Aerospace secondary structures** (aircraft cabin interiors, Airbus ECO).

> [!WARNING]
