Similarly, in NFSPCs, synthetic polymers are usually ductile but not very stiff, and natural fibres — being rich in **cellulose microfibrils** aligned along the fibre axis — provide high specific stiffness and strength in tension. ## 1.3 Classification of Natural Fibres Used in NFSPCs Natural fibres are broadly classified by their origin, and each class carries different implications for composite performance: | Class | Examples | Key Chemical Constituents | Typical Density (g/cm³) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Bast (Stem) Fibres** | Jute, Flax, Hemp, Kenaf, Ramie | Cellulose (60–80%), Hemicellulose, Lignin | **1.3 – 1.5** | | **Leaf Fibres** | Sisal, Abaca, Banana, Pineapple (PALF) | Cellulose (50–70%), Lignin | **1.2 – 1.5** | | **Seed/Fruit Fibres** | Cotton, Coir, Kapok | Cellulose-rich (cotton ~90%) | **1.0 – 1.4** | | **Grass Fibres** | Bamboo, Bagasse, Esparto | Cellulose (40–55%), High Silica | **0.6 – 1.1** | | **Animal Fibres** | Wool, Silk, Hair | Keratin / Fibroin (Proteins) | **1.3 – 1.4** | | **Mineral Fibres** | Asbestos (banned), Wollastonite | Silicates | **2.5 – 3.0** | > [!NOTE] > **Bast and leaf fibres dominate NFSPC literature** because their high cellulose content and microfibril angle (typically 8–12°) yield tensile strengths of **400–900 MPa** — comparable to E-glass fibres (which have density ~2.6 g/cm³ vs. natural fibre ~1.4 g/cm³, giving natural fibres a clear *specific strength* advantage). ## 1.4 Why Combine Natural Fibres with Synthetic Polymers? — The Driving Forces 1. **Cost Reduction:** Natural fibres cost roughly **$0.20–0.50/kg** versus **$1.5–3.0/kg** for glass fibres. 2. **Weight Reduction:** Specific gravity of natural fibres (~1.4) is roughly **half** that of glass fibre (~2.6). 3. **Renewability & Carbon Footprint:** Lignocellulosic fibres sequester CO₂ during growth; processing energy is ~**60% lower** than for glass fibre production. 4. **Tool Wear Reduction:** Natural fibres are non-abrasive, allowing longer mould life compared to mineral/glass fibre composites. 5. **Damping & Acoustic Performance:** Hollow lumen and porous cell-wall structure of natural fibres provide excellent **vibration damping** and **sound absorption** — valuable in automotive interior panels. 6. **Sustainability Marketing Value:** "Bio-content" percentage is a marketable property, especially in European automotive and packaging markets. > [!IMPORTANT] > The synthetic matrix is retained (instead of fully bio-based polymers) because it offers **superior moisture resistance, melt processability, and consistent mechanical properties** — overcoming the well-known drawbacks of natural fibres: high moisture absorption, poor interfacial adhesion with hydrophobic polymers, and thermal degradation above ~**220 °C**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Compositional Architecture of an NFSPC A natural fibre-synthetic polymer composite is a **three-phase system** at the microscale: 1. **Fibre Phase (Reinforcement):** The load-bearing constituent. Provides tensile strength and stiffness. 2. **Matrix Phase (Synthetic Polymer):** Transfers load to fibres, protects fibres from environmental attack, and provides shape and surface finish. 3. **Interphase (Critical Third Phase):** The region of finite thickness at the fibre-matrix boundary where chemical/physical bonding occurs. *This is the single most important factor governing the macroscopic properties of NFSPCs.* > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Examiner Tip:** Whenever a question asks "why are properties of NFSPCs lower than theoretical predictions?", the expected answer is **weak interphase bonding due to the hydrophilic–hydrophobic mismatch between natural fibres and synthetic matrices**. ## 2.2 The Interfacial Chemistry Problem Natural fibres are inherently **hydrophilic** because of the abundance of **hydroxyl (–OH) groups** in cellulose, hemicellulose, and lignin. Synthetic polymers like PP, PE, and PS are **hydrophobic** (non-polar). When these are brought together: - Poor **wetting** of the fibre surface by the molten polymer occurs. - Weak **van der Waals bonding** and minimal **mechanical interlocking** result. - Water molecules migrate to the interface, causing **debonding**, **swelling**, and **microvoid formation**. This is quantified by the **contact angle (θ)** at the fibre-matrix interface: $$\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$$ Where: - $\gamma_{LV}$ = liquid-vapour surface tension of the polymer melt - $\gamma_{SV}$ = solid-vapour surface energy of the fibre - $\theta$ = contact angle (smaller θ → better wetting) For good wetting, **θ < 90°** is required. Untreated natural fibre surfaces typically exhibit θ > 110° with molten PP — confirming poor wetting. ## 2.3 Surface Modification Strategies (Interfacial Engineering) To overcome the hydrophilic–hydrophobic mismatch, several **fibre treatment methods** are industrially employed: ### (a) Physical Treatments - **Corona Discharge:** Ionizes air, generates oxygenated functional groups (–OH, –COOH) on fibre surface. - **Plasma Treatment (O₂, NH₃, Ar):** Etches surface, introduces polar groups, increases surface roughness. - **Mercerization (Alkaline Treatment):** Immersion in **NaOH solution (typically 4–10 wt%)** at room temperature for 1–4 hours. Swells the fibre, removes hemicellulose, waxes, and pectin, exposing more cellulose microfibrils and increasing surface roughness. ### (b) Chemical Treatments - **Silane Coupling Agents:** Compounds of the type **R–Si(OR')₃** (e.g., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane, vinyltrimethoxysilane) react with both –OH groups of cellulose and functional groups of the matrix, forming a chemical bridge. * General reaction: $\text{Fibre–OH} + \text{(R'O)}_3\text{Si–R} \rightarrow \text{Fibre–O–Si(OR')}_2\text{–R} + \text{R'OH}$ - **Maleic Anhydride Grafting (MAPP, MAPP-g-PP):** The most industrially successful method. Polypropylene is grafted with maleic anhydride (~0.5–2 wt%); the anhydride groups react with fibre –OH, while the PP backbone entangles with the matrix. - **Acetylation:** Replaces hydrophilic –OH with hydrophobic acetyl groups, reducing moisture absorption by up to **50%**. ### (c) Biological Treatments - Use of enzymes (e.g., laccase, pectinase) or fungi to selectively remove surface impurities without damaging cellulose. ## 2.4 The Rule of Mixtures and Its Limits For unidirectional, continuous-fibre composites loaded along the fibre axis, the **longitudinal Young's modulus** is given by: $$E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$ And the **longitudinal tensile strength** is given by: $$\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m' V_m$$ Where: - $E_f, E_m$ = Young's moduli of fibre and matrix respectively - $V_f, V_m$ = volume fractions of fibre and matrix ($V_f + V_m = 1$) - $\sigma_f$ = fibre tensile strength - $\sigma_m'$ = matrix stress at fibre failure strain > [!IMPORTANT] > This rule **under-predicts** the modulus of natural-fibre composites in the **transverse direction** and **over-predicts** the longitudinal strength when interfacial bonding is poor. A **modified rule of mixtures** with an efficiency factor $\eta_l$ is often used: > $$E_L = \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m V_m$$ > For untreated natural fibre-PP systems, $\eta_l$ typically ranges from **0.3 to 0.6**, indicating substantial fibre inefficiency. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Units | Application | |:--|:--|:--|:--| | 1 | Volume fraction of fibre | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | Convert weight fraction to volume fraction | | 2 | Rule of Mixtures (modulus) | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | Longitudinal stiffness (ideal case) | | 3 | Inverse Rule of Mixtures | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Transverse stiffness (UD composite) | | 4 | Modified Rule of Mixtures | $E_L = \eta_l E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | With fibre length/orientation efficiency | | 5 | Critical Fibre Length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2 \tau_i}$ | Minimum fibre length for full load transfer | | 6 | Aspect Ratio Check | $L / d \ge L_c / d$ | If satisfied, fibre can carry full load | | 7 | Halpin–Tsai Equation | $E_L = E_m \dfrac{1 + 2(l/d)\eta_L V_f}{1 - \eta_L V_f}$ | Short-fibre composite modulus | | 8 | Halpin–Tsai Parameter | $\eta_L = \dfrac{E_f / E_m - 1}{E_f / E_m + 2(l/d)}$ | Used in Halpin–Tsai equation | | 9 | Composite Density | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | Theoretical composite density | | 10 | Void Content (ASTM D2734) | $V_v = \dfrac{\rho_{th} - \rho_{me}}{\rho_{th}}$ | Quality control | | 11 | Moisture Absorption (Fick's Law) | $M_t = M_\infty \left[ 1 - \exp\left(-7.3 \left(\dfrac{Dt}{h^2}\right)^{0.75}\right) \right]$ | Diffusion kinetics | | 12 | Contact Angle (Young's Eq.) | $\gamma_{LV} \cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$ | Wetting criterion | | 13 | Specific Strength | $SSA = \dfrac{\sigma}{\rho}$ | Material selection efficiency | | 14 | Weight Fraction | $W_f = \dfrac{\rho_f V_f}{\rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m}$ | Convert volume ↔ weight fraction | ## 2.6 Critical Fibre Length — The Load Transfer Limit For short, discontinuous natural fibres (as is common in injection-moulded NFSPCs), the **critical fibre length** $L_c$ determines whether the fibre can be loaded to its ultimate tensile strength: $$L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$$ Where: - $\sigma_f$ = fibre tensile strength (MPa) - $d$ = fibre diameter (m) - $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength (MPa) > **Engineering Rule:** For a fibre to carry the **full matrix load** at fibre failure, the **aspect ratio** $L/d$ must satisfy $L/d \ge L_c/d$. For untreated jute-PP, $\tau_i \approx 4$ MPa, giving $L_c/d \approx 60$ — meaning the jute fibre must be at least ~3 mm long for a 50 µm diameter fibre. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: The Rule of Mixtures from a Strain-Compatibility Argument We derive the longitudinal modulus expression for a unidirectional fibre-reinforced composite loaded in the fibre direction, assuming **isostrain conditions** (both fibre and matrix experience the same strain $\varepsilon$). **Step 1: Force Equilibrium on a Cross-Section** Consider a composite cross-section of total area $A_c$ containing fibre area $A_f$ and matrix area $A_m$. Under tensile load $P_c$ parallel to the fibres: $$P_c = P_f + P_m$$ **Step 2: Express Each Force in Terms of Stress** $$P_c = \sigma_c A_c, \quad P_f = \sigma_f A_f, \quad P_m = \sigma_m A_m$$ Therefore: $$\sigma_c A_c = \sigma_f A_f + \sigma_m A_m$$ **Step 3: Divide Through by the Total Area** This normalizes by area to introduce volume fractions: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \left(\dfrac{A_f}{A_c}\right) + \sigma_m \left(\dfrac{A_m}{A_c}\right)$$ **Step 4: Identify Volume Fractions** For fibres aligned unidirectionally, area fraction = volume fraction: $V_f = A_f/A_c$ and $V_m = A_m/A_c = 1 - V_f$. Thus: $$\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$$ **Step 5: Apply Isostrain Condition ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$)** Divide both sides by the common strain $\varepsilon$: $$\dfrac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon} V_f + \dfrac{\sigma_m}{\varepsilon} V_m$$ **Step 6: Recognize the Engineering Moduli** Since $E = \sigma/\varepsilon$: $$\boxed{E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m}$$ This is the **Voigt upper bound** for elastic modulus. It represents the maximum achievable stiffness (achieved when load is applied exactly parallel to all fibres). ## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Transverse Modulus) For loading **perpendicular** to the fibres, the **isostress condition** applies: $\sigma_c = \sigma_f = \sigma_m = \sigma$. **Step 1: Compatibility on Total Strain** $$\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f V_f + \varepsilon_m V_m$$ **Step 2: Substitute $\varepsilon = \sigma/E$** $$\dfrac{\sigma}{E_T} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E_f} V_f + \dfrac{\sigma}{E_m} V_m$$ **Step 3: Cancel $\sigma$ and Rearrange** $$\boxed{\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}}$$ This is the **Reuss lower bound** — the minimum achievable transverse stiffness. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Insight:** For random/short-fibre NFSPCs (the most common commercial case), the effective modulus lies *between* the Voigt and Reuss bounds. The **Halpin–Tsai equation** provides a more accurate interpolation for short-fibre systems. ## 3.3 Worked Example: Volume Fraction Conversion (KTU 3-mark style) **Problem:** A jute-PP composite contains **40 wt% jute fibre** and **60 wt% PP matrix**. Given the densities: $\rho_{jute} = 1.45 \text{ g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_{PP} = 0.91 \text{ g/cm}^3$. Calculate the **volume fraction of jute** in the composite. **Step 1: Take a basis of 100 g of composite** * Mass of jute: $W_f = 40 \text{ g}$ * Mass of PP: $W_m = 60 \text{ g}$ **Step 2: Calculate the volume occupied by each constituent** $$ \begin{aligned} V_f &= \dfrac{W_f}{\rho_f} = \dfrac{40 \text{ g}}{1.45 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 27.586 \text{ cm}^3 \\[6pt] V_m &= \dfrac{W_m}{\rho_m} = \dfrac{60 \text{ g}}{0.91 \text{ g/cm}^3} = 65.934 \text{ cm}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Total Volume** $$ V_c = V_f + V_m = 27.586 + 65.934 = 93.520 \text{ cm}^3 $$ **Step 4: Calculate the Volume Fractions** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{f,\text{jute}} &= \dfrac{V_f}{V_c} = \dfrac{27.586}{93.520} = 0.295 \;\;(\text{or } 29.5\%) \\[6pt] V_{m,\text{PP}} &= \dfrac{V_m}{V_c} = \dfrac{65.934}{93.520} = 0.705 \;\;(\text{or } 70.5\%) \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examination Note:** This is a frequently asked KTU question. The *key trick* is to convert weight % to volume % using densities. Always show all four steps explicitly for full marks. ## 3.4 Worked Example: Halpin–Tsai Equation Application **Problem:** Estimate the Young's modulus of a **short sisal fibre-PP composite** containing $V_f = 0.30$ sisal fibre. Sisal fibre: $E_f = 15 \text{ GPa}$, $l = 6 \text{ mm}$, $d = 0.25 \text{ mm}$. PP matrix: $E_m = 1.5 \text{ GPa}$. **Step 1: Calculate the Aspect Ratio** $$ l/d = \dfrac{6 \text{ mm}}{0.25 \text{ mm}} = 24 $$ **Step 2: Calculate the Halpin–Tsai Parameter $\eta_L$** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_L &= \dfrac{E_f/E_m - 1}{E_f/E_m + 2(l/d)} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{15/1.5 - 1}{15/1.5 + 2(24)} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{10 - 1}{10 + 48} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{9}{58} = 0.1552 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply the Halpin–Tsai Equation** $$ \begin{aligned} E_L &= E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2(l/d)\eta_L V_f}{1 - \eta_L V_f} \\[6pt] &= 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2(24)(0.1552)(0.30)}{1 - (0.1552)(0.30)} \\[6pt] &= 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2.2349}{1 - 0.04655} \\[6pt] &= 1.5 \cdot \dfrac{3.2349}{0.95345} \\[6pt] &= 1.5 \times 3.393 = 5.09 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compare with Rule of Mixtures** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{ROM} &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m = (15)(0.30) + (1.5)(0.70) = 4.5 + 1.05 = 5.55 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned} $$ The Halpin–Tsai prediction (**5.09 GPa**) is **lower** than the Rule of Mixtures value (**5.55 GPa**), as expected for short-fibre systems where the fibres are not perfectly aligned and load transfer is incomplete. This is a typical result for random short-fibre NFSPCs. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Microstructural Block Architecture of an NFSPC ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["NATURAL FIBRE REINFORCEMENT"] --> nodeB["Cellulose Microfibrils
High axial stiffness"] nodeA --> nodeC["Hemicellulose Matrix
Binds microfibrils"] nodeA --> nodeD["Lignin Sheath
Hydrophobic outer layer"] nodeA --> nodeE["Pectin Waxes Surface Layer
Waxy impurities"] nodeF["INTERPHASE REGION"] --> nodeG["Silanol Coupling Bridge
R-Si-O-Fibre covalent bond"] nodeF --> nodeH["MAPP Compatibilizer Layer
Anhydride reacts with OH"] nodeF --> nodeI["Mechanical Interlock
Surface roughness anchor"] nodeJ["SYNTHETIC POLYMER MATRIX"] --> nodeK["Thermoplastic Examples
PP PE PA PVC PC"] nodeJ --> nodeL["Thermoset Examples
Epoxy UPE Vinyl Ester"] nodeB --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeG --> nodeJ nodeH --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeJ nodeK --> nodeM["FINAL COMPOSITE PROPERTIES"] nodeL --> nodeM nodeM --> nodeN["Tensile Strength 30-80 MPa"] nodeM --> nodeO["Young Modulus 2-8 GPa"] nodeM --> nodeP["Density 0.95-1.20 g per cm3"] nodeM --> nodeQ["Moisture Absorption 1-8 percent"] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram: Compression Moulding Route for NFSPCs ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeR1["Natural Fibre
Jute Sisal Flax"] --> nodeS1 nodeR2["Synthetic Resin
PP PE UPE"] --> nodeS1 nodeR3["Coupling Agent
MAPP Silane"] --> nodeS1 nodeS1["DRY BLENDING AND
COMPOUNDING"] --> nodeS2 nodeS2["EXTRUSION OR
INTERNAL MIXER"] --> nodeS3 nodeS3["GRANULATION OR
PELLETIZING"] --> nodeS4 nodeS4["COMPRESSION MOULDING
150 to 200 C 5 to 20 MPa"] --> nodeS5 nodeS5["COOLING UNDER
PRESSURE"] --> nodeS6 nodeS6["DEMOULDING AND
TRIMMING"] --> nodeS7 nodeS7["FINAL NFSPC
COMPONENT"] nodeR4["Fibre Treatment
Alkaline Silane Acetylation"] -.-> nodeR1 nodeR5["Filler Additives
Fire Retardant UV Stabilizer"] -.-> nodeS1 ``` ## 4.3 Decision Matrix: Selection of Fibre Treatment ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["NEED TO IMPROVE
FIBRE MATRIX BONDING"] --> nodeB{"Fibre Type?"} nodeB -->|"Lignocellulosic
Jute Sisal Hemp"| nodeC{"Matrix Type?"} nodeB -->|"Cellulosic
Cotton"| nodeD["Use Mercerization
or Plasma"] nodeC -->|"Polyolefin
PP PE"| nodeE["USE MAPP
MOST EFFECTIVE"] nodeC -->|"Polyester
UPE Epoxy"| nodeF["USE SILANE
COUPLING AGENT"] nodeC -->|"Polyamide
Nylon 6 6"| nodeG["Use MAPP OR
Epoxy Compatibilizer"] nodeE --> nodeH["Industrial Standard
0.5 to 2 wt percent MAPP"] nodeF --> nodeI["3 percent Silane
in Ethanol Water"] nodeG --> nodeJ["1 to 3 percent
MAPP or Epoxy"] nodeD --> nodeK["Final Treated Fibre
Ready for Compounding"] nodeH --> nodeK nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeK ``` ## 4.4 Schematic: Stress Transfer Mechanism at Fibre-Matrix Interface ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["APPLIED TENSILE
STRESS SIGMA C"] --> nodeB["Matrix Carries Load
SIGMA M"] nodeB --> nodeC["Stress Transfer via
Interfacial Shear TAU I"] nodeC --> nodeD["Fibre Reaches
Maximum Stress SIGMA F"] nodeD --> nodeE{"Aspect Ratio L over d
Greater than Lc over d?"} nodeE -->|"YES"| nodeF["FIBRE FRACTURE
AT SIGMA F"] nodeE -->|"NO"| nodeG["FIBRE PULL OUT
INEFFICIENT LOAD TRANSFER"] nodeF --> nodeH["STRONG COMPOSITE
High Strength"] nodeG --> nodeI["WEAK COMPOSITE
Low Strength"] nodeJ["KEY DESIGN RULE"] --> nodeE nodeJ --> nodeK["L over d greater than
SIGMA F divided by 2 TAU I"] ``` > [!NOTE] > The block diagrams above represent **microstructural architecture, processing flow, treatment selection logic, and load transfer mechanisms** — all critical concepts tested in KTU Module 4 examinations. Note that physical drawings (e.g., SEM micrographs) cannot be rendered in Mermaid; the architecture flow diagrams are used in lieu of complex physical schematics per the protocol safeguards. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model] **Q: Define natural fibre-synthetic polymer composites. List any four commonly used natural fibres and four synthetic polymer matrices used in such composites.** **Model Answer:** > Natural fibre-synthetic polymer composites (NFSPCs) are engineered materials in which natural fibres of plant, animal, or mineral origin are embedded as the reinforcing phase in a synthetic polymer matrix (thermoplastic or thermoset) to obtain a hybrid material that combines the renewability and low density of natural fibres with the processability and durability of synthetic polymers. **Common Natural Fibres:** 1. Jute 2. Flax 3. Sisal 4. Hemp (also: kenaf, coir, banana, abaca, ramie) **Common Synthetic Polymer Matrices:** 1. Polypropylene (PP) 2. Polyethylene (PE) 3. Unsaturated Polyester (UPE) 4. Epoxy (also: PVC, PA/Nylon, Vinyl Ester, PS) ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model] **Q: What is the role of maleic anhydride grafted polypropylene (MAPP) as a compatibilizer in natural fibre-PP composites? Write the chemical interaction involved.** **Model Answer:** > MAPP acts as a **coupling agent / compatibilizer** that bridges the hydrophilic natural fibre and the hydrophobic PP matrix. The maleic anhydride groups in MAPP undergo esterification with the hydroxyl (–OH) groups on the cellulose surface, forming a covalent **ester bond**, while the PP backbone of MAPP entangles with the bulk PP matrix through physical chain interpenetration. The chemical interaction is: $$ \text{Fibre–OH} + \text{(MAPP anhydride)} \longrightarrow \text{Fibre–O–CO–CH=CH–CO–O–PP} $$ This creates a strong fibre-matrix interphase, improving tensile strength, flexural strength, and especially water resistance of the composite. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **interfacial adhesion mechanisms** in natural fibre-synthetic polymer composites. Why is the fibre-matrix interphase considered the most critical factor governing composite performance? **(b) [7 Marks)** With a neat sketch, describe the **compression moulding process** for fabricating jute fibre-reinforced polypropylene composites. List the key process parameters that affect the final composite properties. #### Model Solution to Part (a): The **interfacial adhesion** between natural fibre and synthetic polymer matrix governs the efficiency of load transfer and hence the macroscopic mechanical properties of the composite. In NFSPCs, the interphase is particularly critical because of the inherent **chemical incompatibility** between hydrophilic lignocellulosic fibres and hydrophobic polymer matrices. **The four primary adhesion mechanisms are:** **1. Adsorption and Wetting (Thermodynamic Adhesion):** For good adhesion, the molten polymer must **wet** the fibre surface, characterized by a low contact angle ($\theta < 90°$). Wetting is governed by surface energies, described by **Young's equation**: $$\gamma_{LV}\cos\theta = \gamma_{SV} - \gamma_{SL}$$ Natural fibres have surface energies of ~30–50 mJ/m² (polar), while PP has ~20 mJ/m² (non-polar). The mismatch causes poor wetting and weak adhesion. **2. Mechanical Interlocking:** Surface roughness provides physical anchoring points for the matrix. Alkali (NaOH) treatment of fibres removes surface impurities and hemicellulose, creating micro-roughness that improves mechanical interlocking. **3. Chemical Bonding:** The strongest mechanism. Created by **coupling agents** like silanes (R–Si(OR')₃) and **MAPP**, which form covalent bonds with both fibre and matrix. The anhydride group of MAPP reacts with fibre –OH: $$\text{Fibre–OH} + \text{MAPP} \rightarrow \text{Fibre–OOC–CH=CH–COOH} + \text{PP chain}$$ **4. Electrostatic and Diffusion (Minor) Bonding:** Diffusion of polymer chains into the fibre cell wall and electrostatic attraction between polar groups contribute weakly to adhesion. **Why the Interphase is the Most Critical Factor:** - It is the **load transfer medium**: tensile stress applied to the matrix must be transmitted to the fibre through the interphase. - A weak interphase leads to **fibre pull-out** rather than fibre fracture, drastically reducing strength. - It governs **moisture sensitivity**: water diffuses preferentially to a weak interphase, causing swelling and debonding. - The **rule of mixtures efficiency factor** $\eta_l$ is almost entirely a function of interphase quality. #### Model Solution to Part (b): **Compression Moulding Process for Jute-PP Composites:** **Step 1: Fibre Preparation** Jute fibres (woven mat or chopped fibres) are dried at 80–100 °C for 12–24 hours to reduce moisture content below 4%. **Step 2: Fibre Treatment (Optional)** Alkali treatment (5% NaOH, 2 hours) or silane/MAPP coating to improve interfacial bonding. **Step 3: Lay-up / Preform Preparation** Fibre mats or fibre-matrix preforms are stacked in the mould cavity. For a laminate of 3 mm thickness, 4–6 plies are typically used. **Step 4: Mould Closing and Heating** The mould is closed and heated to **170–190 °C** (above PP melting point of ~165 °C) using electric or oil-heated platens. **Step 5: Application of Pressure** A pressure of **5–15 MPa** is applied to consolidate the laminate, squeeze out trapped air, and ensure intimate fibre-matrix contact. **Step 6: Holding Time** Pressure is held for **5–15 minutes** to allow complete melting, flow, and consolidation. **Step 7: Cooling Under Pressure** The mould is cooled (water-cooled platens) to ~40–60 °C while maintaining pressure to prevent warpage and void formation. **Step 8: Demoulding and Post-processing** The composite is ejected and trimmed. **Key Process Parameters Affecting Properties:** | Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Properties | |:--|:--|:--| | Moulding Temperature | 170–200 °C | Higher T → lower melt viscosity, better impregnation, but fibre degradation >220 °C | | Pressure | 5–15 MPa | Higher P → lower void content, better fibre wetting | | Holding Time | 5–20 min | Longer time → better consolidation, but risk of fibre degradation | | Cooling Rate | 5–20 °C/min | Slow cooling → better crystallinity in PP matrix | | Fibre Content | 20–50 wt% | Higher Wf → higher strength up to optimum (~40%), then processability drops | | Fibre Length | 3–25 mm | Longer fibres → higher strength (up to Lc) | ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **bast fibres, leaf fibres, and seed/fruit fibres** in terms of their chemical composition, mechanical properties, and suitability for use in NFSPCs. Provide specific examples in each category. **(b) [7 Marks]** A sisal fibre-PP composite contains **30 wt% sisal fibre** and **70 wt% PP**. The density of sisal is **1.40 g/cm³** and PP is **0.90 g/cm³**. Calculate: - (i) The volume fraction of sisal fibre and PP matrix. - (ii) The theoretical density of the composite. - (iii) The longitudinal Young's modulus using the Rule of Mixtures, given $E_{sisal} = 12$ GPa and $E_{PP} = 1.5$ GPa. - (iv) The transverse modulus using the inverse rule of mixtures. #### Model Solution to Part (a): | Property | Bast Fibres | Leaf Fibres | Seed/Fruit Fibres | |:--|:--|:--|:--| | **Examples** | Jute, Flax, Hemp, Kenaf, Ramie | Sisal, Abaca, Banana, PALF | Cotton, Coir, Kapok | | **Cellulose Content** | 60–80% (highest) | 50–70% | 70–90% (cotton) | | **Hemicellulose** | 10–20% | 15–25% | 5–10% | | **Lignin** | 5–15% (lower) | 5–15% | <5% (cotton) to 35% (coir) | | **Tensile Strength** | 400–900 MPa (highest) | 300–700 MPa | 200–600 MPa | | **Young's Modulus** | 20–80 GPa | 10–30 GPa | 5–15 GPa (cotton), 4–6 GPa (coir) | | **Density** | 1.3–1.5 g/cm³ | 1.2–1.5 g/cm³ | 1.0–1.4 g/cm³ | | **Microfibril Angle** | 8–12° (low → high stiffness) | 10–20° | 20–45° (cotton) | | **Suitability for NFSPCs** | **Excellent** — best mechanical performance, automotive structural use | **Good** — good strength, used in panels, ropes, twine | **Cotton**: moderate; **Coir**: low stiffness, mainly for low-load applications | > **Summary:** Bast fibres are the most suitable for **high-performance structural NFSPCs** (e.g., automotive door panels, flooring). Leaf fibres offer a good balance for **semi-structural applications**. Seed/fruit fibres (especially coir) are limited to **non-structural, low-load uses** such as packaging, mattresses, and noise-dampening panels. #### Model Solution to Part (b): **(i) Volume Fractions:** Take basis of 100 g composite: $W_f = 30$ g sisal, $W_m = 70$ g PP. $$ \begin{aligned} V_f &= \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{30/1.40}{30/1.40 + 70/0.90} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{21.429}{21.429 + 77.778} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{21.429}{99.207} = 0.216 \quad (\text{21.6\% sisal}) \end{aligned} $$ $$ V_m = 1 - V_f = 0.784 \quad (\text{78.4\% PP}) $$ **(ii) Theoretical Composite Density:** $$ \begin{aligned} \rho_c &= \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m \\[6pt] &= (1.40)(0.216) + (0.90)(0.784) \\[6pt] &= 0.3024 + 0.7056 = 1.008 \text{ g/cm}^3 \end{aligned} $$ **(iii) Longitudinal Young's Modulus (Rule of Mixtures):** $$ \begin{aligned} E_L &= E_f V_f + E_m V_m \\[6pt] &= (12)(0.216) + (1.5)(0.784) \\[6pt] &= 2.592 + 1.176 = 3.768 \text{ GPa} \approx 3.77 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned} $$ **(iv) Transverse Modulus (Inverse Rule of Mixtures):** $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{1}{E_T} &= \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{0.216}{12} + \dfrac{0.784}{1.5} \\[6pt] &= 0.0180 + 0.5227 = 0.5407 \text{ GPa}^{-1} \\[6pt] E_T &= \dfrac{1}{0.5407} = 1.850 \text{ GPa} \approx 1.85 \text{ GPa} \end{aligned} $$ > **Interpretation:** The composite is **2.5× stiffer than pure PP** along the fibre direction, but only ~1.2× stiffer transversely — clearly demonstrating the **anisotropic nature** of fibre-reinforced composites. ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in NFSPC Questions (KTU Board Examiner Insights):** > 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Many students fail to convert weight fraction to volume fraction correctly in numerical problems. Always use densities — *this alone can cost 2–3 marks*. > 2. **Confusing longitudinal vs. transverse modulus:** Applying the wrong rule of mixtures (Voigt vs. Reuss) leads to a ~50% error in numerical answers. > 3. **Omitting the interphase discussion:** Any "explain the properties" question *must* include a discussion of the fibre-matrix interphase and its modification. Generic answers like "fibres are strong" without addressing bonding mechanisms lose 3+ marks. > 4. **Forgetting fibre treatment steps:** When asked about composite processing, students often skip alkali treatment or MAPP addition — this is considered a *major omission* in KTU valuation. > 5. **Using a single rule for short fibres:** Applying the simple Rule of Mixtures to short-fibre composites without mentioning the Halpin–Tsai or critical length concept is incomplete. > 6. **Ignoring moisture effects:** NFSPCs are moisture-sensitive. A complete answer must mention the *adverse effect of moisture on properties* and the role of treatments in mitigating it. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Natural Fibre-Synthetic Polymer Composites** - **Definition:** NFSPCs = natural fibre (reinforcement) + synthetic polymer (matrix) hybrid composites. - **Fibre Types:** Bast > Leaf > Seed/Fruit in terms of mechanical performance for NFSPC applications. - **Common Systems:** Jute-PP, Flax-PP, Sisal-PP, Hemp-UPE, Coir-Epoxy are the most studied commercial systems. - **Interfacial Problem:** Hydrophilic fibre ↔ Hydrophobic matrix → poor adhesion, moisture sensitivity. - **Key Treatments:** Alkali (NaOH) mercerization, Silane coupling, MAPP grafting, Acetylation, Plasma, Corona. - **MAPP is the industrial gold standard** for polyolefin-based NFSPCs. - **Rule of Mixtures (Longitudinal):** $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ — Voigt upper bound. - **Inverse Rule (Transverse):** $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ — Reuss lower bound. - **Critical Fibre Length:** $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f d}{2\tau_i}$ — determines whether short fibres can be fully loaded. - **Halpin–Tsai Equation:** Used for short-fibre composites — interpolates between Voigt and Reuss bounds. - **Processing Routes:** Compression moulding, Injection moulding, Extrusion, Filament winding, Resin transfer moulding (RTM), Hand lay-up. - **Key Process Parameters:** Temperature, Pressure, Holding time, Cooling rate, Fibre content, Fibre aspect ratio. - **Density Advantage:** Natural fibres (~1.4 g/cm³) are half the density of glass fibres (~2.6 g/cm³). - **Cost Advantage:** Natural fibres cost ~10–25% of glass fibre prices. - **Limitations:** Moisture absorption, poor fire resistance, odour emission, fibre-matrix incompatibility, low upper processing temperature (<220 °C). - **Applications:** Automotive interior panels (Mercedes, Toyota, Ford), building materials (door frames, decking), consumer goods, packaging, and electrical housings. - **Anisotropy:** Always mention anisotropy — properties depend on fibre orientation and length. - **Sustainability:** Lignocellulosic fibres are CO₂-neutral, renewable, biodegradable, and require low processing energy. ---

topics.title: Fully Bio-Based Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT415 - ADVANCED BIOPOLYMERS & BIOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fully Bio-Based Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Fully Bio-Based Composite** (also termed a *green composite* or *bio-composite*) is a multiphase engineered material in which **both the matrix (binder) phase and the reinforcement (fibrous/filler) phase are derived entirely from renewable biological origins**. Unlike partially bio-based composites (where only one constituent is renewable), fully bio-based composites achieve 100% bio-based carbon content, eliminating dependence on fossil-derived polymers (e.g., polypropylene, epoxy, polyester) and synthetic fibres (e.g., glass, carbon, aramid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> A fully bio-based composite is a heterogeneous material system composed of a **bio-based polymer matrix** (e.g., polylactic acid (PLA), polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs), starch, cellulose derivatives, soy protein, chitosan) reinforced with **natural fibres or bio-based fillers** (e.g., flax, jute, hemp, kenaf, sisal, bamboo, wood flour, micro/nanocellulose, chitin), whose entire life cycle from feedstock cultivation to end-of-life biodegradation is sourced from renewable biomass.

### 1.2 Two Mandatory Constituents (KTU Mandatory Classification)

| S.No | Constituent | Function | Bio-Based Examples |
|------|-------------|----------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Bio-Matrix** | Continuous phase – binds reinforcement, transfers load, protects fibres | PLA, PHB/PHV, PHBV, starch plastic, soy protein isolate, lignin-based resins, cellulose acetate |
| 2 | **Bio-Reinforcement** | Dispersed phase – bears the mechanical load | Flax, jute, hemp, kenaf, sisal, ramie, abaca, banana fibre, bamboo, nanocellulose (CNC/CNF), chitin nanowhiskers |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Jute Sack & Clay Pot Model"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Builder:** Imagine building a wall where the **mortar** (the sticky paste between bricks) is made from **boiled rice starch** instead of cement, and the **bricks** are made from **sun-dried jute fibre bundles** instead of fired clay. The resulting wall is strong enough to enclose a room, fully organic, and after demolition can be ploughed back into farmland. A fully bio-based composite works exactly like this — the **bio-matrix** is the "mortar" transferring stress, and the **bio-fibre** is the "brick" carrying the load. The difference is that in engineering composites, we tune fibre orientation, length, and interfacial adhesion with surgical precision.

### 1.4 Why "Fully" Bio-Based? — The Distinction

The qualifier *"fully"* is non-trivial in KTU assessments. The hierarchy is:

$$
\text{Conventional Composite} \xrightarrow{\text{1 swap}} \text{Partially Bio-Based} \xrightarrow{\text{2 swaps}} \text{Fully Bio-Based}
$$

- **Conventional**: Epoxy + Glass fibre
- **Partially Bio-Based**: Epoxy (petroleum) + Jute fibre (bio), **OR** PLA (bio) + Glass fibre (petroleum)
- **Fully Bio-Based**: PLA (bio) + Jute fibre (bio) ✅ — qualifies under this module

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical CO Mapping (KTU 2024):** This topic aligns with **CO4** – *“Appraise the structure-property-processing relationships of advanced bio-composites for sustainable engineering applications.”* Students must clearly distinguish "bio-based" (carbon from renewable biomass) from "biodegradable" (degradable by microorganisms) — these are **independent properties**. A composite can be bio-based but non-biodegradable (e.g., bio-based PET with natural fibres under specific conditions) or biodegradable but petrochemical-based (e.g., PBAT with glass fibre).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Five-Pillar Design Framework

A fully bio-based composite is engineered by controlling five interdependent variables. Each pillar directly governs the final **specific stiffness**, **specific strength**, **moisture resistance**, and **biodegradability**.

#### Pillar 1: Bio-Matrix Selection
The matrix determines the upper processing temperature ceiling and the moisture barrier performance. Selecting PLA (Tg ≈ 60 °C) restricts service temperature, whereas PHB (Tg ≈ 5 °C) restricts dimensional stability above body temperature.

$$
\text{Bio-Matrix Decision Tree: } \text{Application} \rightarrow \text{T}_{\text{service}} \rightarrow \text{Process Window} \rightarrow \text{Matrix Choice}
$$

#### Pillar 2: Bio-Fibre Selection
Fibre classification uses four axes:
1. **Origin** — bast (flax, jute, hemp), leaf (sisal, abaca), seed (cotton, coir), fruit, wood
2. **Length** — short (≤ 1 mm), long (1–50 mm), continuous
3. **Aspect Ratio** ($L/d$) — must exceed the **critical fibre length** $L_c$
4. **Cellulose content** — higher cellulose → higher tensile strength

#### Pillar 3: Fibre–Matrix Interfacial Adhesion
The **Interfacial Shear Strength (IFSS)** governs stress transfer. Without it, fibres act as voids. Enhancement methods:
- **Alkali treatment** (mercerization with NaOH) – removes hemicellulose/lignin, exposes hydroxyl groups
- **Silane coupling agents** – e.g., 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES)
- **Maleic anhydride grafting** (e.g., PLA-g-MA) – creates covalent ester linkages
- **Acetylation** – reduces fibre hydrophilicity, improves moisture resistance

#### Pillar 4: Fibre Volume Fraction ($V_f$)
$$
V_f + V_m = 1 \quad (\text{no voids assumed})
$$

In practice, voids $V_v$ exist:
$$
V_f + V_m + V_v = 1
$$

Typical optimum $V_f$ for natural fibre composites lies between **0.20 and 0.40** (20–40 vol%).

#### Pillar 5: Processing Route
| Process | Temperature Window | Matrix Suitability | Fibre Length Retained |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Compression Moulding | 150–200 °C | Thermoplastic (PLA, PHBV) | Long/short |
| Injection Moulding | 170–210 °C | Thermoplastic | Short only (shear degrades long fibres) |
| Hand Lay-up + Hot Press | 25–180 °C | Thermoset bio-resin | Long/continuous |
| Extrusion + Pelletizing | 160–190 °C | Thermoplastic | Short |
| Resin Transfer Moulding (RTM) | 25–80 °C | Low-temp bio-resin (epoxidized soy oil) | Continuous mats |
| 3D Printing (FDM) | 190–230 °C | PLA composites | Short/aligned |

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — All equations required for Module 4 numerical/problem questions.**

| Equation # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variable Definitions | Units |
|:----------:|--------------|-------------------|----------------------|:-----:|
| **F1** | Fibre Volume Fraction | $V_f = \dfrac{w_f / \rho_f}{w_f / \rho_f + w_m / \rho_m}$ | $w_f, w_m$ = weight fractions; $\rho_f, \rho_m$ = densities | dimensionless |
| **F2** | Critical Fibre Length | $L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $\sigma_f$ = fibre tensile strength; $d$ = fibre diameter; $\tau_i$ = interfacial shear strength | mm |
| **F3** | Rule of Mixtures — Longitudinal Modulus | $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$ | $E_f, E_m$ = fibre/matrix Young's moduli | GPa |
| **F4** | Inverse Rule of Mixtures — Transverse Modulus | $\dfrac{1}{E_T} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | Same variables as F3 | GPa |
| **F5** | Rule of Mixtures — Longitudinal Strength | $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$ | $\sigma_f, \sigma_m$ = tensile strengths | MPa |
| **F6** | Density of Composite | $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$ | $\rho$ = densities | g/cm³ |
| **F7** | Specific Strength | $\sigma_L / \rho_c$ | ratio | MPa·cm³/g |
| **F8** | Halpin–Tsai Equation | $E_L = E_m \cdot \dfrac{1 + 2\eta V_f}{1 - \eta V_f}$, where $\eta = \dfrac{(E_f/E_m) - 1}{(E_f/E_m) + 2}$ | more accurate than ROM for discontinuous fibres | GPa |
| **F9** | Kelly–Tyson Equation (Strength with Short Fibres) | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f \left(\dfrac{L}{2L_c}\right) + \sigma_m V_m$ (when $L < L_c$); reduces to F5 when $L \geq L_c$ | $L$ = fibre length | MPa |
| **F10** | Moisture Absorption (Fickian) | $M(t) = M_\infty \left[1 - \exp(-Dt)\right]$ | $M_\infty$ = equilibrium uptake; $D$ = diffusivity | wt% |
| **F11** | Bio-Based Carbon Content | $\text{BCC}(\%) = \dfrac{{}^{14}\text{C carbon mass}}{\text{Total carbon mass}} \times 100$ | ASTM D6866 standard | % |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fully bio-based composites are transitioning into load-bearing and semi-structural applications where **sustainability quotient** (kg CO₂-eq/kg material) is mandated by regulations such as the EU Single-Use Plastics Directive, India's *Plastic Waste Management Rules*, and automotive end-of-life vehicle (ELV) directives (> 85% recyclability target).

| Application Sector | Specific Use Case | Matrix / Fibre Pair |
|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Automotive Interior | Door panels, seat backs, parcel shelves, headliners | PP/PLA + flax/hemp/kenaf (e.g., Mercedes E-Class, Toyota Raum) |
| Construction | Partition panels, ceiling tiles, insulation boards, decking | Soy/PET + jute/coir |
| Packaging | Single-use trays, moulded pulp containers, loose-fill foam | PLA/PHB + cellulose fibre |
| Consumer Goods | Laptop cases, phone shells, sporting goods (snowboards, surfboards) | PLA + flax/bamboo |
| Agriculture | Mulch films, seedling trays, controlled-release matrices | Starch + chitosan fibre |
| Biomedical | Bone scaffolds, tissue engineering, fixation pins | PHBV/PCL + chitin/collagen |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Rule of Mixtures for Longitudinal Modulus

**Statement to Prove:**
$$
E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

**Assumptions (Iso-strain model — Voigt Model):**
1. Fibre and matrix experience **equal strain** under longitudinal loading
2. Perfect bonding at the fibre–matrix interface
3. Fibres are continuous, aligned, and uniformly distributed
4. Both phases are linearly elastic

**Derivation Steps:**

Consider a unidirectional composite loaded along the fibre direction with applied load $P_c$ on cross-sectional area $A_c$.

$$
P_c = P_f + P_m
$$

Divide both sides by $A_c$:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \dfrac{A_m}{A_c}
$$

Recognise that the area ratios equal volume fractions for long aligned fibres:

$$
\sigma_c = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

Apply Hooke's Law at iso-strain condition $\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m = \varepsilon$:

$$
E_L \varepsilon = E_f \varepsilon V_f + E_m \varepsilon V_m
$$

Cancel $\varepsilon$ on both sides:

$$
E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m \quad \blacksquare
$$

This is **Equation F3** from the formula sheet.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Critical Fibre Length ($L_c$)

**Statement to Prove:**
$$
L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

**Physical Setup:**
A short fibre of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is embedded in a matrix. Tensile stress $\sigma_f$ develops in the fibre, and interfacial shear stress $\tau_i$ transfers load at the fibre–matrix boundary.

**Force Balance:**
Tensile load carried by the fibre = shear load transferred through the interface

$$
\sigma_f \cdot A_f = \tau_i \cdot A_{\text{surface}}
$$

Substitute geometric quantities:

$$
\sigma_f \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot (\pi d) \cdot \dfrac{L_c}{2}
$$

The factor of 2 in the denominator accounts for **load transfer from both ends** of the fibre.

Solve for $L_c$:

$$
L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i} \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice Standard)

**Problem:**
A fully bio-based composite is made of **flax fibre** reinforced **PLA matrix**. The properties are:

| Property | Flax Fibre | PLA Matrix |
|----------|:----------:|:----------:|
| Young's Modulus | $E_f = 70$ GPa | $E_m = 3.5$ GPa |
| Density | $\rho_f = 1.50$ g/cm³ | $\rho_m = 1.24$ g/cm³ |
| Fibre Diameter | $d = 20 \ \mu$m | — |
| Interfacial Shear Strength | $\tau_i = 25$ MPa | — |
| Fibre Tensile Strength | $\sigma_f = 800$ MPa | — |

**Given:** $V_f = 0.30$, $V_m = 0.70$, fibre length $L = 2$ mm.

**Task:** Calculate (a) longitudinal modulus, (b) composite density, (c) specific modulus, (d) critical fibre length, and (e) verify whether the chosen fibre length is sufficient.

#### Part (a): Longitudinal Modulus (Eq. F3)

$$
E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

$$
E_L = (70)(0.30) + (3.5)(0.70)
$$

$$
E_L = 21.0 + 2.45 = 23.45 \text{ GPa}
$$

#### Part (b): Composite Density (Eq. F6)

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

$$
\rho_c = (1.50)(0.30) + (1.24)(0.70)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.45 + 0.868 = 1.318 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

#### Part (c): Specific Modulus

$$
\dfrac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{23.45}{1.318} = 17.79 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

Compare to glass-fibre polypropylene ($E/\rho \approx 12$ GPa·cm³/g) — flax/PLA is **superior on a per-mass basis**.

#### Part (d): Critical Fibre Length (Eq. F2)

$$
L_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}
$$

$$
L_c = \dfrac{(800 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})(20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m})}{2 \times (25 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})}
$$

$$
L_c = \dfrac{16}{50} = 0.32 \text{ mm}
$$

#### Part (e): Verification

Given $L = 2$ mm and $L_c = 0.32$ mm, compute the ratio:

$$
\dfrac{L}{L_c} = \dfrac{2.0}{0.32} = 6.25 > 1
$$

Since $L \gg L_c$, the fibre is **long enough** to develop its full tensile strength at the centre. This confirms Kelly–Tyson Eq. F9 simplifies to standard Rule of Mixtures Eq. F5.

$$
\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m \approx (800)(0.30) + (60)(0.70) = 240 + 42 = 282 \text{ MPa}
$$

(Using $\sigma_m \approx 60$ MPa for PLA.)

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Natural Fibres vs. E-Glass

| Property | Flax | Jute | Hemp | Kenaf | E-Glass |
|----------|:----:|:----:|:----:|:-----:|:-------:|
| Density (g/cm³) | 1.50 | 1.45 | 1.48 | 1.40 | 2.55 |
| Tensile Strength (MPa) | 800–1500 | 400–800 | 550–900 | 500–900 | 2000–3500 |
| Young's Modulus (GPa) | 60–80 | 10–30 | 30–70 | 40–60 | 70–75 |
| Specific Modulus (GPa·cm³/g) | 40–53 | 7–21 | 20–47 | 29–43 | 27–29 |
| Elongation at Break (%) | 1.5–2.5 | 1.5–2.0 | 1.5–3.0 | 1.5–2.5 | 2.5–3.0 |
| Moisture Absorption (%) | 7–12 | 12–17 | 6–10 | 8–13 | < 0.1 |
| Cost ($/kg) | 0.5–2.0 | 0.3–1.0 | 0.4–1.5 | 0.3–0.8 | 1.5–3.5 |
| CO₂ Footprint (kg CO₂/kg) | ~0.4 | ~0.3 | ~0.5 | ~0.3 | ~1.7 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Natural fibres have **lower absolute strength** than E-glass but **comparable or higher specific modulus** because their density is nearly half. This is the core reason fully bio-based composites are positioned as "lightweight sustainable alternatives" rather than direct strength replacements.

### 4.4 Schematic Description — Microstructure (Textual Fallback)

> [!NOTE]
> **Microstructure Description (Fully Bio-Based Composite Cross-Section):**
> - **Continuous Phase (Bio-Matrix):** Pale, smooth, amorphous regions between fibres — typically PLA or PHBV. Forms the bulk.
> - **Dispersed Phase (Bio-Fibre):** Dark, elongated, cylindrical bundles aligned in one direction (unidirectional) or randomly oriented (isotropic mat). Each fibre is itself a multi-cell composite of cellulose microfibrils embedded in lignin/hemicellulose.
> - **Interface (ITZ):** A thin transition zone (50–500 nm) where fibre surface chemistry meets matrix. Successful modification produces a gradient zone with covalent bonds; failure produces micro-voids that act as crack initiation sites.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define fully bio-based composites. How do they differ from partially bio-based composites and biocomposites?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **fully bio-based composite** is a multicomponent material in which **both the matrix and the reinforcement** are derived from renewable biological sources — e.g., PLA matrix reinforced with flax fibre. The entire carbon content is biomass-derived.

**Differentiation:**
- **Partially bio-based composite:** Only **one** constituent (either matrix or fibre) is renewable. Example: Bio-based PLA reinforced with E-glass fibre — the glass is petrochemical-derived.
- **Biocomposite:** A broader term encompassing any composite containing a bio-derived component, including partially bio-based variants. "Fully bio-based" is a *subset* of biocomposites.

*Marking pattern:* 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for partial vs. full distinction, 1 mark for biocomposite clarification.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Explain the significance of critical fibre length ($L_c$) in short-fibre bio-composites.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **critical fibre length ($L_c$)** is the minimum fibre length required for the fibre to develop its **maximum tensile stress** at its centre through interfacial shear stress transfer from the matrix.

**Significance:**
1. When $L < L_c$: The fibre is **pulled out** before reaching full strength → inefficient reinforcement. Strength follows Kelly–Tyson equation (F9) with $L/2L_c$ correction.
2. When $L \geq L_c$: The fibre is **fully effective**; tensile strength follows the simple Rule of Mixtures (F5).
3. For natural fibres with high $\sigma_f$ and poor $\tau_i$ (e.g., jute/PP), $L_c$ can be **large** (often > 5 mm), mandating longer fibre retention during processing.
4. Process selection (injection moulding vs. compression moulding) is governed by whether the natural fibre length is preserved above $L_c$.

*Marking pattern:* 1 mark definition + formula, 1 mark two-case explanation, 1 mark processing implication.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Attempt either A or B.**
**(a)** Discuss in detail the various **bio-based polymer matrices** used in fully bio-based composites. Compare their properties, processing windows, and biodegradability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fully bio-based composite is made with **kenaf fibre (35 vol%) in a PLA matrix**. Given: $E_f = 53$ GPa, $E_m = 3.6$ GPa, $\sigma_f = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 55$ MPa, $\rho_f = 1.40$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³. Calculate the **longitudinal tensile modulus**, **composite density**, **specific modulus**, and **longitudinal tensile strength** of the composite. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to A(a):**

**Bio-Based Polymer Matrices — Detailed Classification**

**1. Polylactic Acid (PLA):**
- **Source:** Fermentation of corn starch, sugarcane, or cassava → lactic acid → dimerization to lactide → ring-opening polymerization.
- **Properties:** $T_g \approx 60$ °C, $T_m \approx 170$ °C, tensile strength $\approx 60$ MPa, modulus $\approx 3.5$ GPa.
- **Processing:** Injection moulding, extrusion, 3D printing, thermoforming, fibre spinning.
- **Biodegradability:** Compostable under industrial conditions (EN 13432), $T > 58$ °C, RH > 60%.
- **Drawbacks:** Brittle, poor toughness, slow crystallization, hydrolytic degradation sensitivity.

**2. Polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHAs) — PHB, PHBV, PHBHHx:**
- **Source:** Bacterial fermentation (e.g., *Cupriavidus necator*) on sugar/plant oil substrates.
- **Properties:** $T_g \approx 0$–5 °C (PHB), $T_m \approx 170$–180 °C, highly crystalline.
- **Biodegradability:** Fully biodegradable in soil, marine, and compost — the **highest bio-degradability** among bio-polymers.
- **Drawbacks:** Narrow processing window, thermal degradation near melt temperature, high cost.

**3. Starch-Based Plastics:**
- **Source:** Corn, potato, tapioca starch plasticized with glycerol/sorbitol.
- **Properties:** Low strength, high moisture sensitivity unless blended (e.g., starch-PLA blends).
- **Processing:** Thermoplastic starch (TPS) via extrusion.
- **Biodegradability:** Excellent in soil and composting.
- **Drawbacks:** Retrogradation, brittleness without plasticizer.

**4. Cellulose Derivatives:**
- **Examples:** Cellulose acetate (CA), cellulose acetate butyrate (CAB), regenerated cellulose (cellophane).
- **Properties:** Moderate strength, good optical clarity, moisture-sensitive.
- **Processing:** Solvent casting, extrusion (with plasticizers).
- **Biodegradability:** Depends on degree of substitution — CA is partially biodegradable.

**5. Soy Protein and Lignin-Based Resins:**
- **Source:** Soybean meal, lignin from paper/pulp industry.
- **Properties:** Cross-linkable thermosets, formaldehyde-free.
- **Biodegradability:** Excellent.
- **Applications:** Wood adhesives, foam cores, moulded panels.

| Matrix | $T_g$ (°C) | $T_m$ (°C) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Cost ($/kg) | Biodegradability |
|--------|:----------:|:----------:|:---------------------:|:-----------:|:----------------:|
| PLA | 60 | 170 | 60 | 2–4 | Industrial compost |
| PHB | 5 | 175 | 40 | 5–10 | Soil/marine |
| PHBV | 2 | 170 | 35 | 6–12 | Soil/marine |
| TPS | — | — | 5–10 | 0.5–1 | Soil/compost |
| CA | 180 | 230 | 50 | 3–5 | Partial |
| Soy Resin | — | — | 30 (cross-linked) | 1–3 | Compost |

*Marking pattern:* 1 mark for classification, 4 marks for 5 matrix types (0.8 each), 1 mark for comparison table, 1 mark for application relevance.

---

#### **Model Solution to A(b):**

**Given:** $V_f = 0.35$, $V_m = 0.65$; $E_f = 53$ GPa, $E_m = 3.6$ GPa; $\sigma_f = 600$ MPa, $\sigma_m = 55$ MPa; $\rho_f = 1.40$ g/cm³, $\rho_m = 1.25$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Tensile Modulus (Eq. F3):**

$$
E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m
$$

$$
E_L = (53)(0.35) + (3.6)(0.65)
$$

$$
E_L = 18.55 + 2.34 = 20.89 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Composite Density (Eq. F6):**

$$
\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m
$$

$$
\rho_c = (1.40)(0.35) + (1.25)(0.65)
$$

$$
\rho_c = 0.490 + 0.8125 = 1.3025 \text{ g/cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Specific Modulus:**

$$
\dfrac{E_L}{\rho_c} = \dfrac{20.89}{1.3025} = 16.04 \text{ GPa·cm}^3/\text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Longitudinal Tensile Strength (Eq. F5, assuming $L \gg L_c$):**

$$
\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m
$$

$$
\sigma_L = (600)(0.35) + (55)(0.65)
$$

$$
\sigma_L = 210 + 35.75 = 245.75 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|:-----:|
| Longitudinal Modulus $E_L$ | **20.89 GPa** |
| Composite Density $\rho_c$ | **1.3025 g/cm³** |
| Specific Modulus | **16.04 GPa·cm³/g** |
| Longitudinal Tensile Strength $\sigma_L$ | **245.75 MPa** |

*Marking pattern:* 1.5 marks each for $E_L$, $\rho_c$, $E_L/\rho_c$, and $\sigma_L$ calculations (with formula citation). 0.5 marks for unit consistency.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the various **surface modification techniques** used to improve fibre–matrix adhesion in fully bio-based composites. Discuss alkali treatment in detail with chemical equations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **processing methods** employed for manufacturing fully bio-based composites. Compare compression moulding and injection moulding for natural fibre reinforced thermoplastics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to B(a):**

**Surface Modification Techniques for Natural Fibres**

Natural fibres (cellulose-rich) are **hydrophilic** due to –OH groups, while most bio-polymers are **hydrophobic**. This mismatch causes poor interfacial bonding. Five major modification strategies exist:

**1. Alkali Treatment (Mercerization) — Most Common**

**Mechanism:** Treatment with NaOH (typically 4–10 wt%, 1–2 hours at room temperature) removes hemicellulose, lignin, pectin, wax, and oils from fibre surface, exposing more hydroxyl groups and creating a rougher surface.

**Chemical Reaction:**

$$
\text{Fibre–OH} + \text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Fibre–O}^-\text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The sodium-cellulose complex disrupts hydrogen bonding in the cellulose network:

$$
[\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OH})_3]_n + n\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow [\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{O}_2(\text{OH})_2\text{ONa}]_n + n\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

After rinsing, Na⁺ is replaced by H⁺ upon neutralization, leaving the fibre with a cleaner, more reactive, and rougher surface.

**Effects:** Improved fibre aspect ratio, increased crystallinity, better wettability, 20–40% increase in IFSS with PLA.

**2. Silane Coupling Agents:**
General formula: R–Si(OR')₃. Example: 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES).

**Mechanism:**

$$
\text{R–Si(OC}_2\text{H}_5)_3 + 3\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{R–Si(OH)}_3 + 3\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
$$

The silanol reacts with cellulose –OH:

$$
\text{R–Si(OH)}_3 + \text{Fibre–OH} \rightarrow \text{R–Si(OH)}_2\text{–O–Fibre} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The R-group (e.g., amine) reacts with the matrix, forming a **molecular bridge** between fibre and polymer.

**3. Acetylation:**
Acetic anhydride reacts with fibre –OH to reduce hydrophilicity:

$$
\text{Fibre–OH} + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Fibre–OCOCH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}
$$

**4. Maleic Anhydride Grafting:**
Grafted onto PLA backbone (PLA-g-MA); the anhydride reacts with fibre –OH forming **ester linkages**:

$$
\text{PLA–g–MA} + \text{Fibre–OH} \rightarrow \text{PLA–g–MA–O–Fibre}
$$

**5. Plasma Treatment:**
Cold plasma (O₂, N₂, Ar) introduces polar functional groups and etches surface — environmentally friendly, no chemicals.

*Marking pattern:* 1 mark introduction, 2 marks alkali chemistry with equations, 2 marks for two more methods, 1 mark effect quantification, 1 mark engineering relevance.

---

#### **Model Solution to B(b):**

**Processing Methods for Fully Bio-Based Composites**

The processing route is dictated by matrix form, fibre form, and target geometry.

**1. Compression Moulding**
- **Process:** Pre-impregnated sheets (prepregs) or fibre mats + polymer films stacked in a heated mould → closed under pressure (5–25 MPa) at 150–200 °C.
- **Cycle time:** 3–10 minutes.
- **Fibre length:** Preserved (long fibres can be used).
- **Bio-matrix suitability:** Excellent for PLA, PHBV in film/laminate form.
- **Products:** Automotive interior panels (Mercedes-Benz A-Class flax/PLA door panels), structural boards.
- **Advantages:** Low fibre attrition, high $V_f$ achievable (up to 0.5), simple tooling.
- **Disadvantages:** Long cycle time, limited to flat or moderately curved geometries.

**2. Injection Moulding**
- **Process:** Pellets (extrusion-compounded fibre + polymer) fed into heated barrel → melted → injected into mould cavity at high pressure (50–150 MPa).
- **Cycle time:** 15–90 seconds.
- **Fibre length:** Severely reduced (initial 5 mm fibre → 0.2–0.5 mm in part) due to high shear in screw.
- **Bio-matrix suitability:** PLA grades with good flow.
- **Products:** Complex 3D parts, packaging, consumer electronics casings.
- **Advantages:** High volume, complex geometry, tight tolerances.
- **Disadvantages:** Fibre degradation, lower mechanical performance vs. compression moulding.

**3. Extrusion + Pelletizing → Secondary Forming**
- Used to compound pellets with uniform fibre dispersion; subsequently thermoformed, injection moulded, or 3D-printed.

**4. Hand Lay-up + Hot Pressing (Bio-Thermosets)**
- For epoxidized soy oil (ESO) matrices with woven jute/flax mats; cures at 80–120 °C under pressure.

**5. 3D Printing (FDM/FFF)**
- PLA composites (wood-PLA, hemp-PLA, bamboo-PLA filaments) for prototyping and bespoke products.

| Parameter | Compression Moulding | Injection Moulding |
|-----------|:--------------------:|:------------------:|
| Fibre Length Retention | High (≥ 90%) | Low (10–30%) |
| Cycle Time | 3–10 min | 15–90 s |
| Geometry | Flat/curved sheets | Complex 3D |
| Volume Capacity | Low–Medium | High |
| $V_f$ Achievable | 0.30–0.50 | 0.15–0.30 |
| Surface Finish | Moderate | Excellent |
| Cost per Part | Higher (manual handling) | Lower (automated) |

*Marking pattern:* 1.5 marks process description, 1.5 marks for compression moulding detail, 1.5 marks for injection moulding detail, 1.5 marks comparison table, 1 mark for additional processes.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "bio-based" with "biodegradable" (–2 marks):** Bio-based refers to *carbon source* (renewable biomass); biodegradable refers to *end-of-life microbial degradation*. A bio-based PET is NOT biodegradable; a PBAT with glass fibre is biodegradable but not bio-based.
>
> 2. **Mixing up Rule of Mixtures directions (–2 marks):** Longitudinal ($E_L$) uses direct Voigt formula F3; transverse ($E_T$) uses inverse Reuss formula F4. Many students blindly apply ROM to both directions and lose full marks.
>
> 3. **Unit errors in modulus-strength calculations (–1 mark):** Always verify whether $E$ is in **GPa** and $\sigma$ in **MPa** before substituting into equations — the numerical mismatch (1 GPa = 1000 MPa) is the single most common arithmetic error.
>
> 4. **Omitting $\tau_i$ in critical fibre length derivation (–2 marks):** The denominator **must** be $2\tau_i$, not $\tau_i$. The factor of 2 arises from load transfer at both fibre ends.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to mention surface treatment (–1–2 marks):** Any discussion of natural-fibre composites without acknowledging the hydrophilicity–hydrophobicity mismatch and at least one chemical treatment (alkali/silane) is considered incomplete under CO4.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Fully Bio-Based Composites**

- ✅ **Definition:** Composite where BOTH matrix and reinforcement are biomass-derived (fully renewable carbon).
- ✅ **Bio-matrices (5 majors):** PLA, PHAs (PHB/PHBV), Starch (TPS), Cellulose derivatives (CA/CAB), Soy/Lignin resins.
- ✅ **Bio-fibres (by origin):** Bast (flax, jute, hemp, kenaf, ramie), Leaf (sisal, abaca, banana), Seed (cotton, coir), Wood (cellulose).
- ✅ **Critical equations:** $L_c = \sigma_f d / 2\tau_i$; $E_L = E_f V_f + E_m V_m$; $\sigma_L = \sigma_f V_f + \sigma_m V_m$; $\rho_c = \rho_f V_f + \rho_m V_m$.
- ✅ **Rule of Mixtures — Direction matters:** Use direct ROM for longitudinal (Voigt); inverse ROM for transverse (Reuss); Halpin–Tsai for more accuracy with discontinuous fibres.
- ✅ **Interfacial modification methods:** Alkali (NaOH removes hemicellulose), Silane (forms molecular bridge), Maleic anhydride grafting (ester linkage), Acetylation (reduces –OH).
- ✅ **Specific modulus advantage:** Natural fibres are **lighter** than glass — comparable $E/\rho$ despite lower $E$.
- ✅ **Key advantage:** Negative/zero net CO₂ (carbon absorbed during growth ≈ carbon released during composting).
- ✅ **Major limitation:** Hydrophilicity → moisture absorption → dimensional instability → poor fibre–matrix bonding.
- ✅ **Processing:** Compression moulding preserves fibre length; injection moulding causes severe fibre attrition.
- ✅ **Applications:** Automotive interiors, packaging, construction panels, biomedical scaffolds, consumer goods.
- ✅ **Standards to cite:** ASTM D6866 (bio-based carbon content), EN 13432 (industrial compostability), ISO 14040 (LCA methodology).
- ✅ **Syllabus Outcome Link:** CO4 — *Appraise structure–property–processing relationships of advanced bio-composites for sustainable engineering applications.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Current and Future Applications of Bio-Composites
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT415 - ADVANCED BIOPOLYMERS & BIOCOMPOSITES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Current and Future Applications of Bio-Composites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **bio-composite** is a composite material in which at least one of the constituent phases (the matrix and/or the reinforcement/filler) is derived from a **bio-based**, **biodegradable**, or **biologically sourced** origin. As per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, bio-composites are classified into three primary categories:

1. **Fully Bio-based Composites** – Both matrix and reinforcement are from renewable biological sources (e.g., poly(lactic acid) [PLA] reinforced with flax fibers).
2. **Partially Bio-based Composites** – Either the matrix or the reinforcement is bio-based (e.g., polypropylene reinforced with jute fibers).
3. **Biodegradable Composites** – Components are designed to undergo microbial/enzymatic degradation under specific environmental conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "A bio-composite is a multiphase material comprising a bio-polymer matrix reinforced with natural fibers (or bio-derived fillers) engineered to deliver tailored mechanical, thermal, and barrier properties for specific end-use applications."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a bio-composite like a **reinforced mud-brick house** used in traditional Kerala architecture:
- The **mud (matrix)** is the soft, moldable component that holds everything together.
- The **straw (natural fiber reinforcement)** provides tensile strength, preventing the mud from cracking under stress.
- Together, they create a structure that is **stronger, lighter, and more durable** than mud alone — and crucially, when the house is eventually demolished, the entire structure **biodegrades back into the soil**.

Similarly, in modern engineering bio-composites:
- **Natural fibers (jute, flax, hemp, sisal, bamboo)** act as the "straw" — providing high specific strength.
- **Bio-polymers (PLA, PHA, starch blends, chitosan)** act as the "mud" — providing form, moisture resistance, and binding.
- The result is a material that competes with synthetic composites (like glass-fiber/epoxy) but with a **carbon-negative footprint** of approximately **−1.8 kg CO₂e/kg** versus **+2.7 kg CO₂e/kg** for conventional composites.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Metrics for KTU Board Examinations:**
> - **Density of natural fiber composites:** **1.2 – 1.5 g/cm³** (versus 1.8 – 2.0 g/cm³ for glass-fiber composites).
> - **Specific tensile strength:** Up to **300 MPa/(g/cm³)** for flax/PLA composites.
> - **CO₂ sequestration potential:** **1.5 – 2.0 kg CO₂/kg** of natural fiber.
> - **Biodegradation time (compostable):** **60 – 180 days** (industrial composting, 58 °C, > 50% humidity).
> - **Renewable content threshold for "bio-based":** Minimum **25%** bio-based carbon (ASTM D6866 standard).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Current Applications

### 2.1.1 Automotive Industry (Tier-1 Application)
- **Components:** Door panels, dashboard carriers, seat backs, parcel shelves, trunk liners, underbody shields.
- **Key Players:** Toyota (RAV4 with bioplastic parts), BMW (i-series door trim using kenaf fibers), Mercedes-Benz (E-Class with abaca-fiber door panels), Ford (soy-foam seat cushions).
- **Engineering Logic:** Reduces vehicle weight by **20–30%** vs. talc-filled PP, improving fuel economy by **~6–8%**.

### 2.1.2 Construction & Building Materials
- **Components:** Structural panels, ceiling tiles, insulation boards, door cores, window frames, roofing shingles.
- **Engineering Logic:** Natural fiber composites offer **thermal conductivity of 0.045 – 0.065 W/m·K**, comparable to expanded polystyrene, but with **Class A fire ratings** when treated with intumescent bio-coatings.

### 2.1.3 Biomedical Applications
- **Components:** Bone scaffolds, tissue engineering matrices, dental implants, wound dressings, drug delivery systems.
- **Key Materials:** PLA/PCL + hydroxyapatite (HA), collagen-chitosan scaffolds, PLA-pectin composites.
- **Engineering Logic:** Biocompatibility index > **90%** (ISO 10993-5), controllable degradation rate matching tissue regeneration (**3–24 months**).

### 2.1.4 Packaging Industry
- **Components:** Rigid containers, flexible films, foamed trays, mulch films.
- **Engineering Logic:** PLA has a **heat deflection temperature (HDT) of 55–65 °C**, gas permeability (O₂: 38 cm³·mm/m²·day·atm) suitable for fresh produce.

### 2.1.5 Aerospace & Electronics
- **Components:** Interior panels (flax/epoxy), PCB substrates, casings for consumer electronics.
- **Engineering Logic:** Flax/epoxy laminate shows **specific stiffness of 23 GPa/(g/cm³)** — competitive with carbon-fiber/epoxy at **~40% lower cost**.

## 2.2 Future Application Horizon (2025–2035)

> [!NOTE]
> **Emerging Frontiers Identified in KTU Module 4:**
> - **3D-Printed Bio-Composites** — Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) of wood-PLA, mycelium-based printing.
> - **Self-Healing Bio-Composites** — Microencapsulated linseed oil healing agents in epoxy matrix.
> - **Smart/Responsive Bio-Composites** — Piezoelectric bamboo-fiber composites for energy harvesting.
> - **Mycelium-Bound Composites** — Fungal hyphae binding agricultural waste into structural panels (Ecovative design).
> - **Nano-Bio-Composites** — Cellulose nanocrystals (CNC) reinforced PLA for ultra-high barrier films.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Property / Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables & Units** | **Application Context** |
|------------------------|-------------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Rule of Mixtures (Tensile Strength)** | $\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$ | $\sigma_c$: composite strength (MPa), $V_f$: fiber volume fraction, $V_m$: matrix volume fraction | Predicting tensile strength of aligned bio-fiber composites |
| **Inverse Rule of Mixtures (Young's Modulus)** | $\dfrac{1}{E_c} = \dfrac{V_f}{E_f} + \dfrac{V_m}{E_m}$ | $E_c, E_f, E_m$: moduli of composite, fiber, matrix (GPa) | Transverse modulus estimation |
| **Fiber Volume Fraction** | $V_f = \dfrac{W_f / \rho_f}{W_f / \rho_f + W_m / \rho_m}$ | $W$: weight fraction, $\rho$: density (g/cm³) | Composite design calculations |
| **Composite Density** | $\rho_c = \rho_f \cdot V_f + \rho_m \cdot V_m$ | $\rho_c$: composite density (g/cm³) | Lightweighting analysis |
| **Critical Fiber Length** | $l_c = \dfrac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \tau_i}$ | $l_c$: critical length (mm), $d$: fiber diameter, $\tau_i$: interfacial shear strength (MPa) | Determining effective reinforcement |
| **Biodegradation Kinetics (First Order)** | $\ln(M_t) = \ln(M_0) - k \cdot t$ | $M_t$: mass at time $t$, $k$: rate constant (day⁻¹) | Compostability prediction |
| **Halflife of Biodegradation** | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln(2)}{k}$ | $t_{1/2}$: halflife (days) | Service-life estimation |
| **CO₂ Footprint** | $CF = \sum (m_i \cdot EF_i) - S_{bio}$ | $m$: mass, $EF$: emission factor, $S_{bio}$: biogenic sequestration (kg CO₂e) | Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) |
| **Specific Strength** | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\rho_c}$ | $\sigma_{sp}$: specific strength (MPa/(g/cm³)) | Material selection index |
| **Moisture Absorption (Fickian)** | $M_t = M_\infty \left(1 - \exp\left(-7.5 \left(\dfrac{D \cdot t}{h^2}\right)^{0.75}\right)\right)$ | $D$: diffusivity, $h$: thickness, $M_\infty$: equilibrium moisture | Hygrothermal aging |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility Summary

The "Why" behind bio-composites centers on four pillars:
1. **Environmental Compliance** — EU End-of-Life Vehicle (ELV) directive mandates **85% recyclability/recoverability** by mass.
2. **Performance** — Specific stiffness of flax/epoxy approaches aluminum alloys (~25 GPa/(g/cm³)).
3. **Economic** — Natural fibers cost **\$0.5–2.0/kg** vs. glass fibers at **\$2.5–5.0/kg**.
4. **Regulatory** — USDA BioPreferred Program mandates federal procurement of bio-based products.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Deriving the Rule of Mixtures for Composite Tensile Strength

**Problem Context:** Derive the rule of mixtures used to predict the longitudinal tensile strength of a unidirectional bio-fiber reinforced composite under isostrain conditions.

### Step 1: Define the Isostrain Assumption
When the fiber and matrix are perfectly bonded and loaded parallel to the fiber axis, both phases experience the **same strain** ($\varepsilon_c = \varepsilon_f = \varepsilon_m$). This is the **Voigt model** assumption.

### Step 2: Force Equilibrium
The total load carried by the composite is the sum of loads carried by fiber and matrix phases:

$$F_c = F_f + F_m$$

### Step 3: Convert Force to Stress
Using $\sigma = F/A$, and recognizing that areas are additive ($A_c = A_f + A_m$):

$$\sigma_c \cdot A_c = \sigma_f \cdot A_f + \sigma_m \cdot A_m$$

### Step 4: Divide by Total Area
Dividing through by $A_c$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_c} + \sigma_m \cdot \frac{A_m}{A_c}$$

### Step 5: Recognize Volume Fraction Equivalence
For aligned fibers, $\frac{A_f}{A_c} = V_f$ and $\frac{A_m}{A_c} = V_m$:

$$\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot V_m$$

### Step 6: Apply Constraint
Since $V_f + V_m = 1$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_c = \sigma_f \cdot V_f + \sigma_m \cdot (1 - V_f)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** This equation is linear in $V_f$, meaning tensile strength increases proportionally with fiber volume fraction — a direct justification for why KTU Module 4 emphasizes $V_f$ optimization in flax/PLA composite design (typical target: $V_f = 0.3 - 0.4$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Fiber Length Calculation for Natural Fibers

**Problem Context:** A sisal fiber with diameter $d = 200 \,\mu m$ has tensile strength $\sigma_f = 500$ MPa and interfacial shear strength with PLA matrix $\tau_i = 8$ MPa. Derive the critical fiber length $l_c$.

### Step 1: Force Balance on a Single Fiber
At the critical length, the maximum tensile stress the fiber can sustain equals the shear stress transferred through the fiber-matrix interface over the embedded length:

$$\sigma_f \cdot A_f = \tau_i \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

The factor of $1/2$ appears because shear stress transfers from both ends of the embedded fiber.

### Step 2: Substitute Fiber Cross-Sectional Area
For a circular fiber, $A_f = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \pi \cdot d \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

### Step 3: Cancel Common Terms
Cancel $\pi \cdot d$ from both sides:

$$\sigma_f \cdot \frac{d}{4} = \tau_i \cdot \frac{l_c}{2}$$

### Step 4: Solve for $l_c$

$$l_c = \frac{\sigma_f \cdot d}{2 \cdot \tau_i}$$

### Step 5: Plug in Numerical Values
$l_c = \dfrac{(500 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m})}{2 \times (8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa})}$

$$l_c = \frac{100}{16} = 6.25 \text{ mm}$$

### Step 6: Interpret the Result
For sisal/PLA composites, fibers must be **at least 6.25 mm long** to achieve effective load transfer. Standard sisal fiber lengths of **10–25 mm** comfortably exceed this threshold, confirming adequate reinforcement.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Biodegradation Kinetics — Determination of Rate Constant

**Problem Context:** A PLA/starch bio-composite film with initial mass $M_0 = 100$ g is buried in compost. After $t = 90$ days, the remaining mass is $M_t = 25$ g. Determine the first-order rate constant $k$ and the half-life $t_{1/2}$.

### Step 1: Apply First-Order Kinetics

$$\ln(M_t) = \ln(M_0) - k \cdot t$$

### Step 2: Rearrange for $k$

$$k = \frac{\ln(M_0) - \ln(M_t)}{t} = \frac{1}{t} \ln\left(\frac{M_0}{M_t}\right)$$

### Step 3: Substitute Values

$$k = \frac{1}{90} \ln\left(\frac{100}{25}\right) = \frac{1}{90} \ln(4)$$

### Step 4: Evaluate $\ln(4) = 2 \ln(2) = 2 \times 0.6931 = 1.3863$

$$k = \frac{1.3863}{90} = 0.0154 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

### Step 5: Calculate Half-Life

$$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k} = \frac{0.6931}{0.0154} = 45 \text{ days}$$

### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation
The composite loses **50% of its mass every 45 days** in industrial compost — well within the **EN 13432 standard** requirement of **≥ 90% mineralization in 180 days** under controlled composting conditions.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Current vs. Future Applications

| **Application Domain** | **Current State (2024)** | **Future Projection (2030+)** | **Key Enabling Technology** | **Performance Gap to Bridge** |
|------------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Automotive | Door panels, parcel shelves (Flax/PP, Kenaf/PLA) | Structural body-in-white components | 3D-woven natural fiber preforms, bio-epoxy resins | Impact strength, long-term durability |
| Construction | Non-load-bearing panels, insulation | Load-bearing structural beams | Bamboo-laminated veneer lumber (LVL), mycelium boards | Fire resistance, moisture durability |
| Biomedical | Sutures, bone screws, scaffolds | Smart drug-delivery implants, organ-on-chip substrates | 4D-printed shape-memory bio-composites, NFC-tagged scaffolds | Vascularization, controlled degradation |
| Packaging | Trays, films, loose-fill foam | Active/intelligent packaging with sensors | Cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) barrier layers, PLA-PHA blends | Oxygen barrier, heat resistance |
| Aerospace | Interior panels (flax/epoxy) | Semi-structural wing components | Nano-cellulose reinforced bio-epoxy, vacuum-infused prepregs | Fatigue life, certification standards |
| Electronics | Casings, PCB substrates | Flexible displays, biodegradable sensors | Conductive bio-composites (carbonized lignin, MXene-cellulose) | Conductivity, processability |
| Energy | Wind turbine blade cores | Bio-derived carbon fiber for blades | Lignin-based carbon fiber, cellulose aerogel electrodes | Mechanical performance, scale-up |
| Agriculture | Mulch films, pots | Self-degrading seed tapes | Hydrolytically programmed PLA-PHB copolymers | Cost, controlled degradation kinetics |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Define bio-composites. List any four current applications of bio-composites with examples."**

**Model Answer:**
A **bio-composite** is a composite material in which at least one phase (matrix or reinforcement) is derived from biological/renewable sources. Bio-composites are engineered to combine the performance of synthetic composites with environmental benefits like biodegradability and reduced carbon footprint.

**Four current applications:**
1. **Automotive:** Flax-fiber reinforced polypropylene (PP) door panels in BMW 7-Series.
2. **Packaging:** PLA/starch blend trays for fresh produce (compostable within 90 days).
3. **Construction:** Hempcrete blocks for non-load-bearing walls with thermal conductivity 0.07 W/m·K.
4. **Biomedical:** PLA-hydroxyapatite (HA) scaffolds for bone tissue engineering.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between fully bio-based and partially bio-based composites with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Fully Bio-based Composite** | **Partially Bio-based Composite** |
|---------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Both matrix and reinforcement from renewable sources | Either matrix or reinforcement is bio-based |
| **Matrix** | Bio-polymer (PLA, PHA, starch) | Often synthetic (PP, PE, epoxy) |
| **Reinforcement** | Natural fiber (jute, flax, hemp) | Natural fiber (jute, sisal, kenaf) |
| **Example** | Flax/PLA composite | Jute/PP composite |
| **Biodegradability** | Fully biodegradable | Partially biodegradable |
| **CO₂ footprint** | Negative (−1.5 to −2.0 kg CO₂e/kg) | Reduced (+0.3 to +0.8 kg CO₂e/kg) |
